questions.question: What is the administrative distance of OSPF in Cisco IOS?
questions.explanation: OSPF has an AD of 110. Lower AD wins: Connected=0, Static=1, EIGRP internal=90, OSPF=110, RIP=120.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q10; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 16

questions.question: A router has: EIGRP 10.0.0.0/8, OSPF 10.1.0.0/16, Static 10.1.1.0/24 via 192.168.1.1. A packet arrives for 10.1.1.25. Which route is used?
questions.explanation: Longest prefix match wins regardless of AD. /24 is more specific than /16 or /8, so the static route is selected. Longest-match is always evaluated first.
questions.source_ref: Wendell Odom - CCNA 200-301 OCG Vol 1, Chapter 16: IP Routing

questions.question: Which routing protocol characteristic is NOT true of link-state protocols?
options: Sends LSAs to all routers in an area
Each router executes the SPF algorithm
Routers include the originating router's full routing table in updates
Routers maintain a topological database of the network
questions.explanation: Link-state protocols (like OSPF) advertise only their directly connected links, NOT their full routing table. Distance-vector protocols (like RIP) advertise their routing table to neighbors.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q26; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2 Ch 20

questions.question: What is the default route (gateway of last resort) used for?
options: The route chosen first when multiple equal-cost paths exist
A route used to forward packets when no explicit destination is in the routing table
A static route to the nearest ISP
A summary route for a group of networks
questions.explanation: The default route (0.0.0.0/0) is the fallback for packets whose destination has no more-specific match in the routing table. It is the 'catch-all' and often points to the Internet gateway.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q32; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 16

questions.question: What is the maximum hop count for EIGRP?
questions.explanation: EIGRP supports a maximum hop count of 224 (configured with 'metric maximum-hops'). The default is 100. RIP's maximum is 15 hops (16 = unreachable). EIGRP's high hop count makes it suitable for large enterprise networks.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com CCNA 640-407 Practice Q20; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2 Ch 21

questions.question: Which loop-prevention mechanism ensures a routing update is never sent out the same interface on which it was learned?
questions.explanation: Split horizon prevents routing loops by refusing to advertise a route back out the interface on which it was learned. Poison reverse also prevents loops but works by advertising the route as unreachable (metric = infinity) back to the source.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com CCNA 640-407 Practice Q9; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 17

questions.question: What command enables OSPF process 1 and advertises network 10.0.0.0/8 into OSPF area 0?
questions.explanation: OSPF uses wildcard masks (not subnet masks) in the 'network' command. 'router ospf 1' starts OSPF process 1. 'network 10.0.0.0 0.255.255.255 area 0' advertises all 10.x.x.x interfaces into area 0. The process ID is locally significant only.
questions.source_ref: Cisco Lab Guide v200-301 Lab 20 OSPF Configuration; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2 Ch 20

questions.question: What is the OSPF Router ID selection priority on a Cisco router?
options: Lowest IP of any interface, then loopback, then manual
Manual config first, then highest loopback IP, then highest active physical interface IP
Lowest loopback IP first, then manual config, then lowest physical IP
Random selection from active interfaces on startup
questions.explanation: Cisco OSPF Router ID selection order: 1) Manually configured with 'router-id x.x.x.x'; 2) Highest IP of any loopback interface; 3) Highest IP of any active non-loopback interface. The Router ID identifies the router in the OSPF domain and must be unique.
questions.source_ref: Cisco Lab Guide v200-301 Lab 20 OSPF Configuration; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2 Ch 20

questions.question: What command verifies OSPF neighbor adjacencies on a Cisco router?
questions.explanation: 'show ip ospf neighbor' displays neighbor ID, priority, state (2WAY/FULL), dead timer, neighbor IP, and local interface. 'FULL' = fully adjacent (exchanged LSDBs). 'show ip ospf database' shows the LSDB. 'show ip ospf interface' shows OSPF interface settings.
questions.source_ref: Cisco Lab Guide v200-301 Lab 20 OSPF Configuration; Jeremy's IT Lab - CCNA Course, Day 27

questions.question: PPP (Point-to-Point Protocol) consists of three main components. Which of the following correctly identifies them?
options: HDLC-based encapsulation, LCP (Link Control Protocol), and NCPs (Network Control Protocols)
questions.explanation: PPP has three components: (1) HDLC-based method for encapsulating datagrams over serial links, (2) LCP (Link Control Protocol) — establishes, configures, and tests the data-link connection, (3) NCPs (Network Control Protocols) — configure different network-layer protocols (IP, IPX, etc.).

questions.question: Which PPP authentication method sends the username and password in cleartext?
questions.explanation: PAP (Password Authentication Protocol) sends credentials in cleartext — it is insecure. CHAP uses a three-way challenge-response handshake and never sends the password over the link. CHAP is the preferred method for PPP authentication.

questions.question: What is the correct IOS command sequence to enable PPP encapsulation with CHAP authentication on a serial interface?
questions.explanation: Under interface configuration: 'encapsulation ppp' sets PPP encapsulation, then 'ppp authentication chap' (or 'ppp authentication pap') sets the authentication method. Also requires 'username {hostname} password {secret}' in global config matching the remote router's hostname.

questions.question: In a Cisco 'show ip route' output, which letter prefix indicates a route learned via OSPF?
questions.explanation: In 'show ip route': O = OSPF, R = RIP, S = Static, D = EIGRP, C = Connected, L = Local. The letter prefix indicates the routing protocol source for each route entry.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA - IP Routing; Cisco IOS Reference

questions.question: What is Administrative Distance (AD) used for in Cisco routing?
options: To measure the bandwidth of a link
To determine the preference between routes learned from different routing sources
To calculate the cost of an OSPF route
To define the maximum number of hops in RIP
questions.explanation: Administrative Distance (AD) is used to select the preferred route when multiple routing protocols learn routes to the same destination. Lower AD = more trustworthy. Connected=0, Static=1, EIGRP=90, OSPF=110, RIP=120. The router installs the route with the lowest AD.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 11: Administrative Distance; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1

questions.question: What is the default Administrative Distance of OSPF?
questions.explanation: OSPF has a default Administrative Distance of 110. For comparison: Static route=1, EIGRP internal=90, OSPF=110, RIP=120. When both OSPF and RIP learn a route, OSPF (110) wins over RIP (120) because lower AD is preferred.
questions.source_ref: Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1; Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA AD table

questions.question: Split horizon is a loop-prevention mechanism used by distance vector routing protocols. What does it prevent?
options: Routing updates being sent to directly connected hosts
A router from advertising a route back out the same interface it was learned on
questions.explanation: Split horizon prevents a router from advertising a route back out the interface it was learned on. This prevents routing loops in distance vector protocols like RIP and IGRP/EIGRP. If Router A learned about Network X from Router B, it won't advertise Network X back to Router B.

questions.question: Which command displays the current Frame Relay map entries on a Cisco router?
questions.explanation: 'show frame-relay map' displays the current Frame Relay map entries (DLCI-to-IP mappings). 'show frame-relay lmi' shows LMI statistics. 'show frame-relay pvc' shows PVC statistics. 'clear frame-relay-inarp' clears dynamically created maps from Inverse ARP.

questions.question: What does DLCI stand for in Frame Relay networks?
questions.explanation: DLCI stands for Data Link Connection Identifier. It is a locally significant number that identifies a virtual circuit (PVC) in Frame Relay. The same PVC may have different DLCI numbers on each end. DLCIs are used like MAC addresses at Layer 2 in Frame Relay networks.
questions.source_ref: CCNA_CramGuide.htm Frame Relay section; Wendell Odom OCG

questions.question: In OSPF, what is the significance of the Router ID (RID)?
options: It is the IP address used to forward packets
It is a 32-bit number that uniquely identifies each router in the OSPF domain
It determines which OSPF area a router belongs to
It sets the OSPF process priority
questions.explanation: The OSPF Router ID (RID) is a 32-bit number (written like an IP address) that uniquely identifies each router in the OSPF domain. Selection priority: (1) Manually configured with 'router-id', (2) Highest loopback interface IP, (3) Highest IP on any active physical interface. The RID must be unique across all OSPF routers.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 27: OSPF Part 2; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1

questions.question: In a multi-access network (e.g., Ethernet), OSPF elects a Designated Router (DR) and Backup Designated Router (BDR). What determines the DR election?
options: The router with the lowest Router ID becomes DR
The router with the highest OSPF priority; if tied, the highest Router ID wins
The router with the most OSPF neighbors
The router that first sends a Hello packet becomes DR
questions.explanation: DR election: (1) Highest OSPF interface priority wins (default priority = 1; range 0–255; priority 0 = never become DR). (2) If priorities are tied, the router with the highest Router ID becomes DR. The router with the second highest becomes BDR. Set priority with: 'ip ospf priority {value}'.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 26: OSPF Part 1; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1

questions.question: Which of the following are link-state routing protocols? Choose two.
questions.explanation: OSPF and IS-IS are link-state routing protocols. They advertise the state of links to all routers in the area when changes occur, building a topological database and using the SPF (Dijkstra's) algorithm. RIP is distance vector (sends full routing table periodically). EIGRP is advanced distance vector (hybrid).

questions.question: What is a hold-down timer in distance vector routing, and when is it released?
options: A timer that delays route updates; released when the interface comes back up
A timer that prevents accepting worse routes after a route goes down; released only when a better route is received
A timer that controls how often routing updates are sent
A timer that limits the maximum hop count
questions.explanation: A hold-down timer is started when a route is marked as possibly down. During hold-down: the router WILL accept updates with a BETTER (lower metric) route. The router will NOT accept updates with a WORSE route. Administrators can configure the hold-down time. Hold-down timers + triggered updates + split horizon are loop prevention mechanisms in distance vector protocols.

questions.question: In Frame Relay, BECN and FECN bits are used for:
options: Providing keepalives to maintain PVC status
Error checking in the Frame Relay CRC
Notifying DTEs about congestion in the Frame Relay network
questions.explanation: BECN (Backward Explicit Congestion Notification) and FECN (Forward Explicit Congestion Notification) are congestion notification bits in Frame Relay frames. FECN is set in frames traveling toward the destination when congestion is encountered. BECN is set in frames traveling back toward the source to signal it to slow down.

questions.question: Which of the following is a ROUTED protocol (carries user data), NOT a routing protocol?
questions.explanation: ROUTED protocols carry user data through the network (IP, IPX, AppleTalk). ROUTING protocols determine the best path for routed protocols to travel (OSPF, RIP, EIGRP, IGRP, IS-IS, BGP). IP is a routed protocol — it defines packet addressing and routing. OSPF, RIP, and EIGRP are all routing protocols.

questions.question: Which statement is NOT true about link-state routing protocols (like OSPF)?
options: LSAs are flooded to all routers in the OSPF area
Each router runs the SPF (Dijkstra's) algorithm
LSAs include the originating router's full routing table
questions.explanation: Link-state protocols (OSPF, IS-IS) do NOT share routing tables. Instead, they share Link-State Advertisements (LSAs) describing their directly connected links. Each router independently builds a topological map and runs SPF to compute best paths. Distance vector protocols (RIP) share routing tables periodically.

questions.question: Which of the following would NOT appear in a router's routing table?
questions.explanation: Routing tables contain: destination network/mask, next-hop IP address, outgoing interface, administrative distance, and metric. MAC addresses are Layer 2 information found in a switch's MAC address table (CAM table) or ARP cache — NOT in the routing table. Routers operate at Layer 3.

questions.question: What is a triggered update in distance vector routing protocols?
options: An update sent at fixed intervals regardless of changes
An update immediately sent to neighbors when a route change occurs
An update that only occurs when hold-down timers expire
An update that increases the routing metric for slow links
questions.explanation: A triggered update is sent immediately when a router detects a topology change (e.g., an interface goes down), without waiting for the regular update interval. Combined with hold-down timers, triggered updates help prevent routing loops. Regular periodic updates still occur even when there are no changes.

questions.question: What is the maximum hop count (metric) for RIP (Routing Information Protocol)?
questions.explanation: RIP uses hop count as its metric (each router = 1 hop). Maximum hop count is 15 — a hop count of 16 means 'unreachable/infinity.' This limits RIP to networks with diameter ≤ 15 routers. RIP v1 sends classful updates every 30 seconds; RIP v2 adds VLSM support and authentication.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 24: RIP; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1

questions.question: What is the EIGRP metric based on?
options: Bandwidth and delay (primarily); load, reliability, and MTU can also be used
Cost based on 100 Mbps / link bandwidth
questions.explanation: EIGRP uses a composite metric primarily based on bandwidth (of the slowest link) and delay (cumulative delay of all links). By default, load, reliability, and MTU are not included in the metric calculation (K values: K1=1, K2=0, K3=1, K4=0, K5=0). OSPF uses cost = 100Mbps / bandwidth.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 33: EIGRP; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1

questions.question: In a Frame Relay map statement (`frame-relay map ip <address> <dlci>`), what does each field represent?
options: IP = local router IP address; DLCI = remote DLCI number
IP = next-hop (remote) IP address; DLCI = local DLCI number
IP = next-hop IP address; DLCI = remote DLCI number
IP = Frame Relay switch IP; DLCI = PVC circuit ID
questions.explanation: In `frame-relay map ip <next-hop-ip> <local-dlci>`, the IP address is the NEXT HOP address (the far-end router), and the DLCI is the LOCAL DLCI assigned to your interface by the Frame Relay provider. The DLCI has only local significance.

questions.question: Which Frame Relay parameter must match end-to-end between routers, and which only has local significance?
options: Encapsulation must match end-to-end; LMI type has only local significance
LMI type must match end-to-end; encapsulation has only local significance
Both encapsulation and LMI must match end-to-end
Neither needs to match; they are both locally significant
questions.explanation: Frame Relay encapsulation (Cisco or IETF) must match on both ends of the PVC because it determines the frame format. LMI (Local Management Interface) is a keepalive protocol between the local router and the local Frame Relay switch ONLY — it does not need to match the far end.

questions.question: Frame Relay is best described as a variation of which protocol?
questions.explanation: Frame Relay is a packet-switched WAN protocol that is derived from HDLC. It is a streamlined version that removes error correction and flow control (done at higher layers), making it faster than X.25. DLCIs identify virtual circuits in Frame Relay.

questions.question: What is the last piece of ISDN equipment at the customer premises (CPE) before the local loop?
questions.explanation: NT1 (Network Termination 1) is the last customer-side ISDN device at the U interface (where the local loop from the CO ends). In the US, the NT1 is customer-owned. NT2 is optional PBX/router equipment; TE1 is ISDN-capable terminal equipment; TA adapts non-ISDN devices.

questions.question: What is the maximum OSPF link-state metric (cost) value?
questions.explanation: OSPF uses cost as its metric. The maximum OSPF cost is 65,535 (16-bit unsigned integer). Cost is calculated as 100 Mbps / interface bandwidth. A Fast Ethernet interface has a cost of 1 (100/100). Higher bandwidth = lower cost.

questions.question: Which WAN switching type uses a dedicated physical circuit established for each communication session?
questions.explanation: Circuit switching establishes a dedicated physical path for the entire duration of the communication (like a phone call). ISDN/LAPD uses circuit switching. Packet switching (Frame Relay, X.25) shares the network medium dynamically. Cell switching (ATM) uses fixed-length 53-byte cells.

questions.question: What is the Frame Relay DE (Discard Eligibility) bit used for?
options: Indicates the frame should be delivered with high priority
Marks frames that can be dropped first during network congestion (traffic beyond CIR)
Signals the destination that the frame is the last in a sequence
Enables dynamic routing updates to be forwarded
questions.explanation: The DE bit is set on oversubscribed traffic — traffic received after the CIR (Committed Information Rate) has been exceeded. When a Frame Relay switch detects congestion, it drops DE-marked frames first before dropping frames without the DE bit. CIR is the guaranteed average data rate.

questions.question: What are the channel configuration and speeds for ISDN BRI vs. PRI (US)?
questions.explanation: ISDN BRI: 2 Bearer channels (64 Kbps each for data/voice) + 1 Delta channel (16 Kbps for signaling) = 144 Kbps total. ISDN PRI US/Japan: 23B + 1D (all channels = 64 Kbps) = 24 × 64 = 1.536 Mbps + framing = 1.544 Mbps (T1). Europe PRI: 30B + 1D = 2.048 Mbps (E1).

questions.question: In a WAN connection, what is the difference between DCE and DTE?
options: DCE=Data Center Equipment; DTE=Data Terminal Equipment
DCE=Data Communications Equipment (provides clocking, at carrier side); DTE=Data Terminal Equipment (customer router, receives clock)
DCE and DTE are identical; they are interchangeable
DCE=at the customer; DTE=at the carrier
questions.explanation: DCE (Data Communications Equipment): provides the clocking signal for WAN links (typically the CSU/DSU or Frame Relay switch). DTE (Data Terminal Equipment): the customer router that accepts the clock from the DCE. In lab simulations, the DCE side must be configured with `clock rate <speed>` (e.g., `clock rate 56000`). `show controllers serial0` identifies DCE vs DTE.

questions.question: What are the WAN terms CO, demarcation, and CPE?
options: CO=Central Office (carrier facility); Demarc=boundary between carrier and customer; CPE=Customer Premises Equipment
CO=Customer Office; Demarc=the firewall; CPE=Central Physical Equipment
CO=Circuit Opening; Demarc=the ISP router; CPE=Cloud Provider Edge
All three terms refer to the same equipment at the carrier facility
questions.explanation: WAN terms: CO (Central Office) — the telephone company/carrier's local switching facility. Demarcation (Demarc) — the physical boundary point between carrier and customer responsibility (usually a NID/CSU). CPE (Customer Premises Equipment) — all equipment on the customer side of the demarc (routers, switches, PBX). The local loop connects the demarc to the CO.

questions.question: What are the OSPF router states in the neighbor establishment process?
options: Down → Init → 2-Way → ExStart → Exchange → Loading → Full
questions.explanation: OSPF neighbor states: Down → Init (hello received but not bidirectional) → 2-Way (bidirectional communication, DR/BDR election on multi-access) → ExStart (master/slave negotiation) → Exchange (DBD exchange) → Loading (LSR/LSU exchange) → Full (databases synchronized). Non-DR/BDR routers on broadcast networks stay in 2-Way with each other.

questions.question: How is OSPF cost calculated for an interface?
options: Cost = 100 Mbps / interface bandwidth (reference bandwidth / interface bandwidth)
Cost = hop count to the destination
questions.explanation: OSPF cost = reference bandwidth / interface bandwidth. Default reference = 100 Mbps. FastEthernet (100M) cost = 100/100 = 1. Gigabit Ethernet (1000M) also = 1 with default reference (problem!). Serial (1.544M T1) cost = 100/1.544 ≈ 64. To fix GigE: `ospf auto-cost reference-bandwidth 1000` makes GigE cost = 1, FastEthernet = 10. Lower cost = preferred path.

questions.question: What are the IPv6 address types and their prefixes?
questions.explanation: IPv6 address types: Global Unicast (2000::/3) — routable on internet. Link-local (FE80::/10) — automatically configured, used for routing protocol neighbor discovery, not routable. Multicast (FF00::/8) — one-to-many. Anycast — same address on multiple devices, packet goes to nearest. Loopback = ::1/128. No broadcast in IPv6 (replaced by multicast). Unique local (FC00::/7) = IPv6 private addresses.

questions.question: What replaces ARP in IPv6 for address resolution?
options: NDP (Neighbor Discovery Protocol) using ICMPv6 messages
questions.explanation: IPv6 uses NDP (Neighbor Discovery Protocol, RFC 4861) instead of ARP. NDP uses ICMPv6 messages: Neighbor Solicitation (NS, like ARP request) — sent to solicited-node multicast to find MAC for known IPv6 address. Neighbor Advertisement (NA, like ARP reply). Router Solicitation/Advertisement (RS/RA) — hosts find default gateways. NDP also handles SLAAC (Stateless Address Autoconfiguration).

questions.question: What is a floating static route and when would you use it?
options: A static route that automatically updates when the network changes
A static route with a higher AD than the dynamic routing protocol, used as a backup route that only activates when the primary dynamic route fails
A static route configured with a specific metric to control traffic flow
A default route (0.0.0.0/0) used as a gateway of last resort
questions.explanation: A floating static route has a manually configured AD higher than the dynamic routing protocol (e.g., AD=200 vs OSPF AD=110). When the dynamic route exists, it's preferred and the static route is invisible in the routing table (floats above). If the dynamic route fails, the static route activates as backup. Example: `ip route 10.0.0.0 255.0.0.0 192.168.1.1 200` — AD of 200 makes this a backup to any dynamic route.

questions.question: What is a default route and how is it configured on a Cisco router?
options: A route to the loopback interface; configured with `ip route loopback 0`
A catch-all route (0.0.0.0/0) used when no specific route matches; configured with `ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 <next-hop>`
The first route loaded into the routing table during boot
A route that is automatically learned from DHCP
questions.explanation: A default route (0.0.0.0/0, also called the 'gateway of last resort') matches ANY destination when no more-specific route exists. It's the last resort before a packet is dropped. Configured with: `ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 <next-hop-ip or exit-interface>`. Often distributed to other routers via `default-information originate` (OSPF) or `redistribute static` (EIGRP). Can also be learned from OSPF as an O*E2 route.

questions.question: In ISDN, what are the four reference points and what do they describe?
options: R=non-ISDN to TA; S=user terminals to NT2; T=NT1 to NT2; U=NT1 to local loop
R=NT1 to NT2; S=local loop; T=TA to TE2; U=NT2 to switch
questions.explanation: ISDN reference points define the interface type between components: R = between non-ISDN equipment (TE2) and Terminal Adapter (TA). S = between user terminals (TE1 or TA) and NT2 (PBX). T = between NT2 and NT1. U = between NT1 and the carrier's local loop (2-wire). The S/T reference points are often combined as 'S/T interface' on Cisco BRI interfaces.

questions.question: What is the Cisco troubleshooting methodology — what are the seven steps?
options: Ping → Trace → Debug → Reload → Call TAC → Replace hardware → Give up
Define problem → Gather info → Analyze info → Eliminate possible causes → Propose hypothesis → Test hypothesis → Solve/Document
Check Layer 1 → Check Layer 2 → Check Layer 3 → Check Routing → Check Application
Call TAC → Open ticket → Wait → Escalate → Resolve
questions.explanation: Cisco's structured troubleshooting: (1) Define the problem. (2) Gather information (show commands, logs). (3) Analyze the info. (4) Eliminate possible causes. (5) Propose a hypothesis (most likely cause). (6) Test the hypothesis (resolve or adjust). (7) Solve the problem and document. Approaches: bottom-up (OSI 1→7), top-down (7→1), divide-and-conquer (start at Layer 3). Always document changes.

questions.question: What Cisco IOS commands are used to test Layer 3 connectivity?
options: `ping` and `traceroute` — ping tests reachability, traceroute shows the path hop-by-hop
questions.explanation: `ping <ip>` sends ICMP Echo Requests and tests end-to-end reachability. A successful ping shows '!!!!!' (5 exclamation points = 5/5 success). `traceroute <ip>` shows each hop and its RTT. On Cisco: `traceroute` uses UDP probes (not ICMP as in Windows `tracert`). Extended ping: `ping` with no arguments enters interactive mode for more control (source IP, count, timeout).

questions.question: What is SLAAC (Stateless Address Autoconfiguration) in IPv6?
options: A DHCPv6 server that assigns addresses to clients
A method where IPv6 hosts automatically generate their own global unicast address using Router Advertisements (prefix) + EUI-64 or random interface ID
A protocol that provides DNS server addresses to IPv6 hosts
A security mechanism that prevents duplicate IPv6 addresses
questions.explanation: SLAAC allows IPv6 hosts to self-configure without a DHCPv6 server. Process: (1) Host sends Router Solicitation (RS) to FF02::2 (all-routers). (2) Router replies with Router Advertisement (RA) containing the network prefix (e.g., 2001:db8::/64). (3) Host combines prefix + EUI-64 (from MAC) or random interface ID. (4) Duplicate Address Detection (DAD) verifies uniqueness. No server needed — hence 'stateless'.

questions.question: Compare RIPv2, OSPF, and EIGRP on these dimensions: metric, max hops, and classification.
options: All three use hop count as metric; only max hops differ
questions.explanation: Comparison: RIPv2 — metric=hop count, max=15 (16=unreachable), distance-vector, updates every 30s, AD=120. OSPF — metric=cost (100M/bandwidth), no hop limit, link-state (builds full topology map), SPF algorithm, AD=110. EIGRP — metric=composite (bandwidth+delay primarily), max hops=224, advanced distance-vector (DUAL algorithm), fast convergence, Cisco proprietary, AD=90 (internal) /170 (external).

questions.question: Which switching mode forwards the frame as soon as the destination MAC address is read, without waiting for the full frame?
questions.explanation: Cut-through switching begins forwarding the frame immediately after reading the destination MAC address (first 6 bytes). This minimizes latency but passes corrupt frames since no CRC check is performed. Store-and-forward waits for the entire frame and checks the CRC.

questions.question: Which switching mode receives the complete frame, checks the CRC, and only then forwards it?
questions.explanation: Store-and-forward switching receives the entire frame before forwarding. It checks the CRC (cyclic redundancy check) and discards corrupt frames. This mode has higher latency than cut-through but guarantees error-free forwarding.

questions.question: Which switching mode waits until the first 64 bytes of a frame are received before forwarding?
questions.explanation: Fragment-free (also called modified cut-through) waits for the first 64 bytes — the collision window — before forwarding. Most frame errors and collisions occur within the first 64 bytes. This provides better error detection than cut-through with only slightly more latency.

questions.question: What is a key characteristic of full-duplex Ethernet?
options: Both stations share a single collision domain
CSMA/CD is still required for collision detection
Frames can be transmitted and received simultaneously, creating a collision-free environment
Maximum bandwidth is half of the rated link speed
questions.explanation: Full-duplex Ethernet uses separate transmit and receive circuits, allowing simultaneous transmission in both directions with no collisions. CSMA/CD is disabled in full-duplex mode. Each full-duplex connection offers 100% efficiency in both directions.

questions.question: At which OSI layer does a bridge operate?
questions.explanation: Bridges operate at Layer 2 (Data Link). They learn MAC addresses by examining source addresses of incoming frames and build a MAC address table. Bridges are protocol-independent and transparent to end users. Routers operate at Layer 3.
questions.source_ref: CCNA CramGuide - LAN Segmentation with Bridges

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the Spanning Tree Protocol (STP)?
options: To provide load balancing across redundant links
To provide path redundancy while preventing layer 2 loops
To elect a root bridge for each VLAN
To assign VLANs to switch ports
questions.explanation: STP (IEEE 802.1D) prevents Layer 2 loops while maintaining path redundancy. It calculates a loop-free tree topology by blocking redundant paths. If the active path fails, STP activates the standby path. Without STP, broadcast storms and MAC table instability occur in redundant switched networks.

questions.question: What IEEE standard defines Fast Ethernet?
questions.explanation: IEEE 802.3u defines Fast Ethernet (100 Mbps). It uses CSMA/CD like standard 10 Mbps Ethernet (802.3) but operates 10 times faster. 802.3ab is Gigabit Ethernet over copper (1000BASE-T). 802.3z is Gigabit Ethernet over fiber.

questions.question: What is the number range for standard IP access lists?
questions.explanation: Standard IP access lists use numbers 1–99 (and 1300–1999 for extended range). Extended IP ACLs use 100–199 (and 2000–2699). Standard ACLs filter based on source IP address only. Extended ACLs can filter on source/destination IP, protocol, and port numbers.

questions.question: What does a standard IP access list filter on?
options: Source IP, destination IP, protocol, and port
questions.explanation: Standard IP access lists (1–99) filter only on the source IP address. Extended ACLs (100–199) can match on source IP, destination IP, protocol type, and port numbers, giving administrators much finer control.
questions.source_ref: CCNA CramGuide - Standard vs Extended ACLs

questions.question: In an ACL wildcard mask, what does a binary 0 bit mean?
options: Ignore the corresponding bit in the address
Check (match) the corresponding bit in the address
The bit must be set to 1
The bit can be any value
questions.explanation: In wildcard masks: bit 0 means 'check/match this bit' and bit 1 means 'ignore this bit.' This is the opposite of subnet masks. For example, wildcard 0.0.0.255 means check the first 3 octets exactly and ignore the last octet (matches any host in that /24 network).

questions.question: Where should standard IP ACLs be placed relative to the traffic source?
options: As close to the destination as possible
As close to the source as possible
On every interface in the network
questions.explanation: Standard ACLs filter only on source IP, so placing them close to the destination prevents them from blocking too much traffic (they can't be selective about destination). Extended ACLs, which can match source AND destination, should be placed close to the source to block traffic early and save bandwidth.

questions.question: Which command assigns a switchport to VLAN 10 as an access port?
options:
switchport mode trunk
switchport trunk allowed vlan 10

switchport mode access
switchport access vlan 10
questions.explanation: To configure an access port: enter interface config mode, use `switchport mode access` to set it as access, then `switchport access vlan 10` to assign it to VLAN 10. The VLAN must also exist in the VLAN database (`vlan 10` in global config). Trunk ports use `switchport mode trunk` and can carry multiple VLANs.

questions.question: What encapsulation protocol is used for VLAN tagging on modern Cisco switches?
questions.explanation: IEEE 802.1Q is the industry-standard VLAN tagging protocol. It inserts a 4-byte tag into the Ethernet frame header, including a 12-bit VLAN ID field (supporting 4094 VLANs). ISL was Cisco's proprietary predecessor but is now deprecated. VTP manages VLAN databases. DTP negotiates trunk links.

questions.question: What is 'Router-on-a-Stick' inter-VLAN routing?
options: Using multiple physical router interfaces, one per VLAN
Using a single router interface with subinterfaces, each with 802.1Q encapsulation
Using a Layer 3 switch with SVIs for each VLAN
Connecting VLANs through a dedicated WAN link
questions.explanation: Router-on-a-Stick uses a single physical router interface connected to a trunk port on a switch. The router has logical subinterfaces (e.g., Gi0/0.10, Gi0/0.20), each with `encapsulation dot1q <vlan-id>`. Inter-VLAN traffic goes through the switch trunk to the router and back. Alternatively, a Layer 3 switch with SVIs is faster and more scalable.

questions.question: What is the function of VTP (VLAN Trunking Protocol)?
options: It negotiates trunk ports between switches
It synchronizes the VLAN database across switches in a VTP domain
It tags Ethernet frames with VLAN IDs
It provides redundancy for trunk links
questions.explanation: VTP (Cisco proprietary) allows VLAN configuration to be synchronized across switches in a VTP domain. A VTP Server manages the VLAN database; VTP Clients receive and sync the database. VTP Transparent mode forwards VTP messages but doesn't sync. VTP is risky — a misconfigured switch can delete all VLANs network-wide. Many engineers disable VTP.

questions.question: What is EtherChannel?
options: A protocol for running Ethernet over fiber
Bundling multiple physical links into one logical link for increased bandwidth and redundancy
A method for segmenting Layer 2 domains
questions.explanation: EtherChannel (also called LAG - Link Aggregation Group) bundles 2-8 physical links into one logical link. Benefits: increased bandwidth (up to 8x), redundancy (traffic redistributes if a member fails), and STP sees it as one link (no blocking). Uses LACP (IEEE 802.3ad) or PAgP (Cisco proprietary) for negotiation.

questions.question: How is the STP Root Bridge elected?
options: The switch with the highest MAC address becomes Root Bridge
The switch with the lowest Bridge ID (priority + MAC) becomes Root Bridge
The switch with the most ports becomes Root Bridge
The Root Bridge is manually assigned only
questions.explanation: STP Root Bridge election: the switch with the lowest Bridge ID wins. Bridge ID = 2-byte Priority + 6-byte MAC address. Default priority = 32768. Ties broken by lowest MAC. To influence election, lower the priority (`spanning-tree vlan <x> priority 4096`). The Root Bridge sends BPDUs; all other switches calculate paths to root.

questions.question: What is the difference between an autonomous AP and a lightweight AP?
options: Autonomous APs are newer; lightweight APs are legacy devices
Autonomous APs are self-contained and configured independently; lightweight APs are managed by a WLC
Lightweight APs have more features; autonomous APs are stripped-down devices
questions.explanation: Autonomous APs are standalone, self-contained units — each requires individual configuration. Lightweight APs (LWAP) offload management to a Wireless LAN Controller (WLC) using CAPWAP tunnels. Lightweight APs have minimal intelligence; the WLC handles all configuration, roaming, RF management, and security policies. CAPWAP uses UDP 5246 (control) and UDP 5247 (data).

questions.question: Which 802.11 amendment operates ONLY in the 5 GHz band?
questions.explanation: 802.11a operates only in 5 GHz (up to 54 Mbps). 802.11b/g operate in 2.4 GHz. 802.11n (Wi-Fi 4) operates in both 2.4 and 5 GHz. 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5) operates only in 5 GHz. 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6) operates in both 2.4, 5, and 6 GHz. 5 GHz has more non-overlapping channels but shorter range.

questions.question: In traditional STP (802.1D), a port moves through which sequence of states?
questions.explanation: 802.1D STP port states in order: Disabled (admin down) → Blocking (receiving BPDUs, not forwarding) → Listening (participating in election, 15 sec) → Learning (building MAC table, 15 sec) → Forwarding (normal operation). Convergence takes ~50 seconds. Rapid PVST+ (802.1w) reduces this to under 1 second.

questions.question: How does a switch build its MAC address table?
options: It uses ARP to discover all devices on each port
It learns source MAC addresses from incoming frames on each port
It receives MAC address tables from neighboring switches via CDP
MAC addresses are statically configured by administrators
questions.explanation: Switches learn MAC addresses dynamically: when a frame arrives, the switch reads the SOURCE MAC address and records it in the CAM (Content Addressable Memory) table along with the incoming port number and a timer. Forwarding decisions are made using the DESTINATION MAC. If the destination is unknown, the frame is flooded to all ports except the source.

questions.question: What does a switch do when it receives a frame with an unknown destination MAC address?
options: Sends it back to the source port
Floods the frame out all ports except the incoming port
Queries neighboring switches for the destination
questions.explanation: Unknown unicast flooding: if the destination MAC is not in the CAM table, the switch floods the frame out all ports (in the same VLAN) except the port it arrived on. This is the same behavior as a hub for unknown destinations. Known unicast frames are forwarded only to the correct port. Broadcast/multicast frames are also flooded.

questions.question: In LACP, which port mode combination successfully forms an EtherChannel?
questions.explanation: LACP modes: Active (actively negotiates EtherChannel), Passive (waits for peer to initiate). Combinations: Active+Active = forms EtherChannel. Active+Passive = forms EtherChannel. Passive+Passive = NO EtherChannel (neither side initiates). 'On' mode bypasses negotiation and forces EtherChannel but doesn't use LACP/PAgP.

questions.question: How should you mitigate VLAN hopping via switch spoofing?
options: Enable DTP on all access ports
Disable DTP and manually configure access ports with `switchport mode access`
Enable BPDU Guard on trunk ports
questions.explanation: Switch spoofing attack: attacker sends DTP frames to negotiate a trunk link with the switch, gaining access to all VLANs. Mitigation: explicitly configure access ports with `switchport mode access` (disables DTP negotiation) and `switchport nonegotiate` to prevent DTP frames. Trunk ports should also use `switchport nonegotiate` and have their native VLAN changed from VLAN 1.

questions.question: What are the port states in Rapid PVST+ (802.1w)?
questions.explanation: Rapid PVST+ (802.1w) reduces port states to 3: Discarding (combines 802.1D Disabled/Blocking/Listening), Learning (builds MAC table), Forwarding (normal operation). Rapid STP achieves sub-second convergence through Proposal/Agreement handshakes instead of waiting for timers. PortFast ports go directly from Discarding to Forwarding.

questions.question: In a wireless deployment, what is BSS vs ESS?
options: BSS=single AP and its associated clients; ESS=multiple BSSs sharing the same SSID for roaming
questions.explanation: BSS (Basic Service Set) = one AP and all its associated wireless clients. Identified by BSSID (= AP's MAC). ESS (Extended Service Set) = multiple BSSs using the same SSID, allowing clients to roam between APs while maintaining connectivity. IBSS = ad-hoc network (no AP). SSID = Service Set ID (network name). DS (Distribution System) connects APs in an ESS.

questions.question: How is an access list applied to a router interface?
options: ip access-group <number> in|out (in interface config mode)
questions.explanation: After creating an access list, apply it to an interface with: (config-if)# `ip access-group <number> in|out`. `in` = filter packets entering the router from this interface. `out` = filter packets leaving the router through this interface. One ACL per protocol per interface per direction. Each interface can have both an inbound and outbound ACL.

questions.question: In an IP access list, what does the keyword `any` represent?
options: Any IP protocol (TCP, UDP, ICMP, etc.)
questions.explanation: The `any` keyword is shorthand for `0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255` — it matches all IP addresses. `host <ip>` is shorthand for `<ip> 0.0.0.0` — matches exactly that one host. Example: `access-list 1 permit any` = permit all sources. `access-list 101 deny tcp any any eq 23` = deny Telnet from any source to any destination.

questions.question: For an ACL applied `in` on interface Ethernet0, what traffic does it inspect?
options: Traffic leaving the router through Ethernet0 toward the connected network
Traffic entering the router from the Ethernet0 network
All traffic passing through the router regardless of interface
Traffic between Ethernet0 and the WAN interface only
questions.explanation: ACL direction is from the router's perspective, not the network's. `in` = traffic arriving at the router from the network on that interface (ingress). `out` = traffic the router is sending out to the network on that interface (egress). Standard ACLs (source-only) should be placed close to the destination using `out` to minimize the area of traffic they affect.

questions.question: What is an SVI (Switched Virtual Interface) on a multilayer switch?
options: A physical switch port configured for Layer 3 routing
A virtual interface representing a VLAN, used for inter-VLAN routing and management
A subinterface on a router-on-a-stick configuration
A tunnel interface for VxLAN encapsulation
questions.explanation: SVIs (Switch Virtual Interfaces) are virtual Layer 3 interfaces on a multilayer switch, one per VLAN. Configured with `interface vlan <id>` and assigned an IP address, SVIs perform inter-VLAN routing in hardware (much faster than router-on-a-stick). Also used for management access to the switch. Require `ip routing` to be enabled on the switch.

questions.question: What is co-channel interference in wireless networks?
options: Interference from devices using adjacent (overlapping) channels
Interference when multiple APs use the same channel, causing contention
Interference from microwave ovens on the 2.4 GHz band
Interference from 5 GHz APs affecting 2.4 GHz clients
questions.explanation: Co-channel interference occurs when multiple APs use the same channel — their coverage areas overlap and clients must share bandwidth/contend for the medium. Different from adjacent-channel interference (overlapping channels like 1 and 2). Solution: use non-overlapping channels (1, 6, 11 for 2.4 GHz) and configure proper AP cell sizes. Excessive transmit power can worsen co-channel interference.

questions.question: What are the default STP (802.1D) timer values?
questions.explanation: STP 802.1D default timers: Hello = 2 seconds (how often the Root Bridge sends BPDUs). Forward Delay = 15 seconds (time spent in Listening and Learning states, each). Max Age = 20 seconds (how long a switch holds a BPDU before discarding it). Total convergence = 50 seconds (max age + 2 × forward delay). Rapid PVST+ eliminates these delays.

questions.question: What is DTP (Dynamic Trunking Protocol) and when should it be disabled?
options: DTP is an IETF standard for VLAN tagging; disable it on edge ports
DTP is Cisco proprietary for trunk auto-negotiation; disable on user-facing access ports to prevent switch spoofing
DTP manages VLAN databases across switches; disable when VTP is not used
DTP is for link aggregation negotiation; disable on non-EtherChannel ports
questions.explanation: DTP (Dynamic Trunking Protocol) is Cisco proprietary, used to auto-negotiate trunk links. Modes: Dynamic Auto (passive), Dynamic Desirable (actively tries to trunk), Trunk (forced), Access (forced). Disable DTP on end-user ports with `switchport mode access` + `switchport nonegotiate` to prevent VLAN hopping via switch spoofing. Trunk ports should also use `nonegotiate` after setting trunk mode.

questions.question: What is an evil twin attack in wireless networks?
options: Configuring a legitimate AP with duplicate SSID on the same channel
Setting up a rogue AP mimicking a legitimate SSID to intercept client connections
Using two APs with the same BSSID to confuse clients
Flooding the wireless medium with deauthentication frames
questions.explanation: An evil twin (honeypot AP) sets up a rogue AP with the same SSID as a legitimate network. Clients may connect to it (especially if it has a stronger signal). The attacker can then conduct MITM attacks, credential harvesting, or inject malicious content. Mitigation: WPA2/WPA3 enterprise (mutual authentication), WIDS/WIPS detection, certificate-based authentication.

questions.question: Which command shows the current VLAN assignment of all switch ports?
questions.explanation: `show vlan brief` displays all VLANs, their names, status (active/inactive), and which ports are assigned to each VLAN. `show interfaces trunk` shows trunk ports, their allowed VLANs, and native VLAN. `show spanning-tree` shows STP topology. `show vlan id <number>` shows details of a specific VLAN.

questions.question: Which command verifies the current STP root bridge and port roles/states?
questions.explanation: `show spanning-tree` (or `show spanning-tree vlan <id>`) shows: Root Bridge ID and cost to reach it, local Bridge ID, Hello/Forward/Max timers, and each port's Role (Root/Designated/Alternate/Backup) and State (Forwarding/Blocking/Discarding). Verify with `show spanning-tree detail` for more information.

questions.question: In a split-MAC architecture with a WLC, which functions are handled by the WLC vs. the AP?
options: WLC handles RF transmission; AP handles authentication
WLC handles management/control (authentication, roaming, policies); AP handles RF transmission and frame forwarding
Both WLC and AP handle all functions equally
AP handles encryption; WLC handles decryption
questions.explanation: In CAPWAP split-MAC: WLC handles = SSID/BSS management, authentication (802.1X/PSK), roaming (seamless), QoS policies, RF management (channel/power), client association. AP handles = RF transmission/reception, frame transmission (beacons), time-sensitive 802.11 tasks. Control traffic tunneled via CAPWAP UDP 5246; data can be centralized (through WLC) or local switching.

questions.question: What command shows whether a switchport is currently an access port or trunk port?
questions.explanation: `show interfaces <int> switchport` displays: Administrative Mode (what's configured), Operational Mode (what's actually running), Trunking encapsulation, VLAN assignment (for access ports), allowed VLANs (for trunks), and native VLAN. Useful to troubleshoot why a port isn't trunking or why it's in the wrong VLAN.

questions.question: Which command shows the MAC address table on a Cisco switch?
questions.explanation: `show mac address-table` displays the Layer 2 forwarding table: VLAN, MAC address, type (dynamic/static), and port. Dynamic entries are learned from traffic; static entries are manually configured. Entries age out after 300 seconds (default) without traffic. `show mac address-table dynamic` shows only learned entries. `show arp` shows Layer 3 IP-to-MAC mappings.

questions.question: What is the default VLAN on a Cisco switch port?
questions.explanation: VLAN 1 is the default VLAN on all Cisco switch ports. All ports belong to VLAN 1 unless reassigned. VLAN 1 is also the default native VLAN for trunk ports and is used for management traffic (CDP, STP, VTP). Best practice: create a dedicated management VLAN (e.g., VLAN 10) and change the native VLAN from VLAN 1 for security.

questions.question: Which command verifies EtherChannel status on a Cisco switch?
questions.explanation: `show etherchannel summary` provides a compact overview: Port-channel number, protocol (LACP/PAgP/None), member interfaces, and flags (P=bundled, D=down, I=standalone, etc.). `show etherchannel detail` shows more detail. `show interfaces port-channel <num>` shows the logical interface stats. The (SU) in output means Standalone + in Use.

questions.question: In Rapid PVST+, what is the Alternate port role?
options: A port forwarding traffic as a backup to the Root port
A port that provides an alternate path to the Root Bridge; it is in discarding state, ready for rapid transition
A port that transmits BPDUs but doesn't forward user traffic
A port disabled by the administrator
questions.explanation: Rapid PVST+ port roles: Root port = best path toward Root Bridge (forwarding). Designated port = best path away from Root Bridge on each segment (forwarding). Alternate port = alternate path to Root Bridge (discarding, replaces blocking role) — can rapidly transition to Root if current Root port fails. Backup port = alternate to Designated port on same segment (discarding). Disabled = admin down.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Root Guard feature on a switch?
options: Prevents end devices from connecting to trunk ports
Prevents a port from becoming a Root port — if superior BPDUs are received, the port is placed in root-inconsistent state
Guards against MAC flooding attacks on root-facing ports
Prevents root bridge election from occurring on access VLANs
questions.explanation: Root Guard prevents unauthorized switches from becoming the Root Bridge. Configured on ports where the Root Bridge should never appear. If a superior BPDU (with a lower Bridge ID) is received on a Root Guard port, the port is placed in root-inconsistent (broken) state and stops forwarding traffic. Recovers automatically when superior BPDUs stop. Typically applied on access and edge ports.

questions.question: Which interface on a Cisco WLC is used for management traffic from the administrator?
questions.explanation: Cisco WLC interfaces: Management interface = used for all management traffic (SSH, HTTPS, SNMP), in-band management. AP Manager interface = used for CAPWAP control traffic to APs (in older WLC). Virtual interface = provides gateway IP for DHCP relay and web authentication portal (not routable, typically 1.1.1.1 or similar). Service port = out-of-band management port (direct Ethernet connection).

questions.question: What is the IPv6 equivalent of the IPv4 broadcast address?
questions.explanation: IPv6 has no broadcast. FF02::1 is the all-nodes multicast address (like IPv4 broadcast for local link). FF02::2 targets all routers.

questions.question: Which ICMPv6 message type does an IPv6 host use to discover its default gateway?
questions.explanation: A host sends a Router Solicitation (RS, ICMPv6 type 133) to the all-routers multicast address FF02::2 to request immediate Router Advertisements.

questions.question: What IOS command enables IPv6 routing on a Cisco router?
questions.explanation: `ipv6 unicast-routing` enables the router to forward IPv6 packets between interfaces. Without this command, the router acts as an IPv6 host only.

questions.question: What is the EUI-64 method used for in IPv6 SLAAC?
options: Generates a 64-bit interface ID from the 48-bit MAC address
Assigns a /64 prefix to the interface
questions.explanation: EUI-64 creates a 64-bit interface ID by inserting FF:FE in the middle of the 48-bit MAC and flipping the Universal/Local bit. Used with SLAAC to create a full /128 IPv6 address.

questions.question: Which address type does IPv6 use to identify a group of interfaces where packets are delivered to the nearest one?
questions.explanation: Anycast addresses are assigned to multiple interfaces; packets are delivered to the topologically nearest interface. Used for load balancing services like DNS.

questions.question: What is the OSPF cost formula used by default on Cisco routers?
questions.explanation: Default OSPF cost = 10^8 / interface bandwidth in bps. FastEthernet (100 Mbps) = cost 1. Serial T1 (1.544 Mbps) ≈ cost 64. Use `auto-cost reference-bandwidth` to adjust for Gigabit.

questions.question: What is required for two OSPF routers to become fully adjacent (beyond being neighbors)?
options: Matching hello and dead timers, same area, same stub flags, same subnet, authentication match
questions.explanation: OSPF adjacency requirements: matching area ID, hello/dead intervals, subnet mask, stub area flag, and authentication. Process IDs do NOT need to match.

questions.question: What is the OSPF hello interval on a LAN (broadcast multi-access) interface by default?
questions.explanation: Default OSPF hello interval on broadcast/NBMA = 10 seconds; dead interval = 40 seconds. On point-to-point links: hello=10s, dead=40s (same). On NBMA hub: hello=30s, dead=120s.

questions.question: Which OSPF LSA type is generated by an ABR to describe routes in other areas?
questions.explanation: Type 3 Summary LSAs are generated by ABRs (Area Border Routers) to advertise networks from one area into another. They summarize inter-area routing information.

questions.question: On which network type does OSPF elect a DR and BDR?
questions.explanation: DR/BDR are elected only on broadcast multi-access (Ethernet) and NBMA networks. Point-to-point links do not elect DR/BDR — routers form a direct adjacency.

questions.question: What is the OSPF router ID selection order on a Cisco router?
options: Highest active interface IP → loopback → manual
Manual router-id → highest loopback IP → highest active interface IP
Manual router-id → highest active interface IP → loopback
questions.explanation: OSPF Router ID: (1) `router-id` command (manual), (2) highest loopback IP, (3) highest active physical interface IP. Loopbacks are preferred because they never go down.

questions.question: What algorithm does EIGRP use to calculate loop-free paths?
questions.explanation: EIGRP uses DUAL (Diffusing Update Algorithm) which guarantees loop-free paths and provides instant failover to a feasible successor without recomputation.

questions.question: What is a Feasible Successor in EIGRP?
options: A backup route that meets the feasibility condition and is kept in the topology table
A route with the highest metric
questions.explanation: A Feasible Successor is a backup route whose reported distance (neighbor's metric) is less than the current successor's feasible distance. It enables sub-second failover.

questions.question: Which two metrics does EIGRP use by default in its composite metric calculation?
questions.explanation: By default, EIGRP uses bandwidth (of the slowest link in the path) and cumulative delay. Load, reliability, and MTU can be enabled but are not used by default.

questions.question: What is the administrative distance of EIGRP for internal routes?
questions.explanation: EIGRP internal AD = 90. EIGRP external (redistributed) routes = 170. OSPF = 110. RIP = 120.

questions.question: What is the effect of the wildcard mask 0.0.0.255 in an ACL?
options: Match only the exact host address
Match all hosts in a /24 network
questions.explanation: Wildcard 0.0.0.255 means the first 3 octets must match exactly, last octet is ignored — matching all 256 addresses in a /24 (Class C) network.

questions.question: Which wildcard mask is the shorthand keyword 'host' equivalent to?
questions.explanation: The `host` keyword is shorthand for wildcard mask 0.0.0.0, meaning all bits must match exactly. It matches only the single specified IP address.

questions.question: Where should an extended ACL be placed for maximum efficiency?
options: As close to the destination as possible
As close to the source as possible
On every interface in both directions
questions.explanation: Extended ACLs should be placed near the source to stop unwanted traffic as early as possible, conserving bandwidth. Standard ACLs should be placed near the destination.

questions.question: Which ACL statement permits only traffic from host 10.1.1.5 to web server 172.16.1.10 on port 80?
options: access-list 101 permit tcp host 10.1.1.5 host 172.16.1.10 eq 80
questions.explanation: Extended ACL syntax: `access-list <100-199> permit|deny <protocol> <src> <src-wildcard> <dst> <dst-wildcard> [eq <port>]`. The `host` keyword = 0.0.0.0 wildcard.

questions.question: When a router has multiple routes to the same destination with different administrative distances, which is preferred?
options: The route with the highest AD
The route with the lowest AD
The route with the most specific prefix
The route from the most recent update
questions.explanation: Lower AD = more preferred. AD indicates trustworthiness of the routing source. Connected=0, Static=1, EIGRP=90, OSPF=110, RIP=120. Lower is better.

questions.question: What happens if a router has two routes with the same AD and same metric to a destination?
options: The route with the more specific prefix wins
Both routes are installed and traffic is load-balanced (ECMP)
questions.explanation: Equal-cost multipath (ECMP) — when routes have identical AD and metric, most routing protocols install both and load-balance traffic across them.

questions.question: What IOS command shows only the OSPF routes in the routing table?
questions.explanation: `show ip route ospf` filters the routing table to display only OSPF-learned routes. Similarly, `show ip route static`, `show ip route eigrp`, etc. filter by source.

questions.question: What is a default route and what is its CIDR notation?
options: A route to the loopback network; 127.0.0.0/8
A catch-all route for unknown destinations; 0.0.0.0/0
A summary route for all RFC 1918 space; 10.0.0.0/8
A route to the management network; 192.168.0.0/16
questions.explanation: The default route (0.0.0.0/0) matches any destination. It's the 'gateway of last resort' — used when no more specific route exists. Configured with `ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 <next-hop>`.

questions.question: What is route summarization (supernetting) and what is its primary benefit?
options: Breaking a large network into smaller subnets; reduces broadcast domains
Combining multiple contiguous networks into one advertisement; reduces routing table size
Redistributing routes between protocols; improves convergence
Creating redundant routes; improves fault tolerance
questions.explanation: Route summarization combines multiple contiguous subnet advertisements into one entry, reducing routing table size, speeding up lookups, and improving convergence by limiting LSA/update propagation.

questions.question: What is a floating static route?
options: A static route with no next-hop
A static route with an AD higher than the dynamic protocol, used as a backup
A default route from the ISP
questions.explanation: A floating static route has an administrative distance higher than the dynamic routing protocol (e.g., AD 200 vs OSPF 110). It only appears in the routing table if the dynamic route fails.

questions.question: What is the maximum hop count RIPv2 supports before a route is considered unreachable?
questions.explanation: RIP maximum hop count = 15. A hop count of 16 means 'unreachable/infinity'. This severely limits RIP's scalability in larger networks.

questions.question: How often does RIPv2 send routing updates by default?
questions.explanation: RIPv2 sends full routing table updates every 30 seconds as broadcasts (RIPv1) or multicasts to 224.0.0.9 (RIPv2). This is periodic, unlike OSPF/EIGRP which are event-driven.

questions.question: A packet arrives for 10.1.1.45. The routing table has: 10.0.0.0/8, 10.1.0.0/16, 10.1.1.0/24. Which route is used?
questions.explanation: Longest prefix match wins. 10.1.1.0/24 is the most specific (/24 > /16 > /8) route that matches 10.1.1.45, so it's selected regardless of which protocol learned it.

questions.question: Which OSPFv3 feature allows it to support IPv6?
options: It uses the same packet format as OSPFv2
OSPFv3 runs over IPv6 link-local addresses and supports multiple address families
OSPFv3 uses UDP instead of IP protocol 89
OSPFv3 requires manual router IDs in IPv6 format
questions.explanation: OSPFv3 forms adjacencies over IPv6 link-local addresses (FE80::/10) and supports IPv6 (and optionally IPv4 in address-family mode). Router IDs remain 32-bit dotted decimal.

questions.question: What is the IPv6 address ::1 used for?
questions.explanation: ::1 is the IPv6 loopback address, equivalent to 127.0.0.1 in IPv4. It is used for testing the local IP stack and is never forwarded by routers.

questions.question: Which IPv6 transition mechanism encapsulates IPv6 packets inside IPv4 packets?
questions.explanation: Tunneling (e.g., 6in4, 6to4) encapsulates IPv6 packets within IPv4 headers for transport across IPv4-only infrastructure. Dual-stack runs both protocols natively. NAT64 translates between IPv4 and IPv6.

questions.question: What is an OSPF stub area?
options: An area that does not receive Type 5 External LSAs, receiving a default route instead
An area with only one router
An area that does not run SPF
questions.explanation: An OSPF stub area blocks Type 5 (external) LSAs from entering, replacing them with a default route (0.0.0.0/0) injected by the ABR. Reduces router memory/CPU in remote areas.

questions.question: What type of updates does EIGRP send (unlike RIP's full-table updates)?
options: Partial, bounded updates only for changed routes
Unicast to all known routers every 10 seconds
questions.explanation: EIGRP sends partial updates only for routes that changed, and only to routers that need the information (bounded). This is much more efficient than RIP's full table every 30s.

questions.question: What is a directly connected route and what administrative distance does it have?
options: A route learned via OSPF; AD=110
A route to a network configured on a router's interface that is up; AD=0
A route from another router; AD=1
questions.explanation: A directly connected (C) route represents a network configured on an active router interface. AD=0 — most trusted source. These routes are automatically in the routing table when an interface is up/up.

questions.question: What command configures a static route to network 10.2.0.0/16 via next-hop 192.168.1.1?
questions.explanation: IOS static route syntax: `ip route <network> <subnet-mask> <next-hop-ip or exit-interface>`. Alternatively use the exit interface: `ip route 10.2.0.0 255.255.0.0 GigabitEthernet0/1`.

questions.question: What command configures a static IPv6 default route on a Cisco router?
questions.explanation: `ipv6 route ::/0 <next-hop>` configures the IPv6 default route. ::/0 is the IPv6 equivalent of 0.0.0.0/0. `ipv6 unicast-routing` must be enabled first.

questions.question: What does `show ip protocols` display?
options: Active routing protocols, their timers, networks advertised, and neighbors
questions.explanation: `show ip protocols` shows which dynamic routing protocols are running, their configured networks, timers, redistribution, and neighbor information — useful for verifying routing protocol configuration.

questions.question: What command enables OSPF on all interfaces in the 10.0.0.0/8 network, assigning them to area 0?
questions.explanation: OSPF `network` command uses wildcard masks: `network 10.0.0.0 0.255.255.255 area 0`. This matches any interface with an IP starting with 10. and enables OSPF on it in area 0.

questions.question: What OSPF command enables authentication using a simple plaintext password on an interface?
questions.explanation: Simple OSPF auth requires two commands on the interface: `ip ospf authentication` (enables simple auth) and `ip ospf authentication-key <password>`. MD5 auth uses `ip ospf authentication message-digest`.

questions.question: What does the 'C' code in the routing table indicate?
questions.explanation: Route codes: C = Connected, S = Static, O = OSPF, D = EIGRP, R = RIP, B = BGP, L = Local (the interface's own address, added since IOS 15.x).

questions.question: What is the OSPF passive-interface command used for?
options: Disables OSPF on the interface entirely
Prevents OSPF hello packets from being sent out an interface while still advertising its network
Increases hello timer on the interface
Makes the interface a stub network
questions.explanation: `passive-interface <int>` stops hello packets from being sent/received on that interface (no OSPF neighbors), but the connected network is still included in OSPF advertisements. Used on LAN interfaces facing end devices.

questions.question: Which table does EIGRP use to store all possible routes to a destination, including feasible successors?
questions.explanation: EIGRP maintains three tables: (1) Neighbor table (active EIGRP peers), (2) Topology table (all paths, including feasible successors), (3) Routing table (only the best routes/successors).

questions.question: What is the IPv6 unique local address range and what is it analogous to in IPv4?
options: FC00::/7 — analogous to RFC 1918 private addresses
questions.explanation: FC00::/7 (includes FD00::/8 which is the usable range) is the IPv6 unique local address — similar to RFC 1918 private space. Not routed on the public Internet; used within organizations.

questions.question: How many hexadecimal groups are in a full IPv6 address?
questions.explanation: IPv6 is 128 bits, written as 8 groups of 4 hex digits separated by colons (e.g., 2001:0DB8:ACAD:0001:0000:0000:0000:0001). :: shortens consecutive all-zero groups.

questions.question: What is DHCPv6 used for and what does SLAAC not provide that DHCPv6 can?
options: DHCPv6 can provide additional parameters (DNS server, domain name) that SLAAC Router Advertisements alone cannot reliably provide
DHCPv6 only works in IPv6-only networks
questions.explanation: SLAAC provides prefix and default gateway via RA but cannot push DNS/DHCP options natively. DHCPv6 (stateful or stateless) provides DNS server addresses and other parameters. DHCPv6 uses UDP 546 (client) / 547 (server).

questions.question: What does the 'M' flag (Managed Address Configuration) in an IPv6 Router Advertisement indicate?
options: Multicast is available on the link
Hosts should use stateful DHCPv6 to obtain their IPv6 address
The router is the default gateway
questions.explanation: The M flag (Managed) in RA tells hosts to use stateful DHCPv6 for address assignment. The O flag (Other) tells hosts to use DHCPv6 for other info (DNS) but use SLAAC for the address.

questions.question: What is BGP primarily used for?
options: Routing between autonomous systems on the internet (EGP) — the routing protocol of the internet
Providing fast convergence in enterprise networks
Load balancing within a data center
questions.explanation: BGP (Border Gateway Protocol) is the internet's routing protocol — an EGP (Exterior Gateway Protocol) that exchanges routes between autonomous systems (ASes). Uses TCP 179. ISPs and large enterprises use it for internet connectivity.

questions.question: What is policy-based routing (PBR) used for?
options: Routing traffic based on criteria beyond destination IP (e.g., source IP, application, QoS mark) using route maps
questions.explanation: PBR overrides normal destination-based routing using route maps. It can route traffic differently based on source address, packet size, or DSCP marking — useful for traffic engineering.

questions.question: What is the OSPF DR election priority rule?
options: Highest OSPF priority wins (default 1); ties broken by highest Router ID
questions.explanation: On broadcast networks, the router with the highest OSPF priority (default 1) becomes DR. Priority 0 = never elected. Ties broken by highest Router ID. Priority can be changed with `ip ospf priority <0-255>`.

questions.question: What does the `traceroute` command reveal that `ping` does not?
options: The path (each router hop) packets take to reach the destination and latency at each hop
questions.explanation: `traceroute` uses TTL manipulation to reveal each router in the path and round-trip time to each hop. `ping` only confirms end-to-end reachability. Both help locate where in the path failures occur.

questions.question: A PC cannot ping its default gateway but can ping 127.0.0.1. What does this isolate?
options: The TCP/IP stack is working but there is a Layer 1/2/3 issue between the PC and gateway (cable, switchport, IP config, gateway config)
questions.explanation: 127.0.0.1 (loopback) success = TCP/IP stack is working. Failure to ping gateway = problem is external: check physical connection, IP address/mask config, VLAN assignment, or gateway configuration.

questions.question: Which two IPv6 addresses are required on any IPv6-enabled interface?
options: A global unicast and a unique local address
A link-local address (FE80::/10) and at least one unicast address (global or unique local)
A link-local and a multicast address
questions.explanation: Every IPv6-enabled interface automatically gets a link-local address (FE80::/10) via EUI-64 or random generation. Additionally, it needs a routable unicast address (global or unique local) for communication beyond the link.

questions.question: What is the purpose of route redistribution?
options: Imports routes from one routing protocol domain into another (e.g., OSPF into EIGRP) to enable communication between mixed-protocol networks
Provides load balancing between equal-cost routes
questions.explanation: Route redistribution injects routes learned by one routing protocol into another. Example: `redistribute ospf 1 metric 10000 100 255 1 1500` injects OSPF routes into EIGRP. Requires careful metric conversion to avoid loops.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the IPv6 solicited-node multicast address?
options: Used by routers to advertise default routes
Used for Neighbor Discovery (NDP) to resolve IPv6 addresses to MAC addresses, replacing ARP broadcasts
questions.explanation: Each IPv6 address has a corresponding solicited-node multicast (FF02::1:FF<last 24 bits of IPv6>). NDP Neighbor Solicitation is sent to this address — only the target host listens, avoiding broadcast flooding.

questions.question: What is an ASBR in OSPF?
options: Autonomous System Boundary Router — a router that redistributes external routes (from BGP, EIGRP, or static) into OSPF, generating Type 5 External LSAs
A router with only one OSPF area
An ABR with more than two areas
questions.explanation: An ASBR (Autonomous System Boundary Router) connects OSPF to other routing domains (BGP, EIGRP, RIP, static). It generates Type 5 LSAs advertising external routes into OSPF. ABRs connect OSPF areas to Area 0.

questions.question: `show ip route` shows '10.0.0.0/8 is variably subnetted'. What does this indicate?
options: VLSM — multiple subnets of the major network exist with different prefix lengths
questions.explanation: 'Variably subnetted' means VLSM is in use — the major network (10.0.0.0/8) has been divided into subnets of different sizes (/24, /30, etc.). The router knows about multiple prefixes within that major network.

questions.question: What does the `ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 Null0` configuration accomplish?
options: A 'black hole' or discard route — packets matching this route are silently dropped (used with summary routes to prevent routing loops)
Creates a default route to the internet
questions.explanation: A Null0 static route discards packets that match. Used with summary routes: if the summary matches but no more specific route exists, the packet is dropped instead of looping via the default route.

questions.question: What is OSPF incremental SPF (iSPF)?
options: A faster version of OSPF for small networks
An optimization that recalculates only the changed portion of the SPF tree instead of running a full SPF calculation on every topology change
A feature that reduces LSA flooding
questions.explanation: iSPF reduces CPU overhead by recalculating only the affected branch of the SPF tree when a change occurs, rather than running full Dijkstra from scratch. Enabled with `ispf` under OSPF process.

questions.question: What is an OSPF totally stubby area?
options: An area with no external LSAs
An area that blocks both Type 3 (inter-area) AND Type 5 (external) LSAs — only a default route is injected by the ABR, minimizing the LSDB
An area with only one router
A stub area with no ABR
questions.explanation: Totally stubby areas (Cisco extension) block Type 3 (inter-area summary), Type 4, and Type 5 (external) LSAs. The ABR injects only a Type 3 default route (0.0.0.0/0). Minimizes memory/CPU in remote areas.

questions.question: What is the EIGRP hello and hold timer on a LAN interface by default?
questions.explanation: EIGRP on LAN (high-bandwidth): hello=5s, hold=15s. On WAN/low-bandwidth (T1 or slower): hello=60s, hold=180s. Hold timer = 3x hello. Neighbors declared down if hold timer expires without a hello.

questions.question: What is the EIGRP Feasibility Condition (FC)?
options: The neighbor's AD must equal the current successor's FD
The neighbor's Reported Distance (RD) must be strictly less than the current successor's Feasible Distance (FD)
The neighbor must be in the same area
The path cost must be less than 15
questions.explanation: FC: A route is a Feasible Successor only if the neighbor's Reported Distance (RD = their cost) < current Successor's Feasible Distance (FD = our best cost). This mathematically guarantees loop-freedom.

questions.question: What is the IPv6 multicast address FF02::5 used for?
options: OSPF — All OSPF routers (equivalent to 224.0.0.5 in IPv4)
questions.explanation: FF02::5 = OSPFv3 all SPF routers (equivalent to IPv4 224.0.0.5). FF02::6 = OSPFv3 all DRs (equivalent to 224.0.0.6). FF02::9 = RIPng. FF02::A = EIGRP. FF02::1:2 = DHCPv6 relay agents.

questions.question: What is the difference between stateful and stateless DHCPv6?
options: Stateful uses SLAAC; stateless uses DHCPv6
Stateful DHCPv6 assigns the IPv6 address AND other info; stateless DHCPv6 only provides other config (DNS) while SLAAC provides the address
questions.explanation: Stateful DHCPv6: server assigns complete IPv6 address + prefix + DNS (M flag set in RA). Stateless DHCPv6 (O flag in RA): client uses SLAAC for the address but queries DHCPv6 server only for DNS/other options.

questions.question: How does NDP Neighbor Discovery replace ARP in IPv6?
options: NDP uses ICMPv6 Neighbor Solicitation (multicast to solicited-node address) and Neighbor Advertisement — avoiding broadcast flooding
NDP uses a separate protocol port
questions.explanation: IPv6 NDP replaces ARP using ICMPv6: Neighbor Solicitation (type 135) sent to the target's solicited-node multicast address (not broadcast), Neighbor Advertisement (type 136) sent in reply. Much more efficient than ARP broadcasts.

questions.question: What is IPv6 privacy extensions (RFC 4941) and why is it used?
options: Generates random, temporary interface IDs instead of EUI-64 (MAC-based) addresses to prevent tracking of devices across different networks
questions.explanation: RFC 4941 privacy extensions generate random interface IDs that change periodically, replacing the predictable EUI-64 (derived from MAC address). This prevents user/device tracking based on a stable IPv6 address.

questions.question: Two OSPF routers are stuck in EXSTART/EXCHANGE state. What is the likely cause?
options: MTU mismatch — if one router has a larger MTU, DBD packets may be fragmented and cause negotiation to stall
questions.explanation: EXSTART/EXCHANGE stuck usually indicates MTU mismatch. OSPF DBD (Database Description) packets can be as large as the interface MTU. If one side has MTU 1500 and the other 1476, packets get fragmented/dropped.

questions.question: An OSPF neighbor is in 2-WAY state and never progresses to FULL. What does this indicate?
options: The routers are DROther on a broadcast segment — 2-WAY is normal between DROther routers; they only form FULL adjacency with the DR and BDR
questions.explanation: On broadcast networks, DROther routers only form FULL adjacency with the DR and BDR. Between two DROther routers, 2-WAY is the expected (correct) state — not a problem. Full mesh would be wasteful.

questions.question: What does OSPF LSA Type 4 describe?
options: The path to an ASBR (ASBR Summary LSA) — generated by ABRs to tell other areas how to reach the ASBR
questions.explanation: Type 4 LSA (ASBR Summary LSA) is generated by an ABR to advertise the path to an ASBR into other areas. Type 5 External LSAs generated by the ASBR need Type 4 to be routable across area boundaries.

questions.question: What is OSPF NSF (Non-Stop Forwarding)?
options: A feature that speeds up SPF calculation
Allows a router to continue forwarding traffic during an OSPF process restart by using the last known routing information — minimizing traffic disruption
questions.explanation: NSF/graceful restart allows a router to continue packet forwarding using stale routes during a process restart (software upgrade, process crash). Neighboring routers cooperate by not withdrawing their LSAs during the restart period.

questions.question: What is the purpose of OSPF `ip ospf network point-to-point` on an Ethernet interface?
options: Changes the interface to use HDLC encapsulation
Tells OSPF to treat the Ethernet link as a point-to-point connection, skipping DR/BDR election and directly forming full adjacency — useful for /30 or /31 subnets with only two routers
questions.explanation: Setting `ip ospf network point-to-point` on an Ethernet interface with only two OSPF routers avoids the DR/BDR election overhead and speeds up adjacency formation. The two routers form a direct FULL adjacency.

questions.question: What happens in EIGRP when a route's successor fails and there is NO feasible successor?
options: EIGRP sends the route to Active state and queries all neighbors for an alternate path — if no reply within the active timer, the route is marked Stuck-In-Active (SIA)
EIGRP uses RIP as a backup
questions.explanation: Without a FS, EIGRP sets the route to Active and sends Query packets to all neighbors. If a Query is unanswered within the active time (3 min default), the neighbor is declared SIA and reset — a sign of a network design problem.

questions.question: What is EIGRP named mode and how does it differ from classic mode?
options: A naming convention for EIGRP processes
