A configuration mode (`router eigrp <name>`) that provides a hierarchical address-family structure, supports IPv4 and IPv6 in one process, and has additional features over classic `router eigrp <AS>`
questions.explanation: EIGRP named mode uses `router eigrp <name>` with address-family sub-configuration for IPv4/IPv6. It provides better scalability, supports named configurations that are easier to manage, and is the recommended modern approach.

questions.question: What IOS command verifies EIGRP neighbors and their states?
questions.explanation: `show ip eigrp neighbors` lists EIGRP neighbor adjacencies: neighbor IP, interface, uptime, hold time, Q count (pending sends), and sequence numbers. Q count > 0 for extended time indicates retransmission issues.

questions.question: An ACL has: `permit tcp any host 10.1.1.1 eq 80` and `deny ip any any`. What traffic reaches 10.1.1.1?
options: Only TCP port 80 traffic — all other traffic (UDP, ICMP, other TCP ports) is dropped by the implicit deny
No traffic — the deny overrides the permit
questions.explanation: ACLs are processed top-down, first match wins. TCP port 80 matches the permit statement. Everything else (TCP 443, ICMP ping, UDP) falls through to `deny ip any any` and is dropped. The permit only covers HTTP.

questions.question: What is a reflexive ACL?
options: An ACL that applies to both inbound and outbound simultaneously
An ACL that dynamically creates temporary permit entries for return traffic of established sessions — providing stateful-like filtering with named extended ACLs
An ACL applied to the loopback interface
An ACL that denies all traffic
questions.explanation: Reflexive ACLs use `permit ip reflect <name>` to dynamically add temporary permit entries for return traffic when a session is initiated. They expire after the session ends. Simpler alternative to a full stateful firewall on IOS.

questions.question: What IOS command inserts a new entry at line 15 in a named ACL called CORP-ACL?
options:
ip access-list extended CORP-ACL
 15 permit tcp 10.0.0.0 0.0.0.255 any eq 443
questions.explanation: Named ACLs support sequence numbers. `ip access-list extended CORP-ACL` then `15 permit tcp 10.0.0.0 0.0.0.255 any eq 443` inserts a new entry at sequence number 15. Entries are processed in numeric order.

questions.question: What does SLAAC require from the network to generate a host's IPv6 address?
options: A DHCPv6 server with a pool
A /64 prefix advertised in a Router Advertisement (RA) — the host combines this with a self-generated 64-bit interface ID
questions.explanation: SLAAC: host sends RS to FF02::2 > router replies with RA containing /64 prefix > host appends interface ID (EUI-64 or random) > runs DAD to verify uniqueness. No DHCP server needed for address assignment.

questions.question: What is Duplicate Address Detection (DAD) in IPv6?
options: Before using a new IPv6 address, host sends NS to its solicited-node multicast — if no NA reply is received, the address is unique and usable
A router feature preventing duplicate routes
questions.explanation: DAD sends an NS (Neighbor Solicitation) to the solicited-node multicast of the tentative address. If no NA reply comes back, the address is unique. If an NA reply arrives, another device has the same address — conflict.

questions.question: What is a recursive vs. non-recursive static route?
options: Recursive specifies only a next-hop IP (router must look up exit interface); non-recursive specifies exit interface directly
Recursive routes are for IPv6 only
questions.explanation: Recursive: `ip route 10.0.0.0 255.0.0.0 192.168.1.1` (router looks up 192.168.1.1 to find exit interface). Non-recursive: `ip route 10.0.0.0 255.0.0.0 GigabitEthernet0/0` (exit interface explicit). Both work; non-recursive is slightly faster.

questions.question: What does BFD (Bidirectional Forwarding Detection) provide for OSPF?
options: Sub-second failure detection that immediately triggers OSPF reconvergence — much faster than waiting for the 40-second OSPF dead timer
questions.explanation: BFD sends rapid hellos (milliseconds) between neighboring devices. When BFD detects a link failure, it immediately notifies OSPF (and other protocols) to converge — versus waiting for the 40-second OSPF dead timer.

questions.question: What is a routing loop and how do distance-vector protocols prevent it?
options: A condition where routers continuously forward packets to each other because both think the other has a better path. Prevented by split horizon, hold-down timers, and poison reverse
questions.explanation: Routing loops cause packets to circulate indefinitely until TTL expires. DV protocols prevent loops with: split horizon (don't advertise a route back out the interface it was learned on), poison reverse (advertise failed routes with infinity metric), hold-down timers.

questions.question: What is split horizon and why is it important?
options: A way to divide routing tables
A loop-prevention rule: do not advertise a route back out the interface it was learned on — prevents two-node routing loops
questions.explanation: Split horizon: if Router A learned about network X from Router B (via interface Gi0), A does not advertise X back to B via Gi0. This prevents A and B from each thinking the other has a valid path when the route fails.

questions.question: What is poison reverse (route poisoning)?
options: Advertising a failed route with an infinite metric back out the interface it was learned on — more aggressive than split horizon to prevent loops
A mechanism used only by EIGRP
questions.explanation: Poison reverse is a stronger alternative to split horizon: when a route fails, the router advertises it back with metric=infinity. The neighbor immediately marks it unreachable instead of waiting for timers to expire.

questions.question: What does RIP count to infinity mean?
options: When a route fails, routers may increment its metric back and forth (counting up to 16) before declaring it unreachable — causing long convergence
questions.explanation: Without loop prevention, failed RIP routes increment hop count between routers until they reach the maximum (16 = infinity/unreachable). This slow convergence is called 'counting to infinity'. Hold-down timers and split horizon mitigate this.

questions.question: What is the IPv6 global unicast address range and its defining characteristic?
options: 2000::/3 — globally routable, assigned by IANA, begins with 2 or 3 in the first hex digit
questions.explanation: Global unicast addresses (2000::/3) are publicly routable — equivalent to public IPv4 addresses. IANA assigns blocks to RIRs, which assign to ISPs, which assign to customers. Currently allocated range: 2000:: to 3FFF::.

questions.question: What is the IPv6 header fixed size and what advantage does this provide?
options: 40 bytes — fixed size simplifies router processing since there is no variable-length Options field in the main header
questions.explanation: The IPv6 base header is always 40 bytes (vs IPv4's variable 20-60 bytes). Fixed size enables faster hardware processing. IPv6 extension headers replace IPv4's options field — they appear between the base header and upper-layer payload.

questions.question: How does IPv6 handle fragmentation differently from IPv4?
options: IPv6 fragments at any router like IPv4
Only the source host performs IPv6 fragmentation using the Fragment extension header — routers do NOT fragment IPv6 packets
IPv6 uses ICMP to request fragmentation
questions.explanation: In IPv6, intermediate routers do NOT fragment packets. If a packet is too large, routers send an ICMPv6 'Packet Too Big' message. Only the source host (using Path MTU Discovery) fragments packets via the Fragment extension header.

questions.question: What is the default administrative distance for OSPF?
questions.explanation: OSPF has an AD of 110. Common ADs: Connected=0, Static=1, EIGRP internal=90, OSPF=110, IS-IS=115, RIP=120, BGP external=20.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 26; CCNA 200-301 OCG Ch. 19

questions.question: What is the OSPF Hello interval on a broadcast multi-access network by default?
questions.explanation: OSPF Hello interval is 10 seconds on broadcast/point-to-point links and 30 seconds on non-broadcast links. Dead interval defaults to 4× Hello (40s on broadcast).
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 27; CCNA 200-301 OCG Ch. 20

questions.question: Which OSPF LSA type describes routes external to the OSPF domain?
questions.explanation: Type 5 LSAs (AS External) are flooded throughout the OSPF domain (except stub areas) and describe routes redistributed into OSPF from other protocols. They are generated by ASBRs.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 28; CCNA 200-301 OCG Ch. 21

questions.question: On a broadcast segment, OSPF elects a DR and BDR. Which router becomes DR?
options: Router with the highest OSPF priority, then highest Router ID
Router with the lowest Router ID
Router that sent the first Hello
questions.explanation: DR election: highest interface OSPF priority (default 1) wins. Ties broken by highest Router ID. Priority 0 = ineligible. DR/BDR election is non-preemptive.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 27; CCNA 200-301 OCG Ch. 20

questions.question: Which command configures a default route on a Cisco router?
questions.explanation: 'ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 [next-hop | exit-interface]' creates a default route (quad-zero route). 'ip default-gateway' is used on Layer 2 switches for management traffic only.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 25; CCNA 200-301 OCG Ch. 18

questions.question: What is the OSPF metric (cost) formula for Cisco routers?
questions.explanation: OSPF cost = Reference bandwidth / Interface bandwidth. Default reference = 100 Mbps (10^8 bps). A 100 Mbps interface has cost 1; a 10 Mbps interface has cost 10. Adjust with 'auto-cost reference-bandwidth' for Gigabit+ links.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 26; CCNA 200-301 OCG Ch. 20

questions.question: Router A is in OSPF area 0. It has three interfaces: G0/0 (cost 1), G0/1 (cost 4), S0/0 (cost 10). What is the total OSPF cost to reach a network connected to Router B (cost 1 on Router B's interface) via G0/0?
questions.explanation: OSPF cost is cumulative from the calculating router to the destination. Cost = outgoing interface cost (G0/0 = 1) + Router B's interface cost (1) = 2. OSPF does NOT count the incoming interface on the far-end router.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 26; CCNA 200-301 OCG Ch. 20

questions.question: Which IPv6 routing protocol is the IPv6-capable version of OSPF?
questions.explanation: OSPFv3 supports IPv6 routing. It operates on the same SPF algorithm as OSPFv2 but uses IPv6 link-local addresses for neighbor communication and supports multiple address families.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34; CCNA 200-301 OCG Ch. 25

questions.question: What is the IPv6 all-OSPF-routers multicast address?
questions.explanation: FF02::5 is the all-OSPF-routers multicast address (equivalent to 224.0.0.5 in IPv4). FF02::6 is all-DR-routers (224.0.0.6). FF02::1 is all-nodes; FF02::2 is all-routers.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34; CCNA 200-301 OCG Ch. 25

questions.question: Which type of IPv6 address is automatically assigned to every IPv6-enabled interface and is used for link-local communication?
questions.explanation: Link-local addresses (FE80::/10) are auto-configured on every IPv6 interface using EUI-64 or random generation. They are used for neighbor discovery, routing protocol hellos, and default gateway communication — not routable beyond the local link.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 31; CCNA 200-301 OCG Ch. 23

questions.question: What is the purpose of Router-on-a-Stick (ROAS) for inter-VLAN routing?
options: Uses multiple physical router interfaces, one per VLAN
Uses a single physical interface with 802.1Q subinterfaces for each VLAN
Uses the switch's SVI for routing between VLANs
questions.explanation: ROAS uses a single router physical interface connected to a trunk port, with logical subinterfaces (e.g., G0/0.10, G0/0.20) each tagged with a VLAN ID using 802.1Q encapsulation.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 16; CCNA 200-301 OCG Ch. 17

questions.question: Which command enables IPv6 routing on a Cisco router?
questions.explanation: 'ipv6 unicast-routing' (global config) enables IPv6 forwarding on a Cisco router. Without this, the router will not forward IPv6 packets between interfaces.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 31; CCNA 200-301 OCG Ch. 23

questions.question: A floating static route is configured with AD 200 pointing to ISP-B. The primary OSPF route (AD 110) via ISP-A is in the routing table. What happens when the OSPF route is lost?
options: The floating static route is installed in the routing table
The router drops all traffic to that destination
The router uses the connected route
questions.explanation: A floating static route has a higher AD than the primary protocol. It stays out of the RIB while the preferred route exists. When OSPF loses the route, the floating static route's AD (200) is now the only option, so it gets installed.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 25; CCNA 200-301 OCG Ch. 18

questions.question: Which OSPF network type does NOT elect a DR/BDR?
questions.explanation: Point-to-Point OSPF network type (used on serial links and P2P Ethernet) does not elect DR/BDR since there are only two routers. Broadcast and Non-Broadcast types elect DR/BDR.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 27; CCNA 200-301 OCG Ch. 20

questions.question: What does the 'ip ospf passive-interface' command do?
options: Stops OSPF from forming neighbors on that interface but still advertises the subnet
Removes the interface from OSPF completely
Enables OSPF on the interface without advertising it
Sets the interface as a stub link
questions.explanation: A passive interface stops sending/receiving OSPF Hello packets (no adjacency formed) but still includes the connected subnet in OSPF LSAs. Useful for LAN interfaces connected to end hosts.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 26; CCNA 200-301 OCG Ch. 20

questions.question: What is the IPv6 prefix length equivalent of an IPv4 /24?
questions.explanation: The /24 prefix length concept is the same in IPv6 — it indicates 24 bits of network prefix. However, in practice, IPv6 LAN subnets typically use /64. The prefix length notation is identical.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 31; CCNA 200-301 OCG Ch. 23

questions.question: Which command advertises a default route into OSPF from a router that has a default route configured?
questions.explanation: 'default-information originate' (OSPF router config) causes the router to generate a Type 5 LSA for the default route (0.0.0.0/0) if a default route exists. Add 'always' to advertise regardless.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 26; CCNA 200-301 OCG Ch. 20

questions.question: OSPF uses Dijkstra's Shortest Path First (SPF) algorithm to calculate the best path.
questions.explanation: OSPF is a link-state protocol. Each router builds an identical LSDB and independently runs Dijkstra's SPF algorithm to find the shortest (lowest-cost) path to every destination.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 26; CCNA 200-301 OCG Ch. 20

questions.question: EIGRP is an open standard routing protocol supported by all router vendors.
questions.explanation: EIGRP (Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol) is Cisco proprietary, though Cisco published an informational RFC (RFC 7868) in 2016. It is NOT an open standard and is primarily used in Cisco environments.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 29; CCNA 200-301 OCG Ch. 22

questions.question: A router uses the longest prefix match when selecting a route from multiple matching entries.
questions.explanation: Longest prefix match = most specific route wins. /28 beats /24 beats /16 etc. Only if prefix lengths are equal does administrative distance then metric come into play.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 24; CCNA 200-301 OCG Ch. 18

questions.question: OSPFv3 is used exclusively for IPv6 routing and cannot route IPv4.
questions.explanation: OSPFv3 was originally designed for IPv6 but was extended (RFC 5838) to support multiple address families including IPv4. In modern implementations, OSPFv3 can route both IPv4 and IPv6.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34; CCNA 200-301 OCG Ch. 25

questions.question: Order the OSPF neighbor adjacency states from initial to Full:
questions.explanation: OSPF states: Down → Init → 2-Way (DR/BDR election here) → ExStart → Exchange → Loading → Full. '2-Way' means Hello received; 'Full' means LSDB synchronized.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 27; CCNA 200-301 OCG Ch. 20

questions.question: What is the significance of :: in the IPv6 address 2001:0:11:1::1:1AB1/64?
options: It is used to represent a single section of 0000.
It is used to represent consecutive sections of 0000.
It is used to represent a single section of 1111.
It is used to represent consecutive sections of 1111.
questions.explanation: The :: may be used once in an address in order to represent consecutive sections of 0000.

questions.question: What command causes a router interface to autoconfigure its host portion of the address?
questions.explanation: The modified EUI-64 method is used in this example.

questions.question: What command allows you to see the multicast addresses that an interface has joined in IPv6?
questions.explanation: The show ipv6 interface command provides this level of detail. It shows the multicast and link-local addressing joined.

questions.question: If you are using DHCP combined with the SLAAC feature in IPv6, you are most likely using what form of DHCP?
questions.explanation: Stateless DHCP is often used with SLAAC.

questions.question: If you use the ipv6 enable command on an interface, what address do you have on that interface?
questions.explanation: The ipv6 enable command ensures that a link-local address exists. questions.question: What must be in place before you can route other devices’ IPv6 traffic through a Cisco router?
options: A loopback 0 interface with an IPv4 address assigned
The ipv6 unicast-routing global configuration command
questions.explanation: Cisco routers are able to support IPv6 on interfaces by default. They cannot, however, route other devices’ IPv6 traffic without the global ipv6 unicast-routing command.

questions.question: What prefix is reserved for IPv6 link-local addresses?
questions.explanation: fe80::/10 is the prefix reserved for link-local addressing in IPv6.

questions.question: Which of the following is used by an IPv6 multicast address?
questions.explanation: Multicast addresses in IPv6 use the ff00::/8 prefix (per RFC 4291, section 2.7).

questions.question: What command does a client use to enable stateless autoconfiguration of IPv6 address information on an interface?
questions.explanation: The ipv6 address autoconfig command is used on the client. The ipv6 address enable command does not exist. The ipv6 enable com- mand enables IPv6 and configures only an IPv6 link-local address using EUI-64 on the interface. The ipv6 address dhcp command relies on a DHCP server to determine configuration information in order to assign an IPv6 address to the interface. The ipv6 address autoconfig command only relies on a router trans- mitting RAs to determine a global IPv6 addr

questions.question: Which kind of IPv6 address is not routable?

questions.question: Which IPv6 address type involves configuring the same address on multiple hosts?
questions.explanation: Anycast An anycast address is indistinguishable from a unicast address; the only difference is that an anycast address is configured on multiple hosts. This is useful when providing services over the internet; by configuring the same IP address on servers in different global locations, client devices can access the server closest to their location. questions.question: If a router cannot find a better match, what might the router use to route traffic?
questions.explanation: Routers use the default route (0.0.0.0/0) to send packets that have no other better match in the routing table.

questions.question: When a router forwards packets onto Ethernet, what is rewritten?
options: The source and destination IP addresses
The source and destination MAC addresses
questions.explanation: The router must rewrite the source and destination MAC addresses in the Ethernet header before forwarding a frame.

What command permits you to view the IPv6 routing table?
questions.explanation: The show ipv6 route command is the equivalent of show ip route but shows the IPv6 routing table.

questions.question: What command enters router configuration mode for OSPF version 2?
questions.explanation: The router ospf 1 command enters router configuration mode for OSPF. It uses the local process ID 1.

questions.question: You have configured OSPF on a router by using the command network 10.10.0.0 0.0.255.255 area 0. On which interface is OSPF running?
questions.explanation: The network 10.10.0.0 0.0.255.255 area 0 command ensures that OSPF runs on any interfaces that have IP addresses that have 10.10 in the first two octets. This is the Gi0/1 interface in this question.

questions.question: What does not have to match in order for an OSPF neighborship to form?
options: Use of the network command versus the ip ospf command
questions.explanation: Neighborships can form in OSPF if one router uses the network command and the other uses the interface-level ip ospf command. Process IDs do not need to match between routers either.

questions.question: You have allowed a router to self-assign its router ID. What is the first option considered for the assignment on the device?
options: The IP address on the highest-numbered interface name
The highest IP address on a physical interface
The highest IP address on a loopback interface
questions.explanation: If you do not manually configure a router ID, the highest IP address on a loopback interface is used as the router ID. If there are no loopback interfaces, the router uses the highest IP address on a physical interface. If OSPF cannot find a configured IPv4 address, the OSPF process does not start.

questions.question: Which network type in OSPF features the use of a DR and a BDR?
questions.explanation: The DR and BDR devices are used in the broadcast and non- broadcast network types. Using the routing table of the IGP
questions.explanation: Virtual MAC addresses are used to distribute the load from clients among healthy routers in the infrastructure.

questions.question: In HSRP, what is the term for routers that are not forwarding traffic for the LAN but are waiting to see if they are needed?
questions.explanation: HSRP uses the concept of active routers and standby routers.

questions.question: In VRRP, what is the name of the router that is forwarding traffic for the clients?
questions.explanation: The forwarding device in VRRP is termed the virtual router master. questions.question: A network engineer has configured a Layer 3 142 switch with SVIs for VLANs 4 and 5. A VLAN interface will only be up/up if the matching VLAN (with the same VLAN number) exists on the switch. questions.explanation: 192.168.222.127 To calculate this, write the final octet in binary: 0d101 = 0b01100101. Then, change the bits of the host portion (the last 6 bits) all to 1: 01111111. Then convert back to deci- mal: 0b01111111 = 0d127. Therefore, the broadcast address is 192.168.222.127.

questions.question: When multiple routes to the same destination network are learned via different routing protocols, which parameter is used to select which route(s) will enter the routing table?
questions.explanation: 4096 subnets A /12 address block has 20 host bits (32 − 12 = 20). To create subnets that support at least 200 usable host addresses, 8 host bits are needed (28 − 2 = 254). That leaves 12 bits that you can borrow to make subnets (20 − 8 = 12), allowing for 212 = 4096 subnets.
questions.source_ref: Acing the CCNA Vol1 — Ch9: Routing Fundamentals

In this case, only the route to 192.0.2.0/27 (option A) matches the packet’s destination IP address, so the router will forward the packet according to that route’s instructions.

In this case, all three statements match the appropriate bits in the interfac

questions.question: What command should you configure on a router to ensure that all GigabitEther- net interfaces have an OSPF cost of 10?
questions.explanation: auto-cost reference-bandwidth 10000 OSPF’s automatic cost calculation uses the formula “reference bandwidth/interface bandwidth”. The command is configured in auto-cost reference-bandwidth Mbps, so to make a 1 Gbps (1000 Mbps) interface have a cost of 10, the reference bandwidth should be 10000 Mbps (10000/1000 = 10).

questions.question: Four routers connected to a segment with the OSPF broadcast network type have the default OSPF priority. R4 is the DR and R3 is the BDR. You configure ip on R2’s interface connected to the segment and reset OSPF ospf priority 100 on R4 with . Which of the following statements is true clear ip ospf process after you reset R4’s OSPF process?
options: R2 is the DR and R3 is the BDR.
R3 is the DR and R2 is the BDR.
R2 is the DR and R4 is the BDR.
R3 is the DR and R4 is the BDR
questions.explanation: R3 is the DR and R2 is the BDR. When the OSPF process of R4 (the DR) is reset, R3 (the BDR) immediately takes over its role as the DR, even though R2’s interface now has the highest priority (100, whereas others have the default of 1). Then, an election is held to determine the new BDR; R2 wins this due to its higher priority and becomes the BDR.
questions.source_ref: Acing the CCNA Vol1 — Ch18: OSPF

questions.question: Which of the following commands can be used to prevent routers from electing a DR/BDR?
questions.explanation: Router(config-if)# ip ospf network point-to-point Whereas OSPF neighbors using the broadcast network type hold DR/BDR elections, those using the point-to-point network type do not. The default network type of Eth- ernet interfaces is broadcast, but you can use to ip ospf network point-to-point configure the point-to-point network type.

questions.question: Which of the following are requirements for two routers to become OSPF neigh- bors? (select two)
options: Hello and dead timers must match.
questions.explanation: RIDs must be unique. C: Hello and dead timers must match. For two routers to become OSPF neighbors, their RIDs must be unique, and their hello and dead timers must match. The other two options are incorrect because the area numbers must match (not be unique) and the RIDs must be unique (not match).

questions.question: Which of the following represents the order of priority when routers elect a DR/ BDR?
questions.explanation: 1) Highest interface priority, 2) Highest RID The router with the highest interface priority is elected as the DR of an OSPF broad- cast network. If there is a tie, the router with the highest RID becomes the DR. Then, an election using identical rules is held to determine the BDR. Chapter 19: First hop redundancy protocols
questions.source_ref: Acing the CCNA Vol1 — Ch18: OSPF

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the virtual IP and virtual MAC used by HSRP are true?
options: The active and standby routers share a VIP, but each uses a different virtual
The active and standby routers each use a different VIP, but share a virtual
The active and standby routers share a VIP and virtual MAC address.
The active and standby router each use different VIPs and virtual MAC
questions.explanation: The active and standby routers share a VIP and virtual MAC address. The Hot Standby Router Protocol (HSRP) active and standby routers share a virtual IP address (VIP) and virtual MAC address. This allows for a seamless failover from the active router to the standby router; end hosts don’t have to update their default gate- way IP address or ARP table when a failover occurs.
questions.source_ref: Acing the CCNA Vol1 — Ch18: OSPF

questions.question: During HSRP failover, what is the purpose of the new active router sending GARP messages?
options: To make end hosts update their ARP table.
To make end hosts update their default gateway IP address.
To make switches update their MAC address table.
To inform the former active router of the failover.
questions.explanation: To make switches update their MAC address table. As the previous explanation mentioned, end hosts don’t have to update their default gateway IP address or ARP table when a failover from active to standby occurs. Instead, the gratuitous ARP (GARP) messages sent by the new active router serve to make switches update their MAC address tables. This allows them to correctly forward frames to the new active router.
questions.source_ref: Acing the CCNA Vol1 — Ch18: OSPF

questions.question: Which virtual MAC address is used by VRRP group 12?
questions.explanation: 0000.5e00.010c The Virtual Router Redundancy Protocol (VRRP) virtual MAC uses the format 0000.5e00.01XX, where XX is the VRRP group number. However, because MAC addresses are written in hexadecimal, the virtual MAC format for VRRP group 12 is 0000.5e00.010c (0d12 = 0xc), not 0000.5e00.0112.
questions.source_ref: Acing the CCNA Vol1 — Ch18: OSPF

questions.question: R1 is a master router, actively forwarding packets between the LAN and external networks. R2 is a backup router, waiting to take over in case R1 fails. Which FHRP are R1 and R2 using?

questions.question: Which Cisco NAT term describes the IP address of a host in the internal network from the perspective of hosts in the external network?
questions.explanation: Tail drop Tail drop, which occurs when packets are dropped from the end of a full queue, can lead to TCP global synchronization, where multiple TCP flows reduce their window sizes simultaneously, leading to network underutilization. This can lead to a global TCP window size increase, leading to congestion and tail drop again— a vicious cycle. Random Early Detection (RED) and Weighted RED (WRED) are techniques to avoid tail drop, not causes of it.

questions.question: ACL 1 only permits packets sourced from the 10.0.0.0/24 subnet. After issuing the ip nat inside source list 1 gigabitethernet0/0 command on R1, what action will it take on packets denied by ACL 1?
options: It will forward them without translation.
It will buffer them until they can be translated.
It will translate their source IP to GigabitEthernet0/0’s IP.
questions.explanation: It will forward them without translation. In the context of NAT, an ACL is used to determine which packets are eligible for NAT; it isn’t used to decide which packets will be forwarded or discarded. Packets denied by the ACL are not translated, but will be forwarded as normal according to the router’s routing table. However, this will likely result in those untranslated packets being discarded by the ISP’s router due to their private addresses.
questions.source_ref: Acing the CCNA Vol2 — Ch9: NAT

questions.explanation: ip nat inside source list 1 pool POOL1 overload This command configures dynamic PAT using the specified access list and pool, with the overload keyword enabling multiple inside local addresses to share one inside global address.

questions.question: When PAT is configured, what happens when two pre-translation messages use the same source port number?

questions.explanation: The second packet’s port number is translated to a new number. In PAT, if two packets have the same source port, NAT will assign a unique port number for the second packet. The inside global IP address of each packet will be identical after translation, so a unique source port number is needed for identification. Chapter 10: Quality of service

questions.question: What does a router use to make forwarding decisions?
questions.explanation: The routing table (RIB) maps destination networks to outgoing interfaces or next-hop IPs. It contains: directly connected routes, static routes, and dynamically learned routes from OSPF, EIGRP, etc.
questions.source_ref: CCNA Field Ops Trainer v2.1; Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA 200-301

questions.question: Which concept determines which routing source a router trusts MORE when multiple protocols know a route?
questions.explanation: Administrative Distance (AD) ranks routing source trustworthiness. Connected=0, Static=1, EIGRP=90, OSPF=110, RIP=120. When two protocols know the same destination, lower AD wins. Only one route enters the routing table (the one with lowest AD).
questions.source_ref: CCNA Field Ops Trainer v2.1; Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA 200-301

questions.question: Where does R1 forward a packet to 10.1.1.50?
questions.explanation: Longest Prefix Match: all three routes match 10.1.1.50, but /24 is more specific than /16, which is more specific than /0. Router always picks longest match, regardless of AD or metric. This is fundamental to how all IP routing works.
questions.source_ref: CCNA Field Ops Trainer v2.1; Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA 200-301

questions.question: What is the purpose of a default gateway on a host?
options: Forward traffic to networks the host doesn't know about
questions.explanation: The default gateway is the local router's IP on the host's subnet. Any traffic destined outside the local subnet is forwarded to the default gateway. Without it, the host is isolated to its own subnet — it can't reach anything beyond.
questions.source_ref: CCNA Field Ops Trainer v2.1; Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA 200-301

questions.question: What type of routing protocol is OSPF, and what algorithm does it use?
questions.explanation: OSPF (Open Shortest Path First) is a Link State protocol. Routers flood LSAs (Link State Advertisements) to build a complete topology database, then run Dijkstra's Shortest Path First (SPF) algorithm to find best routes. EIGRP is hybrid/advanced distance vector using DUAL. BGP is path vector.
questions.source_ref: CCNA Field Ops Trainer v2.1; Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA 200-301

questions.question: Which route wins for destination 10.1.5.1?
questions.explanation: When prefix length is equal, Administrative Distance decides. Static=1, OSPF=110. Static route wins. To prefer OSPF, you'd need to adjust AD or remove the static route. This is why knowing AD values matters on the exam.
questions.source_ref: CCNA Field Ops Trainer v2.1; Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA 200-301

questions.question: What does the "ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 [next-hop]" command create?
options: A host route for one specific IP
A default route — matches any destination not in the routing table
A summary route for all RFC1918 space
A null route to blackhole traffic
questions.explanation: "ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0" creates the default route — the routing table's "catch-all." Any packet without a more specific match gets forwarded via this route. On a branch router, this typically points toward the ISP. Equivalent to "ip default-gateway" on L2 switches.
questions.source_ref: CCNA Field Ops Trainer v2.1; Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA 200-301

questions.question: How many bits is an IPv6 address, and how is it written?
options: 128 bits in hexadecimal groups separated by colons
128 bits in decimal groups separated by dots
64 bits in hex separated by dashes
questions.explanation: IPv6 = 128 bits written as 8 groups of 4 hex digits separated by colons. Example: 2001:0db8:85a3:0000:0000:8a2e:0370:7334. You can compress with :: (consecutive zero groups) and drop leading zeros: 2001:db8:85a3::8a2e:370:7334.
questions.source_ref: CCNA Field Ops Trainer v2.1; Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA 200-301

questions.question: What does :: mean in an IPv6 address?
options: One or more consecutive groups of all zeros (can only appear once)
The host portion of the address
questions.explanation: :: replaces one or more consecutive groups of 0000. 2001:db8::1 = 2001:0db8:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001. Rules: :: can only appear once per address. The loopback address is ::1 (127 zero groups + 1). The unspecified address is :: (all zeros).
questions.source_ref: CCNA Field Ops Trainer v2.1; Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA 200-301

questions.question: What is the IPv6 link-local address range and what is it used for?
options: FE80::/10 — automatically configured, used on local link only
questions.explanation: FE80::/10 addresses are link-local — auto-configured on every IPv6 interface, never forwarded beyond the local link. Used for: neighbor discovery, router discovery, routing protocol hellos (like OSPF). Generated using EUI-64 from the MAC address. Every IPv6 interface will have at least a link-local address.
questions.source_ref: CCNA Field Ops Trainer v2.1; Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA 200-301

questions.question: What replaced ARP in IPv6?
questions.explanation: NDP (Neighbor Discovery Protocol) replaces ARP in IPv6, using ICMPv6 messages. Neighbor Solicitation (NS) = ARP request. Neighbor Advertisement (NA) = ARP reply. NDP also handles Router Discovery (RS/RA), Duplicate Address Detection (DAD), and SLAAC address configuration. More secure than ARP (no broadcasts).
questions.source_ref: CCNA Field Ops Trainer v2.1; Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA 200-301

questions.question: What is SLAAC in IPv6?
options: A routing protocol for IPv6 networks
Stateless Address Autoconfiguration — hosts self-generate a global IPv6 address from the router's prefix
A replacement for NAT in IPv6
questions.explanation: SLAAC (Stateless Address Autoconfiguration) lets hosts auto-configure global IPv6 addresses without DHCP. Process: (1) Host gets /64 prefix from Router Advertisement. (2) Host generates 64-bit interface ID (from MAC via EUI-64 or random). (3) Combines prefix + interface ID = global unicast address. (4) Runs DAD to verify uniqueness.
questions.source_ref: CCNA Field Ops Trainer v2.1; Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA 200-301

questions.question: An organization receives a /48 prefix. How many /64 subnets can they create?
questions.explanation: From /48 to /64 = 64-48 = 16 bits for subnets. 2^16 = 65,536 possible /64 subnets. Each /64 has 2^64 ≈ 18.4 quintillion host addresses. IPv6 is designed so every subnet is a /64, giving enormous scale. No more fighting over host bits!
questions.source_ref: CCNA Field Ops Trainer v2.1; Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA 200-301

questions.question: What is EUI-64 and how does it generate an IPv6 interface ID?
options: A method to derive a 64-bit interface ID from a 48-bit MAC address by inserting FFFE and flipping the 7th bit
A routing protocol metric for IPv6
A compression algorithm for IPv6 headers
questions.explanation: EUI-64 converts a 48-bit MAC to a 64-bit interface ID: (1) Split MAC at 24-bit boundary. (2) Insert FF:FE in the middle. (3) Flip bit 7 (universal/local bit) of the first byte. Example: MAC 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E → 02:1A:2B:FF:FE:3C:4D:5E. Privacy concern: this embeds MAC in IP, enabling tracking — hence RFC4941 temporary addresses.
questions.source_ref: CCNA Field Ops Trainer v2.1; Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA 200-301

questions.question: What IPv6 address type replaced IPv4 broadcast?
questions.explanation: IPv6 eliminates broadcast entirely. Instead: FF02::1 = all-nodes multicast (like a broadcast to all IPv6 devices). FF02::2 = all-routers multicast. FF02::5 = OSPF routers. FF02::6 = OSPF DRs. Multicast is more efficient — only subscribed devices process it, reducing CPU load on non-interested hosts.
questions.source_ref: CCNA Field Ops Trainer v2.1; Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA 200-301

questions.question: What would Building 1's /64 subnet be using the 4th hextet for the subnet ID?
questions.explanation: From /48 prefix 2001:db8:cafe::/48, the 4th hextet is the subnet field (bits 49-64). Building 1 = 2001:db8:cafe:0001::/64 (or :1::/64 compressed). Building 2 = :2::/64, etc. Each building gets 2^64 host addresses — effectively unlimited. The interface ID (last 64 bits) is all zeros for the network address.
questions.source_ref: CCNA Field Ops Trainer v2.1; Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA 200-301

questions.question: How does HSRP provide first hop redundancy?
options: A. It load-balances traffic by assigning the same metric value to more than one route to the same destination in the IP routing table.
B. It load-balances Layer 2 traffic along the path by flooding traffic out all interfaces configured with the same VLAN.
C. It forwards multiple packets to the same destination over different routed links in the data path
D. It uses a shared virtual MAC and a virtual IP address to a group of routers that serve as the default gateway for hosts on a LAN
questions.explanation: This virtual IP address is in the same subnet as the interface IP address, but it is a different IP address. The router then automatically creates the virtual MAC address. All the cooperating HSRP routers know these virtual addresses, but only the HSRP active router uses these addresses at any one point in time. The virtual router is responsible for host communications such as an ARP request for the host’s default gateway. Technically, this is served by the active router since it is hosting the virtual router. However, it is the virtual router’s IP address and MAC address that are used for outgoing packets.

questions.question: Which attribute does a router use to select the best path when two or more different routes to the same destination exist from two different routing protocols?
questions.explanation: Administrative distance is the feature used by routers to select the best path when there are two or more different routes to the same destination from different routing protocols. Administrative distance defines the reliability of a routing protocol.

questions.question: An engineer configured an OSPF neighbor as a designated router. Which state verifies the designated router is in the proper mode?
questions.explanation: Full is the state for adjacent routers that have fully synchronised databases.

questions.question: A router running EIGRP has learned the same route from two different paths. Which parameter does the router use to select the best path?
questions.explanation: If a router learns two different paths for the same network from the same routing protocol, it has to decide which route is better and will be placed in the routing table. Metric is the measure used to decide which route is better (lower number is better). Each routing protocol uses its own metric. For example, RIP uses hop counts as a metric, while OSPF uses cost.

questions.question: When a floating static route is configured, which action ensures that the backup route is used when the primary route fails?
options: A. The floating static route must have a higher administrative distance than the primary route so it is used as a backup
B. The administrative distance must be higher on the primary route so that the backup route becomes secondary.
C. The floating static route must have a lower administrative distance than the primary route so it is used as a backup
D. The default-information originate command must be configured for the route to be installed into the routing table
questions.explanation: By default, IOS considers static routes better than OSPF-learned routes. By default, IOS gives static routes an administrative distance of 1 A floating static route floats or moves into and out of the IP routing table depending on whether the better (lower) administrative distance route learned by the routing protocol happens to exist currently.

questions.question: Router A learns the same route from two different neighbors, one of the neighbor routers is an OSPF neighbor and the other is an EIGRP neighbor. What is the administrative distance of the route that will be installed in the routing table?
questions.explanation: The Administrative distance (AD) of EIGRP is 90 while the AD of OSPF is 110 so EIGRP route will be chosen to install into the routing table.

questions.question: You have configured a router with an OSPF router ID, but its IP address still reflects the physical interface. Which action can you take to correct the problem in the least disruptive way?
questions.explanation: Once an OSPF Router ID selection is done, it remains there even if you remove it or configure another OSPF Router ID. So the least disruptive way is to correct it using the command “clear ip ospf process”.

questions.question: When OSPF learns multiple paths to a network, how does it select a route?
options: A. It multiple the active K value by 256 to calculate the route with the lowest metric.
B. For each existing interface, it adds the metric from the source router to the destination to calculate the route with the lowest bandwidth.
C. It divides a reference bandwidth of 100 Mbps by the actual bandwidth of the existing interface to calculate the router with the lowest cost.
D. It count the umber of hops between the source router and the destination to determine the router with the lowest metric
questions.explanation: See official Cisco CCNA documentation.

questions.question: A user configured OSPF and advertised the Gigabit Ethernet interface in OSPF By default, which type of OSPF network does this interface belong to?
options: D. nonbroadcast Get Latest & Actual 200-301 Exam's Question and Answers from Passleader. http://www.passleader.com 51
questions.explanation: See official Cisco CCNA documentation.

questions.question: Which command should you enter to view the error log in an EIGRP for IPv6 environment?
questions.explanation: show ip eigrp events

questions.question: Which command automatically generates an IPv6 address from a specified IPv6 prefix and MAC address of an interface?
questions.explanation: The "ipv6 address autoconfig" command causes the device to perform IPv6 stateless address auto-configuration to discover prefixes on the link and then to add the EUI-64 based addresses to the interface. Addresses are configured depending on the prefixes received in Router Advertisement (RA) messages. The device will listen for RA messages which are transmitted periodically from the router (DHCP Server). This RA message allows a host to create a global IPv6 address from: + Its interface identifier (EUI-64 address) + Link Prefix (obtained via RA) Note: Global address is the combination of Link Prefix and EUI-64 address

questions.question: R1 has learned route 192.168.12.0/24 via IS-IS, OSPF, RIP and Internal EIGRP Under normal operating conditions, which routing protocol is installed in the routing table?
questions.explanation: With the same route (prefix), the router will choose the routing protocol with lowest Administrative Distance (AD) to install into the routing table. The AD of Internal EIGRP (90) is lowest so it would 62 be chosen. The table below lists the ADs of popular routing protocols. Note: The AD of IS-IS is 115. The "EIGRP" in the table above is "Internal EIGRP". The AD of "External EIGRP" is 170. An EIGRP external route is a route that was redistributed into EIGRP.

questions.question: Which IPv6 address block sends packets to a group address rather than a single address?
questions.explanation: FF00::/8 is used for IPv6 multicast and this is the IPv6 type of address the question wants to ask. FE80::/10 range is used for link-local addresses. Link-local addresses only used for communications within the local subnetwork (automatic address configuration, neighbor discovery, router discovery, and by many routing protocols). It is only valid on the current subnet. It is usually created dynamically using a link-local prefix of FE80::/10 and a 64-bit interface identifier (based on 48-bit MAC address).

questions.question: Which IPv6 address type provides communication between subnets and cannot route on the Internet?
questions.explanation: A IPv6 Unique Local Address is an IPv6 address in the block FC00::/7. It is the approximate IPv6 counterpart of the IPv4 private address. It is not routable on the global Internet. Note: In the past, Site-local addresses (FEC0::/10) are equivalent to private IP addresses in IPv4 but now they are deprecated. Link-local addresses only used for communications within the local subnet. It is usually created dynamically using a link-local prefix of FE80::/10 and a 64-bit interface identifier (based on 48-bit MAC address).

questions.question: A user configured OSPF in a single area between two routers A serial interface connecting R1 and R2 is running encapsulation PPP. By default which OSPF network type is seen on this interface when the user types show ip ospf interface on R1 or R2?
questions.explanation: The default OSPF network type for HDLC and PPP on Serial link is point-to-point (while the default OSPF network type for Ethernet link is Broadcast).

questions.question: An engineer must configure a /30 subnet between two routers. Which usable IP address and subnet mask combination meets this criteria?
options: A. interface e0/0 description to HQ-A371:10975 ip address 172.16.1.4 255.255.255.248
B. interface e0/0 description to HQ-A371:10975 ip address 10.2.1.3 255.255.255.252
C. interface e0/0 description to HQ-A371:10975 ip address 192.168.1.1 255.255.255.248
D. interface e0/0 description to HQ-A371:10975 ip address 209.165.201.2 255.255.255.252
questions.explanation: A /30 subnet means subnet mask of 255.255.255.252. But 10.2.1.3 255.255.255.252 is a broadcast IP address; only 209.165.201.2/30 is the usable IP address.

questions.question: What is the binary pattern of unique ipv6 unique local address?
options: C. 11111111 Get Latest & Actual 200-301 Exam's Question and Answers from Passleader. http://www.passleader.com 78
questions.explanation: A IPv6 Unique Local Address is an IPv6 address in the block FC00::/7, which means that IPv6 Unique Local addresses begin with 7 bits with exact binary pattern as 1111 110 -> Answer B is correct. Note: IPv6 Unique Local Address is the approximate IPv6 counterpart of the IPv4 private address. It is not routable on the global Internet.

questions.question: Which statement about VLAN configuration is true?
options: A. The switch must be in VTP server or transparent mode before you can configure a VLAN
B. The switch must be in config-vlan mode before you configure an extended VLAN
C. Dynamic inter-VLAN routing is supported on VLAN2 through VLAN 4064
D. A switch in VTP transparent mode save the VLAN databases to the running configuration only
questions.explanation: You can only create, add, delete edit vlan in server and transparent mode, you won’t be able to create, delete vlan in client mode.

questions.question: Which technique can you use to route IPv6 traffic over an IPv4 infrastructure?
questions.explanation: Overlay tunneling encapsulates IPv6 packets in IPv4 packets for delivery across an IPv4 infrastructure (a core network or the figure below). By using overlay tunnels, you can communicate with isolated IPv6 networks without upgrading the IPv4 infrastructure between 86 them. Overlay tunnels can be configured between border devices or between a border device and a host; however, both tunnel endpoints must support both the IPv4 and IPv6 protocol stacks. IPv6 supports the following types of overlay tunneling mechanisms: 1 Manual 2 Generic routing encapsulation (GRE) 3 IPv4-compatible 4 6to4 5 Intrasite Automatic Tunnel Addressing Protocol (ISATAP)

questions.question: A network administrator is troubleshooting the OSPF configuration of routers R1 and R2. The routers cannot establish an adjacency relationship on their common Ethernet link. Get Latest & Actual 200-301 Exam's Question and Answers from Passleader. http://www.passleader.com 93 The graphic shows the output of the show ip ospf interface e0 command for routers R1 and R2. Based on the information in the graphic, what is the cause of this problem?
options: A. The OSPF area is not configured properly.
B. The priority on R1 should be set higher.
C. The cost on R1 should be set higher.
D. The hello and dead timers are not configured properly.
E. A backup designated router needs to be added to the network. F. The OSPF process ID numbers must match.
questions.explanation: In OSPF, the hello and dead intervals must match and here we can see the hello interval is set to 5 on R1 and 10 on R2. The dead interval is also set to 20 on R1 but it is 40 on R2.

questions.question: If all OSPF routers in a single area are configured with the same priority value, what value does a router use for the OSPF router ID in the absence of a loopback interface?
options: A. the IP address of the first Fast Ethernet interface
B. the IP address of the console management interface
C. the highest IP address among its active interfaces
D. the lowest IP address among its active interfaces
E. the priority value until a loopback interface is configured
questions.explanation: A router ID is determined in the following order: 1. using the router-id command under the OSPF process to statically configure the router ID. 2. using the highest IP address of the router’s loopback interfaces. 3. using the highest IP address of the router’s active physical interfaces.

questions.question: Which IPv6 address block forwards packets to a multicast address rather than a unicast address?
questions.explanation: Multicast: FF00/8 -- FF00:: - FFFF:: Global Unicast: 2000::/3, 2001::/3, 2002::/4, 2001:db8::/32 98 Link Local Unicast: FE80::/10 -- FE80:: - FEBF:: Unique Local Unicast: FC00::/7 -- FC00:: - FDFF:: Loopback: ::1/128

questions.question: Which action must be taken to assign a global unicast IPv6 address on an interface that is derived from the MAC address of that interface?
options: A. explicitly assign a link-local address
B. disable the EUI-64 bit process
C. enable SLAAC on an interface
D. configure a stateful DHCPv6 server on the network
questions.explanation: SLAAC stands for Stateless Address Autoconfiguration and the name pretty much explains what it does. It is a mechanism that enables each host on the network to auto-configure a unique IPv6 address without any device keeping track of which address is assigned to which node.

questions.question: Which type of ipv6 address is publicly routable in the same way as ipv4 public addresses?
questions.explanation: Global unicast addresses (GUAs), also known as aggregatable global unicast addresses, are globally routable and reachable in the IPv6 Internet. They are equivalent to public IPv4 addresses. They play a significant role in the IPv6 addressing architecture.

questions.question: By default, how does EIGRP determine the metric of a route for the routing table?
options: A. It uses the bandwidth and delay values of the path to calculate the route metric.
B. It uses a default metric of 10 for all routes that are learned by the router.
C. It counts the number of hops between the receiving and destination routers and uses that value as the metric.
D. It uses a reference bandwidth and the actual bandwidth of the connected link to calculate the route metric.
questions.explanation: EIGRP uses the minimum bandwidth on the path to a destination network and the total delay to compute routing metrics.

questions.question: A packet is destined for 10.10.1.22. Which static route does the router choose to forward the packet?
R1 has learned route 10.10.10.0/24 via numerous routing protocols. Which route is installed?
options: A. route with the next hop that has the highest IP
B. route with the lowest cost
C. route with the lowest administrative distance
D. route with the shortest prefix length
questions.explanation: Route Preference: 1. Longest Prefix 2. Administrative Distance 3. Metric In this specific question, the first option is: Administrative Distance.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a First Hop Redundancy Protocol?
options: A. It allows directly connected neighbors to share configuration information.
B. It allows a router to use bridge priorities to create multiple loop-free paths to a single destination.
C. It reduces routing failures by allowing Layer 3 load balancing between OSPF neighbors that have the same link metric.

D. It reduces routing failures by allowing more than one router to represent itself, as the default Get Latest & Actual 200-301 Exam's Question and Answers from Passleader. http://www.passleader.com 127 gateway of a network.
questions.explanation: A first hop redundancy protocol (FHRP) is a computer networking protocol which is designed to protect the default gateway used on a subnetwork by allowing two or more routers to provide backup for that address; in the event of failure of an active router, the backup router will take over.

questions.explanation: See official Cisco CCNA documentation.

questions.question: What is a benefit of VRRP?
options: A. It provides traffic load balancing to destinations that are more than two hops from the source.
B. It provides the default gateway redundancy on a LAN using two or more routers. Get Latest & Actual 200-301 Exam's Question and Answers from Passleader. http://www.passleader.com 171
C. It allows neighbors to share routing table information between each other.
D. It prevents loops in a Layer 2 LAN by forwarding all traffic to a root bridge, which then makes the final forwarding decision.
questions.explanation: See official Cisco CCNA documentation.

questions.question: What is the effect when loopback interfaces and the configured router ID are absent during the OSPF Process configuration?
options: A. No router ID is set, and the OSPF protocol does not run.
B. The highest up/up physical interface IP address is selected as the router ID.
C. The lowest IP address is incremented by 1 and selected as the router ID.
D. The router ID 0.0.0.0 is selected and placed in the OSPF process.
questions.explanation: See official Cisco CCNA documentation.

questions.question: Which network action occurs within the data plane?
options: A. compare the destination IP address to the IP routing table.
B. run routing protocols (OSPF, EIGRP, RIP, BGP)
C. make a configuration change from an incoming NETCONF RPC
D. reply to an incoming ICMP echo request
questions.explanation: The role of ICMP is to provide information about the path the data is taking from its point of origin to its destination. It has the same basic structure as an IP packet, but despite that, it's not really goodput. It's there to control 'how things are done', therefore, is part of the control plane.

questions.question: Which networking function occurs on the data plane? Get Latest & Actual 200-301 Exam's Question and Answers from Passleader. http://www.passleader.com 201
options: C. processing inbound SSH management traffic
D. sending and receiving OSPF Hello packets
questions.explanation: Networking devices operate in two planes; the data plane and the control plane. The control plane maintains Layer 2 and Layer 3 forwarding mechanisms using the CPU. The data plane forwards traffic flows

questions.question: Get Latest & Actual 200-301 Exam's Question and Answers from Passleader. http://www.passleader.com 215 What is the purpose of using First Hop Redundancy Protocol in a specific subnet?
options: A. Filter traffic based on destination IP addressing
B. Sends the default route to the hosts on a network
C. ensures a loop-free physical topology
D. forwards multicast hello messages between routers
questions.explanation: FHRP is layer 3 protocol whose purpose is to protect the default gateway by offering redundancy of the gateway in a subnet. This is achieved by allowing two or more routers to provide a backup for the first-hop IP router address. If a failure of an active router occurs, the backup router will take over the address. The routers negotiate their roles (Active/Standby) with each other by multicast hello messages to share the VIP (virtual IP address) between the FHRP routers. The terms Active/Standby vary between the different types of FHRP. The active router will act as the default gateway and the standby router acts as a backup the active router.

questions.question: Why was the RFC 1918 address space defined?
options: B. preserve public IPv6 address space
C. reduce instances of overlapping IP addresses
questions.explanation: 222 An RFC1918 address is an IP address that is assigned by an enterprise organization to an internal host. These IP addresses are used in private networks, which are not available, or reachable, from the Internet.

B. IPv6 unicast nodes must be explicitly configured to recognize the unicast address, but IPv6 anycast nodes require no special configuration
C. An individual IPv6 unicast address is supported on a single interface on one node but an IPv6 anycast address is assigned to a group of interfaces on multiple nodes.

questions.question: A Cisco engineer is configuring a factory-default router with these three passwords: - The user EXEC password for console access is p4ssw0rd1 - The user EXEC password for Telnet access is s3cr3t2 - The password for privileged EXEC mode is pnv4t3p4ss Which command sequence must the engineer configured?
options: A. enable secret priv4t3p4ss ! line con 0 password p4ssw0rd1 ! line vty 0 15 password s3cr3t2
B. enable secret priv4t3p4ss ! line con 0 password p4ssw0rd1 login ! line vty 0 15 password s3cr3t2 login

C. enable secret priv4t3p4ss ! Get Latest & Actual 200-301 Exam's Question and Answers from Passleader. http://www.passleader.com 260 line con 0 password login p4ssw0rd1 ! line vty 0 15 password login s3cr3t2 login
D. enable secret privilege 15 priv4t3p4ss ! line con 0 password p4ssw0rd1 login ! line vty 0 15 password s3cr3t2 login

The "login" keyword is used to enable password authentication for the console and Telnet access.

questions.question: Which type of IPv6 address is similar to a unicast address but is assigned to multiple devices on the same network at the same time?
questions.explanation: An IPv6 anycast address is any IPv6 unicast address that can be assigned to multiple devices.

questions.question: What is a function spine-and-leaf architecture?

B. Exclusively sends multicast traffic between servers that are directly connected to the spine.
C. Mitigates oversubscription by adding a layer of leaf switches.
D. Limits payload size of traffic within the leaf layer.
questions.explanation: With a spine-and-leaf architecture, no matter which leaf switch to which a server is connected, its traffic always has to cross the same number of devices to get to another server (unless the other server is located on the same leaf). This approach keeps latency at a predictable level because a payload only has to hop to a spine switch and another leaf switch to reach its destination. Reference: 424 paper-c11-737022.html

questions.explanation: The control plane is the part of a network that controls how data packets are forwarded — meaning how data is sent from one place to another. questions.explanation: Load balancing is a standard functionality of Cisco IOS Software that is available across all router platforms. questions.question: The routing protocol that was designed and in-tended for use between different autonomous systems is called ____________________________.

Exterior Gateway Protocol (EGP) is a protocol for ex-changing routing information between two neighbor gateway hosts (each with its own router) in a network of autonomous systems. EGP is commonly used between hosts on the Internet to ex-change routing table information. The routing table contains a list of known routers, the addresses they can reach, and a cost metric associated with the path to each router so that the best available route is chosen.

questions.question: Given the following OSPF network diagram, what router will be added to R1’s routing table as a next-hop?
questions.explanation: R4 is the correct answer. The SPF algo-rithm calculates all the routes for a subnet—that is, all possible routes from the router (R1) to the destination subnet (R8). If more than one route exists, the router compares the metrics, picking the best (lowest) metric route to add to the routing table. Once SPF has identified a route, OSPF calculates the metric based on the sum of the OSPF interface costs for all outgoing interfaces in the route. The three possible routes to 175.25.30.0/24 are: Route 1: R1-R2-R3-R8 Cost: 10 + 80 + 40 = 130 Route 2: R1-R4-R5-R6-R8 Cost: 20 + 30 + 10 + 20 = 80 Route 3: R1-R7-R8 Cost: 60 + 100 = 160 As a result of the SPF algorithm’s analysis of the LSDB, R1 adds a route to subnet 175.25.30.0/24 to its routing table, with the next-hop router of R4.

questions.question: A network engineer connects routers R5 and R6 to the same Ethernet LAN and configures them to use OSPFv2. Which answers describe a combination of settings that would prevent the two routers from becoming OSPF neighbors?
options: (A) Both routers’ interface IP addresses are in the same subnet
(B) Both routers’ OSPF process uses process ID 3
(C) Both routers’ OSPF process uses router ID 42.42.42.42
(D) Both routers’ interfaces use an OSPF Dead interval of 80
questions.explanation: Both routers’ OSPF process uses router ID 42.42.42.42 is the correct answer. The use of an identical OSPF router ID (RID) on the two routers prevents them from be-coming neighbors. Both routers must have the same Dead interval, so both using a Dead interval of 80 causes no issues. The two routers can use any OSPF process ID either the same or a different value, making that answer incorrect. Finally, the two routers’ IP addresses must be in the same sub-net, so again that scenario does not prevent R5 and R6 from becoming neighbors. 175

questions.question: Which of the following terms described as an OSPF router with interfaces connected to the backbone area and to at least one other area?
questions.explanation: Area Border Router is the correct answer. You should be familiar with the following terms for the CCNA 200-301 Area Border Router (ABR) – An OSPF router with interfaces connected to the backbone area and to at least one other area. Backbone router – A router connected to the backbone area (includes ABRs). Internal router – A router in one area (not the backbone area) Area – A set of routers and links that shares the same detailed LSDB information, but not with routers in other areas, for better efficiency. Backbone area – A special OSPF area to which all other areas must connect—area 0. Intra-area route – A route to a subnet inside the same area as the router. Interarea route – A route to a subnet in an area of which the router is not a part.

questions.question: Which of the following route types has 110 as default Administrative Distance (AD)?
questions.explanation: OSPF is the correct answer. Administrative distance is the feature that routers use in order to select the best path when there are at least two different routes to the same destination from two different routing proto-cols. Administrative distance defines the reliability of a routing proto-col. Each routing protocol is prioritized in order of most to least reliable (believable) with the help of an administrative distance value. The default Administrative Distances (AD) from the given route types are: Route type Connected Static BGP (external routes [eBGP]) EIGRP (internal routes) IGRP Administrative OSPF 110 IS-IS 115 RIP 120 EIGRP (external routes) 170 BGP (internal routes [iBGP]) 200 DHCP default route 254 Unusable 250 CHAPTER 6 IP VERSION 6 Questions 121-136 Question 121. questions.question: Which of the following addresses is the unab-breviated version of IPv6 address 8002:AA3::100:30?
questions.explanation: 8002:0AA3:0000:0000:0000:0000:0100:0030 is the correct answer. The unabbreviated version of an IPv6 ad-dress must have 32 digits, and only one answer has 32 hex digits. In this case, the original number shows four quartets (sets of four hex digits) and a ::. So, the :: was replaced with four quartets of 0000, making the number have eight quartets. Then, for each quartet with fewer than four digits, leading 0s were added so that each quartet has four hex digits.

questions.question: Given the following IPv6 address 2340:0000:0010:0100:1000:ABCD:0101:1010. Which of the following is the the abbreviated form?
questions.explanation: 2340:0:10:100:1:ABCD:101:1010 is the correct answer. To abbreviate IPv6 addresses, only leading 0s in a quartet (one set of four hex digits) should be removed. Many of the quartets have trailing 0s (0s on the right side of the quartet), so make sure to not remove those 0s. The unabbreviated version of an IPv6 address must have 32 digits. The :: was replaced with four quartets of 0000, making the number have eight sets of hex digits total (32 digits). Also, for each quartet with fewer than four digits, leading 0s were added so that each quartet has four hex digits.

questions.question: Given the following IPv6 address 1230::. Which of the following is the the unabbreviated form?
questions.explanation: 1230:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000 is the correct answer. The unabbreviated version of an IPv6 ad-dress must have 32 digits. The :: was replaced with four quar-tets of 0000, making the number have eight sets of hex digits total (32 digits). Also, for each quartet with fewer than four dig-its, leading 0s were added so that each quartet has four hex digits. To abbreviate IPv6 addresses, only leading 0s in a quartet (one set of four hex digits) should be removed. Many of the quartets have trailing 0s (0s on the right side of the quartet), so make sure to not remove those 0s.

questions.question: End-user hosts need to know the IPv6 address of a default router, to which the host sends IPv6 packets if the host is in a different subnet.
questions.explanation: TRUE is the correct answer. IPv6 routing looks just like IPv4 routing. IPv6 uses these ideas the same way as IPv4: 1. To be able to build and send IPv6 packets out an interface, end-user devices need an IPv6 address on that interface. 2. End-user hosts need to know the IPv6 address of a default router, to which the host sends IPv6 packets if the host is in a different subnet. 3. IPv6 routers de-encapsulate and re-encapsulate each IPv6 packet when routing the packet. 4. IPv6 routers make routing decisions by comparing the IPv6 packet’s destination address to the router’s IPv6 routing table; the matched route lists directions of where to send the IPv6 packet next.

questions.question: One of the following multicast addresses is de-fined as the address for sending packets to only the IPv6 routers on the local link.
questions.explanation: FF02::2 is the correct answer. FF02::2 is used to send packets to all IPv6 routers on a link. 1. FF02::1 is used by all IPv6 hosts on the link 2. FF02::5 is used by all OSPFv3 routers 3. FF02::A is used by all EIGRPv6 routers

questions.question: PC1, PC2, and Router R1 all connect to the same VLAN and IPv6 subnet. PC1 wants to send its first IPv6 packet to PC2. What protocol or message will PC1 use to dis-cover the MAC address to which PC1 should send the Ethernet frame that encapsulates this IPv6 packet?
questions.explanation: NDP NS is the correct answer. PC1 needs to discover PC2’s MAC address. Unlike IPv4, IPv6 does not use ARP, instead using NDP. Specifically, PC1 uses the NDP Neigh-bor Solicitation (NS) message to request that PC2 send back an NDP Neighbor Advertisement (NA) NS — Neighbor Solicitation is a message that sent by a node to determine the link-layer address of a neighbor, or to verify that a neighbor is still reachable via a cached link-layer ad-dress. NSes are also used for Duplicate Address Detection (DAD). NA — Neighbor Advertisement is a message that sent in re-sponse to an NS message. A node may also send unsolicited NAs to announce a link-layer address change.

questions.question: Router R2 has an interface named Gigabit Eth-ernet 0/2, whose MAC address has been set to 5555.4444.3333. This interface has been configured with the ipv6 address 7000:1:1:1::/64 eui-64 subcommand. What unicast address will this interface use?
questions.explanation: 7000:1:1:1:5755:44FF:FE44:3333 is the correct answer. With the eui-64 parameter, the router will cal-culate the interface ID portion of the IPv6 address based on its MAC address. Beginning with 5555.4444.3333, the router injects FF FE in the middle (5555.44FF.FE44.3333). Then the router inverts the seventh bit in the first byte. Mentally, this converts hex 55 to binary 0101 0101, changing the seventh bit so the string is 0101 0111 and converting back to hex 52. The final interface ID value is 5755:44FF:FE44:3333

questions.question: The show ipv6 interface brief command gives you interface IPv6 address info, and prefix length info.
questions.explanation: FALSE is the correct answer. The show ipv6 interface brief command gives you interface IPv6 ad-dress info, but not prefix length info, similar to the IPv4 show ip interface brief command. The show ipv6 interface command gives the details of IPv6 interface settings, much like the show ip interface command does for IPv4.

questions.question: You are responsible to configure a static route on R1’s outgoing interface, in order to support traffic between the subnets. R2 is already configured. Which of the following commands will you type to configure a static route on R1’s out-going interface?
questions.explanation: ipv6 route 2000:1:1:1::/64 s0/1/1 is the correct answer. IPv4 and IPv6 static routes, when the com-mand references an interface, the interface is a local interface. To support traffic between the subnets requires both routers to have a static route. R1 is now configured. Host A from the sub-net 2000:1:1:1::/64 will forward all its IPv6 packets to its default router (R1), and R1 can now route those packets out S0/1/1 to R2 next. The question declared that the R2 is already configured so, now both subnets can communicate. If the R2 wasn’t config-198 ured then you should have added the following command on R2. R2(config)# ipv6 route 2000:2:2:2::/64 S0/1/0 Static routes instead of using the local interfaces, they can use the IP address of the next router as a next-hop. Below we provide an example of how the command looks like on both routers if you were used the IP of the next router to create static route. R1(config)# ipv6 route 2000:1:1:1::/64 2000:1:2:3::2 R2(config)# ipv6 route 2000:2:2:2::/64 2000:1:2:3::1 CHAPTER 7 SECURITY FUNDAMENTALS Questions 137-153 Question 137. (A) TRUE (B) FALSE Question 146. (A) Social engineering (B) Phishing (C) Whaling (D) Pharming Question 148.You are responsible to enable DHCP snooping on the SW1. The R1 is a DHCP relay agent that needs to be trusted. questions.question: R1 and R2 are attached to the same Ethernet VLAN, with subnet 192.168.1.0/24, and addresses 192.168.1.1, 192.168.2 respectively. The routers use an FHRP. Host A and host B attach to the same LAN and have correct default router settings per the FHRP configuration. Which of the following statements is true for this LAN?
options: (A) You can't connect two routers to the same LAN subnet.
(B) If one router fails, hosts can't send packets off-subnet
(C) If one router fails, both hosts will use the one re-maining router as a default router
(D) if one router fails, only one of the two hosts will still be able to send packets off-subnet
questions.explanation: If one router fails, both hosts will use the one remaining router as a default router is the correct answer. The use of an FHRP in this design pur-posefully allows either router to fail and still support off-subnet traffic from all hosts in the subnet. Both routers can attach to the same LAN subnet per IPv4 addressing rules. FHRPs make this design work better. The two routers appear to be a single default router. The users never have to do any-thing: their default router setting remains the same, and their ARP table even remains the same. To allow the hosts to remain unchanged, the routers have to do some more work, as defined by one of the FHRP protocols. Generically, each FHRP makes the following happen: 1. All hosts act like they always have, with one default router setting that never has to change 2. The default routers share a virtual IP address in the subnet, defined by the FHRP 3. Hosts use the FHRP virtual IP address as their default router address 4. The routers exchange FHRP protocol messages so that both agree as to which router does what works at any point in time 5. When a router fails or has some other problem, the routers use the FHRP to choose which router takes over responsibilities from the failed router The three FHRP protocols are: 1) Hot Standby Router Protocol (HSRP) 2) Virtual Router Redundancy Protocol (VRRP) 305 3) Gateway Load Balancing Protocol (GLBP)

questions.question: What bit pattern is found at the beginning of an IPv4 packet?
questions.explanation: 0100 The first field of the IPv4 header is the four-bit Version field, which indicates the IP ver-sion of the packet. IP version 4 is indicated with 0b0100 (which is equal to 0d4).
questions.source_ref: Acing the CCNA Exam Vol1 (Jeremy McDowell)

questions.question: What is indicated by the Total Length field of the IPv4 header?
options: The length of the IPv4 header
The length of the Ethernet header and trailer
questions.explanation: The length of the Packet The Total Length field in the IPv4 header specifies the entire packet’s length, includ-ing both the header and the encapsulated payload, in bytes.
questions.source_ref: Acing the CCNA Exam Vol1 (Jeremy McDowell)

questions.question: Which field of the IPv4 header is used to prevent packets infinitely looping around the network?
questions.explanation: TTL The Time To Live (TTL) field in the IPv4 header is used to prevent packets from looping infinitely around the network. The TTL value is decremented by one by each router that forwards the packet. If the TTL reaches zero, the packet is discarded.
questions.source_ref: Acing the CCNA Exam Vol1 (Jeremy McDowell)

questions.question: Which netmask is equivalent to a /16 prefix length?
questions.explanation: 255.255.0.0 A /16 prefix length is indicated with a netmask consisting of 16 1s followed by 16 0s: 11111111.11111111.00000000.00000000, which is 255.255.0.0 in dotted decimal.
questions.source_ref: Acing the CCNA Exam Vol1 (Jeremy McDowell)

questions.question: Which type of route represents the exact IP address of a router’s interface with a /32 prefix length?
questions.explanation: L A local (L) route in the routing table represents the exact IP address of a router’s inter-face with a /32 prefix length. A local route is automatically created by the router for each of its active interfaces.
questions.source_ref: Acing the CCNA Exam Vol1 (Jeremy McDowell)

questions.question: Given a /25 address block, what prefix length is appropriate to create 16 subnets of equal size?
questions.explanation: /29 To create 16 subnets, you need to borrow 4 bits from the host portion (since 24 = 16). This results in a /29 prefix length, adding 4 to the original /25 prefix length.
questions.source_ref: Acing the CCNA Exam Vol1 (Jeremy McDowell)

questions.question: Given a /15 address block, what prefix length is appropriate to create 1024 sub-nets of equal size?
questions.explanation: /25 Making 1024 subnets requires 10 borrowed bits (since 210 = 1024). Therefore, the answer is /25, adding 10 to the original /15 prefix length.
questions.source_ref: Acing the CCNA Exam Vol1 (Jeremy McDowell)

questions.question: If a Cisco router learns multiple routes to the same destination via the same rout-ing protocol, and all routes have the same metric, what is the maximum number of routes to that destination that the router will insert into its routing table by default?
questions.explanation: 4 By default, Cisco routers can load-balance across up to four equal-cost paths; this is called equal-cost multi-path (ECMP) routing.
