If multiple routes to the same destina-tion have the same metric, up to four of them can be inserted into the routing table for load balancing.
questions.source_ref: Acing the CCNA Exam Vol1 (Jeremy McDowell)

questions.question: A router receives a packet destined for 10.23.107.126. It has the following four routes in its routing table. Which route will it select to forward the packet?
questions.explanation: D 10.23.107.112/28 [90/3456] via 172.16.1.13 In this case, three routes match the packet’s destination IP address: the route to 10.23.107.0/25 (A), the route to 10.23.107.96/27 (C), and the route to 10.23.107.112/28 (D). The route to 10.23.107.112/28 has the longest prefix length, so it is the most specific matching route; the router will forward the packet according to that route’s instructions.
questions.source_ref: Acing the CCNA Exam Vol1 (Jeremy McDowell)

questions.question: Which IPv6 address is equivalent to an IPv4 address in the 127.0.0.0/8 range?
questions.explanation: ::1 This is the IPv6 loopback address. Whereas IPv4 reserves a whole range (127.0.0.0/8) for loopback addresses, IPv6 uses only ::1. Messages sent to this address are “looped back” to the local device; they are not sent out of any interface.
questions.source_ref: Acing the CCNA Exam Vol1 (Jeremy McDowell)

questions.question: Which of the following commands configures a fully specified default route?
questions.explanation: ipv6 route ::/0 GigabitEthernet0/1 2001:db8::1 A default route is a route to ::/0, matching all possible IPv6 addresses. A fully specified route includes both the exit interface and the next-hop address, so option C is correct.
questions.source_ref: Acing the CCNA Exam Vol1 (Jeremy McDowell)

questions.question: An engineer connects Routers R13 and R14 to the same Ethernet LAN and configures them to use OSPFv2. Which answers describe a combination of settings that would prevent the two routers from becoming OSPF neighbors?
options: Both routers' interface IP addresses reside in the same 	subnet.
Both routers' OSPF process uses process ID 13.
Both routers' OSPF process uses router ID 13.13.13.13.
Both routers' interfaces use an OSPF Dead interval of 40.
questions.source_ref: CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide Vol 1 (Wendell Odom)

questions.question: Router R15 has been a working part of a network that uses OSPFv2. An engineer then issues the shutdown command in OSPF configuration mode on R15. Which of the following occurs?
options: R15 empties its IP routing table of all OSPF routes but keeps 	its LSDB intact.
R15 empties its OSPF routes and LSDB but keeps OSPF 	neighbor relationships active.
R15 keeps OSPF neighbors open but does not accept new 	OSPF neighbors.
R15 keeps all OSPF configuration but ceases all OSPF 	activities (routes, LSDB, neighbors).
questions.source_ref: CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide Vol 1 (Wendell Odom)

questions.question:
Using OSPF, Router R1 learns three routes to subnet 
10.1.1.0/24. It first calculates a route through Router R2 with metric 15000, then a route through Router R3 with metric 15001, and then a route through Router R4 with metric 15000. Which routes does the router place into its routing table, 
assuming all default configuration settings for any features that would impact the answer?
options: Only the route with R2 as the next-hop router
Only the router with R3 as the next-hop router
questions.source_ref: CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide Vol 1 (Wendell Odom)

questions.question:
Router R2 runs both EIGRP and OSPF. It learns two OSPF routes to subnet 172.16.1.0/24, one with metric 1000 and one with metric 2000. It learns two EIGRP routes with metrics 
1,000,000 and 2,000,000. If using default settings for any settings that might impact the answer, which route(s) will the router place into the IP routing table?
options: The metric 1000 OSPF route and the metric 1,0000,000 EIGRP 	route
The metric 2000 OSPF route and the metric 2,0000,000 EIGRP 	route
questions.source_ref: CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide Vol 1 (Wendell Odom)

questions.question: Router R3 receives a packet with destination IP address 172.20.89.100. How many of the address ranges defined in the routes per the show ip route command match the packet's destination address?
questions.source_ref: CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide Vol 1 (Wendell Odom)

questions.question: Router R3 receives a packet with destination IP address 172.20.90.1. Which next-hop IP address does Router R3 use when forwarding the packet?
options:
R3# show ip route 
Gateway of last resort is 172.20.15.5 to network
questions.source_ref: CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide Vol 1 (Wendell Odom)

questions.question:
An engineer needs to add a static IPv6 route for prefix 
2000:1:2:3::/64 to Router R5 in the figure. Which of the following answers shows a valid static IPv6 route for that subnet on Router R5?
questions.source_ref: CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide Vol 1 (Wendell Odom)

questions.question: When displaying an IPv6 static default route, how does the show ipv6 route command represent the concept of the default destination?
options: With the phrase "Gateway of Last Resort" just above the list 	of routes
With a prefix value ::/0 in the line for the route
With a prefix value ::/128 in the line for the route
With a prefix value 2000::/3 in the line for the route
With the keyword "default" in the line for the route
questions.source_ref: CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide Vol 1 (Wendell Odom)

questions.question:
Router R1 has a useful IPv6 configuration so that Router R1 learns a route for subnet 2001:db8:1:2::/64 with OSPF. The route lists an outgoing interface that is an Ethernet WAN link. The engineer then installs cellular interfaces on both routers for WAN backup. As part of the configuration, the engineer 
configures the ipv6 route 2001:db8:1:2::/64 cellular0/2/0 200 command. What does the router do in response?
options: It rejects the ipv6 route command because of the existing 	OSPF route for the same prefix.
It accepts the ipv6 route command into the configuration but 	does not add a route to the routing table.
It accepts the ipv6 route command into the configuration and adds the route to the routing table, but it leaves the OSPF-learned route in the routing table.
It accepts the ipv6 route command into the configuration, adds the route to the routing table, and removes the OSPF-learned route in the routing table.
questions.source_ref: CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide Vol 1 (Wendell Odom)

questions.question:
An engineer types the command ipv6 route 
2001:DB8:8:8::/64 2001:DB8:9:9::9 129 in configuration mode of Router R1 and presses Enter. Later, a show ipv6 route 
command lists no routes for subnet 2001:DB8:8:8::/64. Which of the following could have caused the route to not be in the IPv6 routing table?
options: The command uses a next-hop global unicast address, which 	is not allowed, preventing the router from adding a route.
The command must include an outgoing interface parameter, 	so IOS rejected the ipv6 route command.
The router has no routes that match the next-hop address 	2001:DB8:9:9::9.
A route for 2001:DB8:8:8::/64 with administrative distance 	110 already exists.
questions.source_ref: CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide Vol 1 (Wendell Odom)

questions.question: Which two commands are required to configure a trunk link on a Cisco switch? (Select two)
questions.explanation: On switches that support both ISL and 802.1Q, you must set the encapsulation before setting the mode. `switchport mode trunk` enables trunking. PortFast and access VLAN are unrelated to trunk setup.

questions.question: Which two EtherChannel protocols can negotiate channel formation automatically? (Select two)
questions.explanation: LACP (IEEE 802.3ad) and PAgP (Cisco proprietary) are negotiation protocols. Static ON mode does not negotiate. BPDU Guard is an STP feature, not an EtherChannel protocol.

questions.question: Which two conditions must be true for two OSPF routers to become neighbors? (Select two)
options: They must be in the same OSPF area
They must have the same hello and dead intervals
They must have the same router-id
questions.explanation: OSPF neighbors must share the same area and have matching hello/dead timer intervals. Router IDs must be unique, not the same.

questions.question: Which two OSPF LSA types are flooded only within a single area? (Select two)
questions.explanation: Type 1 (Router) and Type 2 (Network) LSAs are confined to the originating area. Type 3 is generated by ABRs and crosses areas; Type 5 is flooded throughout the OSPF domain.

questions.question: Order the steps to form an EtherChannel bundle using LACP:
items: Physical links are bundled into a single logical Port-channel interface
Physical interfaces are configured with channel-group mode active
Traffic is load-balanced across all member links
LACP PDUs are exchanged and both sides agree to form the bundle
questions.explanation: EtherChannel formation: Configure member interfaces with channel-group active → LACP negotiates → bundle forms as Port-channel → traffic hashed across links.

questions.question: Order the four fields of an 802.1Q VLAN tag as they appear in the Ethernet frame (left to right):
questions.explanation: 802.1Q tag (4 bytes total): TPID(2B=0x8100) | PCP(3b) | DEI(1b) | VID(12b). TPID identifies the frame as tagged; VID carries the VLAN number (0-4094).

questions.question: Order the OSPF LSA types from Type 1 to Type 5:
items: Type 3 — Summary LSA (generated by ABR, crosses areas)
Type 1 — Router LSA (all routers, flooded within area)
Type 5 — AS External LSA (from ASBR, flooded domain-wide)
Type 2 — Network LSA (generated by DR, flooded within area)
Type 4 — ASBR Summary LSA (generated by ABR)
questions.explanation: LSA types: 1=Router (intra-area), 2=Network (DR, intra-area), 3=Summary (ABR, inter-area), 4=ASBR Summary (ABR), 5=AS External (ASBR, whole domain). Types 1-2 stay within an area.

questions.question: An OSPF router connects to a broadcast segment. Its OSPF priority is 0. What role does it take?
options: DR — highest RID wins when priorities are equal
DROther — priority 0 disqualifies it from DR/BDR
It does not participate in OSPF on broadcast segments
questions.explanation: OSPF priority 0 explicitly disqualifies a router from being elected DR or BDR. It becomes a DROther and only forms FULL adjacency with the DR and BDR, not with other DROthers. Useful for intentionally preventing a router from becoming DR.
hints: Priority 0 = never DR or BDR
Priority range: 0–255; default is 1

questions.question: R1 (RID 2.2.2.2) and R2 (RID 3.3.3.3) are OSPF neighbors in state 2WAY/DROTHER. What does this mean?
options: They have exchanged full LSDBs and are fully adjacent
Both have exchanged hellos but are DROthers — they form 2WAY not FULL adjacency with each other
They are exchanging database descriptor packets
questions.explanation: 2WAY state: bidirectional communication established (each has seen the other in hellos). On broadcast networks, DROther routers form 2WAY with each other — only FULL adjacency with DR and BDR. FULL requires a complete LSDB exchange.
hints: 2WAY = bidirectional hellos confirmed
DROthers: FULL with DR/BDR only, 2WAY with each other

questions.question: R1 has both an OSPF E2 default route (O*E2) and a static default route (S*). Which is preferred and why?
options: OSPF E2 AD=110 vs Static AD=1; static is preferred
OSPF E2 AD=110 vs Static AD=254; OSPF E2 is preferred
OSPF E2 is preferred because it has a lower metric
Both have equal AD; lower metric wins
questions.explanation: Static routes have AD=1 by default. OSPF E2 routes have AD=110. Lower AD = more trusted. Static default (AD=1) wins over OSPF E2 (AD=110) and is installed in the routing table.
hints: AD comparison: lower = more trusted
Static route default AD = 1; OSPF = 110

questions.question: Default OSPF reference bandwidth is 100 Mbps. What is the OSPF cost of a 1 Gbps interface?
questions.explanation: Cost = reference bandwidth / interface bandwidth = 100 Mbps / 1000 Mbps = 0.1, which rounds UP to 1. All interfaces >= 100 Mbps get cost 1 with default reference bandwidth. Use "auto-cost reference-bandwidth 10000" (10 Gbps) to differentiate gigabit links.
hints: Default reference bandwidth = 100 Mbps
Any interface faster than reference gets cost 1 (minimum)

questions.question: "ip route 10.0.0.0 255.0.0.0 192.168.1.2 200" is a floating static route. When is it active?
options: Always — highest AD means always preferred
Only when all other routes to 10.0.0.0/8 are absent (lower AD routes removed)
Only when OSPF is disabled globally
questions.explanation: A floating static has manually-assigned AD higher than the dynamic protocol (AD=200 > OSPF=110). When a better route exists (lower AD), the floating static is suppressed. When the better route disappears, the floating static activates as a backup.
hints: Floating static AD must be HIGHER than primary protocol AD
AD=200 > OSPF=110 → OSPF wins when present; floating static activates when OSPF route is gone

questions.question: EIGRP DUAL: what condition allows a feasible successor to instantly become the new successor without a QUERY?
options: Feasible successor reported distance (RD) < current feasible distance (FD)
Feasible successor FD equals current successor metric
Feasible successor is on the same interface as failed route
Feasible successor was learned more than 5 seconds ago
questions.explanation: Feasibility Condition (FC): a neighbor's reported distance (RD) must be strictly less than the local router's current feasible distance (FD). If met, the neighbor is a feasible successor — provably loop-free — and can be instantly promoted without triggering QUERY messages, enabling sub-second failover.
hints: FC: neighbor RD < local FD
Feasible successor = loop-free backup; FC prevents routing loops

questions.question: "show ip ospf neighbor" shows a neighbor stuck in EXSTART/EXCHANGE. Most likely cause?
options: MTU mismatch between the two interfaces
questions.explanation: EXSTART/EXCHANGE is where DBD (database descriptor) packets are exchanged. An MTU mismatch causes DBD packets to be fragmented or dropped, stalling the exchange. Use "ip ospf mtu-ignore" as a workaround or fix the MTU. Timer mismatches prevent reaching EXSTART at all (neighbors don't form).
hints: EXSTART/EXCHANGE = DBD packet exchange phase
MTU mismatch causes DBD loss; use "ip ospf mtu-ignore"

questions.question: R1 has two equal-cost OSPF paths to 10.1.1.0/24. CEF is enabled. How does Cisco IOS load-balance by default?
options: Per-destination — same destination always uses the same path
Only first path; second is standby
questions.explanation: CEF defaults to per-destination load balancing: a hash of source and destination IP determines which path a flow uses. All packets to the same destination follow the same path, preserving packet ordering. Per-packet is configurable but not the default.
hints: CEF default = per-destination (source+dest IP hash)
Per-packet load balancing can cause out-of-order delivery

questions.question: "show ip ospf database" shows only Type 1 (Router) LSAs — no Type 2 (Network) LSAs. What does this indicate?
options: OSPF is in stub area — Type 2 LSAs suppressed
All OSPF links are point-to-point — Type 2 LSAs are only on broadcast/NBMA segments
R1 is not DR on any segment — Type 2 sent only by DR
questions.explanation: Type 2 (Network) LSAs are generated only by the DR on broadcast or NBMA segments. On point-to-point links (serial, PPP, loopbacks), there is no DR election and no Type 2 LSA — only Type 1 LSAs are exchanged.
hints: Type 2 = Network LSA, generated by DR on broadcast/NBMA only
Point-to-point links: no DR, no Type 2 LSA

questions.question: RIP v2 invalid timer fires at 180s. What happens to the route between the invalid timer (180s) and flush timer (240s)?
options: Route removed at 180s, no further updates
Route stays, marked metric 16 (unreachable), poison reverse sent to neighbors
Route is held down, no updates sent or received
Router queries neighbors for alternate paths
questions.explanation: Between invalid (180s) and flush (240s) timers: the route is marked metric 16 (infinity/unreachable) and kept in the table. Poison reverse updates advertise metric 16 to neighbors, preventing routing loops before the route is fully flushed at 240s.
hints: RIP metric 16 = infinity (unreachable)
Invalid timer: mark metric=16; Flush timer: remove from table

questions.question: R1 "network 10.0.0.0" (no wildcard) in OSPF. R2 uses "network 10.0.0.0 0.0.0.255 area 0". Link is 10.1.1.0/30. Do they become OSPF neighbors?
options: Yes — both cover the /30 link addresses
No — "network 10.0.0.0" with no wildcard uses 0.0.0.0 (exact match), which does not match 10.1.1.1
Yes but only if same OSPF process ID
Yes — classful network statement matches all 10.x.x.x addresses
questions.explanation: "network 10.0.0.0" without a wildcard in OSPF uses a 0.0.0.0 wildcard (exact host match for 10.0.0.0 only). R1's interface 10.1.1.1 does not match — the interface is not activated for OSPF, and the routers will not form a neighbor relationship.
hints: "network x.x.x.x" without wildcard in OSPF = exact host match
10.1.1.1 does not match "network 10.0.0.0 0.0.0.0"

questions.question: OSPF LSA types: which LSA type is generated by an ASBR to advertise external routes into OSPF?
questions.explanation: OSPF LSA types: Type 1 (Router LSA) = each router for its own links. Type 2 (Network LSA) = DR for broadcast segments. Type 3 (Summary LSA) = ABR for inter-area routes. Type 4 (ASBR Summary) = ABR to locate ASBRs. Type 5 (AS External LSA) = ASBR to advertise external routes (redistributed from other protocols). Type 7 (NSSA External) = for NSSA areas.
hints: ASBR = Autonomous System Border Router (connects OSPF to other protocols)
Type 5 = external routes redistributed into OSPF by the ASBR

questions.question: Two OSPF routers are directly connected but will not form a neighbor relationship. "show ip ospf interface" confirms both are in the same area. What should you check FIRST?
options: Verify the OSPF process IDs match on both routers
Check hello/dead timers match and check authentication configuration
Verify both are using the same reference bandwidth
Check that both routers have the same RID
questions.explanation: OSPF neighbor prerequisites (must all match): same area ID, same hello/dead timers, same authentication (type and key), same MTU (unless mtu-ignore), same subnet/mask on the connecting interface, and stub area flags must match. OSPF process IDs do NOT need to match between routers. RIDs must be unique but not equal.
hints: Process IDs do NOT need to match between OSPF neighbors
Hello/dead timers, authentication, area ID, and MTU must match

questions.question: What does the "show ip route" output entry "O 10.1.2.0/24 [110/20] via 192.168.1.1, 00:02:15, Gi0/0" tell you?
options: OSPF route, AD=110, metric (cost)=20, via next-hop 192.168.1.1, learned 2 minutes 15 seconds ago via Gi0/0
OSPF route, metric=110, cost=20, age=2 hours 15 seconds
Open Shortest Path route, age 2:15, AD=20
OSPF external route, Gi0/0 is the destination interface
questions.explanation: "O" = OSPF. [110/20] = [AD/metric]. AD=110 (OSPF default). Metric=20 (OSPF cost to reach 10.1.2.0/24). "via 192.168.1.1" = next-hop IP. "00:02:15" = time since last update. "Gi0/0" = outgoing interface.
hints: [AD/metric] format: first number = administrative distance, second = metric
OSPF "O" = intra-area; "O IA" = inter-area; "O*E2" = external type 2

questions.question: OSPF hello packets are sent to which multicast address on a broadcast segment?
questions.explanation: OSPF uses two multicast addresses: 224.0.0.5 (AllSPFRouters) — used by all OSPF routers to send hellos and LSAs on broadcast/NBMA segments. 224.0.0.6 (AllDRouters) — used by DROther routers to send updates specifically to the DR and BDR. On point-to-point links, 224.0.0.5 is always used.
hints: 224.0.0.5 = AllSPFRouters (all OSPF routers listen)
224.0.0.6 = AllDRouters (only DR and BDR listen)

questions.question: What is the default OSPF dead interval and how does it relate to the hello interval?
options: Dead = 40s (4x hello), hello = 10s on broadcast; dead = 120s (4x), hello = 30s on NBMA
questions.explanation: OSPF defaults: broadcast/point-to-point: hello=10s, dead=40s. NBMA (Frame Relay, ATM): hello=30s, dead=120s. Dead interval = 4x hello interval by default. If a hello is not received within the dead interval, the neighbor is declared down. Both sides MUST have matching hello and dead intervals to form a neighbor.
hints: Dead interval = 4 × hello interval
Broadcast/p2p: hello=10s, dead=40s; NBMA: hello=30s, dead=120s

questions.question: A router has "router ospf 1" with "network 0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255 area 0". What does this accomplish?
options: Advertises a default route 0.0.0.0/0 into OSPF
Activates OSPF on ALL interfaces of the router in area 0 (wildcard mask 255.255.255.255 matches any IP)
Creates a summary route for all subnets
Disables OSPF on all specific interfaces
questions.explanation: "network 0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255 area 0" uses the "any" wildcard mask (255.255.255.255 matches any address). This activates OSPF on every interface of the router, placing them all in area 0. It's a shortcut equivalent to listing every interface individually. Note: this does NOT advertise 0.0.0.0/0 as a default route (use "default-information originate" for that).
hints: Wildcard 255.255.255.255 = match any IP address
"network 0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255 area 0" = enable OSPF on ALL interfaces in area 0

questions.question: What is the EIGRP metric formula using the default K-values?
options: Metric = (10^7 / min-bandwidth) + cumulative-delay (using K1=1, K3=1, all others 0)
questions.explanation: EIGRP default metric (K1=1, K2=0, K3=1, K4=0, K5=0): Metric = 256 × [(10^7 / min-bandwidth-kbps) + (sum-of-delays / 10)]. Only bandwidth (minimum in path) and delay (cumulative) are used by default. Bandwidth contribution: 10^7 / min-bw-kbps. Delay contribution: sum of delays in tens-of-microseconds / 10.
hints: Default: only bandwidth and delay matter (K1 and K3 = 1)
Min bandwidth across the path; cumulative delay (sum of all interface delays)

questions.question: "ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 null0" with "ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 203.0.113.1" — both in routing table. Which is preferred and what is the use case for null0?
options: 203.0.113.1 route is preferred (AD 1 vs AD 1 — tie, so null0 suppressed as less specific)
The more specific null0 route wins
Both are used for load balancing
questions.explanation: Both are static routes with AD=1. When two routes have equal AD and equal prefix length (both 0.0.0.0/0), IOS installs both as equal-cost paths. However, a common use case is null0 as a "summary discard" route: for example, summarize 10.0.0.0/8 via null0 to prevent routing loops when no specific match exists — traffic to unknown subnets within the summary hits null0 and is discarded rather than following a default gateway and looping.
hints: Both AD=1 static routes; equal-cost toward null0 and 203.0.113.1
Null0 use case: summary discard to prevent loops in BGP/OSPF redistribution

questions.question: R1 redistributes RIP routes into OSPF. By default, what OSPF external route type are they assigned and what metric?
options: Type 2 (E2), metric 20 — IOS default for redistribution into OSPF
questions.explanation: When redistributing into OSPF without specifying metric or type, IOS uses: Type E2 (external type 2) and metric 20 by default. E2 = the external metric does NOT increase as the route propagates through OSPF (the metric stays at 20 everywhere). E1 = external metric increases by adding internal OSPF cost as the route propagates.
hints: Default OSPF redistribution: E2, metric 20
E2 metric is constant; E1 metric grows with OSPF path cost

questions.question: "show ip protocols" on R1 shows EIGRP with "Routing for Networks: 10.0.0.0". R1 has interfaces 10.1.1.1/24 and 172.16.1.1/24. Which interface participates in EIGRP?
options: Both — "10.0.0.0" is a classless match covering all 10.x.x.x
Only 10.1.1.1/24 — EIGRP "network 10.0.0.0" uses the classful Class A mask, matching all 10.0.0.0/8 addresses
Neither — network statements require wildcard masks in EIGRP
172.16.1.1/24 only — it is the primary interface
questions.explanation: "network 10.0.0.0" in EIGRP without a wildcard uses the classful network boundary (Class A = /8 = 0.255.255.255 wildcard). This matches all interfaces in the 10.0.0.0/8 range, including 10.1.1.1. 172.16.1.1 is a Class B network — not matched by "network 10.0.0.0". EIGRP uses classful matching without explicit wildcard masks.
hints: "network 10.0.0.0" without wildcard in EIGRP = classful Class A (/8)
Matches all 10.x.x.x addresses; 172.16.x.x is Class B, not matched

questions.question: What happens to OSPF adjacencies if the "router-id" is changed on a running router?
options: Adjacencies update automatically with the new RID
The OSPF process must be cleared ("clear ip ospf process") for the new RID to take effect; until then, the old RID remains in use
Adjacencies reset immediately upon RID change
RID changes are not permitted on a running OSPF process
questions.explanation: OSPF RID is set at process startup. Changing "router-id" in config takes effect only after the OSPF process restarts ("clear ip ospf process" or reload). Until restarted, the old RID is used. After clearing, all adjacencies drop and reform with the new RID — this causes a brief traffic disruption.
hints: RID change requires OSPF process restart to take effect
clear ip ospf process = drops all adjacencies, reforms with new RID

questions.question: OSPF stub area: which LSA types are blocked from entering a stub area?
options: Type 5 (AS External) LSAs; the ABR injects a default route (Type 3) instead
questions.explanation: Stub area: blocks Type 5 (AS External) LSAs from ASBRs. The ABR injects a single Type 3 default route (0.0.0.0/0) into the stub area. Totally stubby area (Cisco extension): also blocks Type 3 inter-area LSAs — only Type 1 and Type 2 intra-area LSAs allowed; single default route for everything external. Reduces LSDB size significantly.
hints: Stub: no Type 5 (external) LSAs; ABR injects default route
Totally stubby: no Type 3 or Type 5; only intra-area + default

questions.question: Two OSPF areas: Area 0 (backbone) and Area 1. Which router type connects them and what LSA does it use?
options: ABR (Area Border Router) using Type 3 (Summary) LSAs to advertise inter-area routes
DR using Type 2 LSAs to connect areas
No special router needed — OSPF areas connect directly
questions.explanation: ABR (Area Border Router): router with interfaces in multiple OSPF areas, including Area 0. Generates Type 3 (Summary) LSAs to advertise routes from one area into another. ABR maintains a separate LSDB per area. Rules: all non-backbone areas must connect to Area 0 via an ABR.
hints: ABR = connects non-backbone area to Area 0
Type 3 = Summary LSA, generated by ABR for inter-area routes

questions.question: "ip route 192.168.1.0 255.255.255.0 Gi0/0" — what problem may arise with this recursive static route on a multi-access (Ethernet) interface?
options: It works correctly on Ethernet; no problem

The route requires the router to ARP for every destination in 192.168.1.0/24 individually since the next-hop is an interface not an IP — results in proxy ARP flooding or incomplete ARP entries on large subnets
Static routes cannot use interfaces — only IP addresses
It only works with directly connected networks
questions.explanation: Static routes pointing to an exit interface (no next-hop IP) on Ethernet: the router treats every destination in 192.168.1.0/24 as directly connected and ARPs for each destination IP. On large subnets this is inefficient — better to specify a next-hop IP: "ip route 192.168.1.0 255.255.255.0 10.0.0.1". Point-to-point interfaces (serial) are fine with interface-only routes.
hints: Interface-only static on Ethernet = ARP for each destination IP (proxy ARP issue)
Always use next-hop IP on Ethernet: "ip route x.x.x.x mask next-hop-ip"

questions.question: EIGRP has a concept of "stuck-in-active" (SIA). When does this occur?
options: When a route is always the best and never needs to be recalculated
When a query is sent for a lost route but no reply is received within the active timer (default 3 minutes); the neighbor relationship is then reset
When a route has equal metrics to two destinations
When EIGRP hello packets are delayed
questions.explanation: SIA (Stuck-In-Active): when a route's successor and all feasible successors fail, EIGRP sends QUERY messages to all neighbors. If a neighbor does not reply within the active timer (3 minutes default), the neighbor is declared SIA-stuck and the adjacency is reset. Common cause: slow WAN links or a neighbor that is also in active state and waiting for ITS neighbors.
hints: SIA: QUERY sent, no REPLY within active timer (3 min)
Result: neighbor adjacency reset; indicates EIGRP scalability issue

questions.question: What does "maximum-paths 4" in OSPF configuration do?
options: Allows OSPF to install up to 4 equal-cost routes to the same destination in the routing table (ECMP)
Sets the maximum hop count to 4
questions.explanation: "maximum-paths" sets how many equal-cost paths OSPF installs in the routing table for a destination. Default is 4 on most platforms (max 16 or 32). All paths must have equal cost (same OSPF metric). CEF then load-balances traffic across the paths using per-destination or per-packet hashing.
hints: maximum-paths = ECMP (equal-cost multi-path) entries per destination
All paths must have equal OSPF metric to be included

questions.question: What is the purpose of EIGRP passive-interface?
options: Disables EIGRP completely on the interface
Prevents EIGRP hello packets from being sent/received on the interface while still advertising the interface's network into EIGRP
Puts the interface in standby mode for failover
Blocks all routing updates but allows hello packets
questions.explanation: "passive-interface" in EIGRP (and OSPF/RIP) stops hello packets on the specified interface — no neighbor adjacency forms. The network attached to that interface is still advertised by EIGRP to other neighbors. Useful for: loopback interfaces (no neighbor needed), user-facing interfaces (prevent EIGRP PDU exposure to end users), stub WAN links.
hints: passive-interface: no hellos, no adjacency — but network still advertised
Use on loopbacks and user-facing interfaces

questions.question: BGP (Border Gateway Protocol) uses which transport protocol and port?
options: TCP 179 — BGP uses TCP for reliable session establishment
EIGRP uses RTP; BGP uses UDP
questions.explanation: BGP uses TCP port 179. BGP relies on TCP's reliable delivery for its UPDATE messages — no need for its own reliability mechanism. BGP neighbors (peers) are configured manually (not discovered automatically). eBGP peers are typically directly connected; iBGP peers can be anywhere within the AS (often via loopback-to-loopback sessions).
hints: BGP = TCP 179
Neighbors configured manually; TCP provides reliability

questions.question: What is the difference between eBGP and iBGP?
options: eBGP = exterior (between different ASes); iBGP = interior (within the same AS). eBGP default TTL=1 (direct connection required); iBGP default TTL=255
eBGP uses TCP; iBGP uses UDP
eBGP is for IPv4; iBGP is for IPv6
eBGP requires IGP; iBGP is the IGP
questions.explanation: eBGP (external BGP): between routers in different Autonomous Systems. TTL=1 by default — peers must be directly connected (or use "ebgp-multihop" for indirect). iBGP (internal BGP): between routers in the same AS. TTL=255. iBGP requires full mesh or route reflectors (iBGP split horizon: routes learned via iBGP not advertised to other iBGP peers).
hints: eBGP = different AS; iBGP = same AS
eBGP TTL=1 (direct); iBGP TTL=255 (any path within AS)

questions.question: OSPF area 0 is the backbone. What happens if a non-zero area is not connected to Area 0?
options: The area works normally — Area 0 is optional
Routes from that area cannot reach other areas — inter-area routing fails. Solution: virtual link through a transit area or redesign
The area becomes a stub area automatically
questions.explanation: OSPF rule: all non-backbone areas MUST connect to Area 0 directly. If Area 2 connects to Area 1 (not Area 0) and Area 1 connects to Area 0, Area 2 routes cannot be redistributed properly into Area 0. Fix: virtual link through Area 1 (if Area 1 is not stub) to logically connect Area 2 to Area 0, or redesign the area topology.
hints: All OSPF areas must connect to Area 0
Disconnected area: use virtual link as temporary fix, or redesign

questions.question: "show ip route" shows "L 10.1.1.1/32" on a router interface. What does the "L" prefix mean?
options: L = Local route — automatically created by IOS for the exact IP address of a configured interface (host route /32); used to receive packets addressed to the router itself
L = learned via OSPF local area
questions.explanation: IOS automatically creates a /32 "L" (Local) route for each interface IP address. This ensures packets addressed to the router itself (management, OSPF hellos, SSH sessions) are handled locally without using the routing table. "C" routes are the connected subnet. "L" routes are the specific host address.
hints: L = Local: /32 host route for the router's own interface IP
C = Connected subnet; L = Exact interface IP address

questions.question: What is split horizon and how does it prevent routing loops in RIP?
options: Prevents routes from being advertised back out the interface they were learned on; if R1 learns route to 10.0.0.0 from R2 via Gi0/1, R1 will not advertise 10.0.0.0 back to R2 on Gi0/1
Splits large routing tables into multiple update packets
Limits the number of hops a route can traverse to 15
Sends routes with metric 16 to prevent loops
questions.explanation: Split horizon: do not advertise a route back out the interface it was received on. Prevents: if R2's link to 10.0.0.0 fails, R1 would otherwise re-advertise 10.0.0.0 back to R2 (thinking it has an alternate path), causing R2 to think 10.0.0.0 is still reachable via R1 — a routing loop. Split horizon with poison reverse sends metric 16 (infinity) back to explicitly announce the route is unreachable.
hints: Split horizon: don't advertise routes back the way they came
Poison reverse: advertise the route back with metric 16 (explicit unreachable)

questions.question: A router has OSPF and EIGRP both running and both learn 192.168.50.0/24. Which installs in the routing table?
options: EIGRP — lower default AD (90) wins over OSPF (110)
questions.explanation: When different protocols learn the same route, the one with lower Administrative Distance wins and is installed in the routing table. EIGRP internal AD=90. OSPF AD=110. 90 < 110, so EIGRP's route wins. The OSPF route sits in the protocol's topology table but is not installed in the IP routing table.
hints: EIGRP internal AD=90; OSPF AD=110
Lower AD = installed in routing table; higher AD route held in reserve

questions.question: What is the purpose of OSPF "area X range" command on an ABR?
options: Defines the IP range for OSPF area membership
Summarizes multiple intra-area routes into a single Type 3 LSA for advertisement into other areas (manual summarization at the ABR)
Limits the number of routes in an area
Creates a summary route injected into area X
questions.explanation: "area X range A.B.C.D mask" on an ABR: summarizes all routes within Area X that fall within the specified range into a single Type 3 Summary LSA advertised to other areas. Reduces LSDB size and improves scalability. The summary covers the range even if individual routes do not all exist — creates an aggregated advertisement.
hints: area X range = ABR manual summarization → one Type 3 LSA per summary
Reduces inter-area LSA count and LSDB size

questions.question: A router's routing table has: 10.0.0.0/8 via OSPF, 10.1.0.0/16 via EIGRP, 10.1.1.0/24 via RIP. A packet arrives for 10.1.1.50. Which route is used?
options: 10.1.1.0/24 — longest prefix match wins regardless of AD
Tied — all three contain the destination
questions.explanation: Longest prefix match always wins in IP routing, regardless of AD or metric. 10.1.1.0/24 (/24 = 24 bits match) is more specific than 10.1.0.0/16 (/16 = 16 bits) and 10.0.0.0/8 (/8 = 8 bits). The most specific matching route is always used first.
hints: Longest prefix match: most specific route wins — AD and metric are irrelevant
/24 > /16 > /8 in specificity

questions.question: What is the administrative distance of a static route configured with `ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 gi0/1`?
questions.explanation: Static routes have AD of 1. Connected interfaces have AD 0. EIGRP internal=90, OSPF=110, RIP=120. Static routes (AD=1) are preferred over dynamic routing protocols.
hints: AD: Connected=0, Static=1, EIGRP=90, OSPF=110, RIP=120

questions.question: An OSPF router has four area 0 neighbors. One neighbor shows 'EXSTART' for an extended time. What is the most likely cause?
options: MTU mismatch between the two routers — the DBD exchange cannot complete
Duplicate Router IDs in the area
The hello interval is set differently on each router
questions.explanation: EXSTART state means the routers are trying to elect a master/slave for DBD exchange. It stalls here when MTU values differ, causing DBD packets to be dropped by the lower-MTU side.
hints: EXSTART stall = MTU mismatch; check ip ospf mtu-ignore or fix MTU

questions.question: Which OSPF packet type is used to synchronize the link-state database between OSPF neighbors?
questions.explanation: DBD (Type 2) packets summarize the LSDB during adjacency formation. Routers compare DBDs and send LSRs for missing LSAs; the neighbor responds with LSUs containing the full LSAs.
hints: Hello=discover, DBD=summarize DB, LSR=request, LSU=send LSAs, LSAck=ack

questions.question: In OSPF, which router type connects Area 0 to a non-backbone area?
questions.explanation: ABR connects to Area 0 and at least one other area. ASBR redistributes routes from external routing domains. DR/BDR are for multi-access segments, not inter-area roles.
hints: ABR = between areas; ASBR = between AS (external routes)

questions.question: What does the OSPF DR/BDR election prevent on a multi-access segment?
options: N*(N-1)/2 adjacencies — all routers form full adjacencies with the DR/BDR only, reducing LSDB flooding overhead
Route loops in the OSPF domain
OSPF from forming adjacencies across subnets
Duplicate LSAs from reaching all routers
questions.explanation: Without DR/BDR, every OSPF router on the segment would form full adjacency with every other router (O(N^2) adjacencies). DR/BDR means DROthers only fully adjoin with DR/BDR, reducing adjacencies to O(N).
hints: DR/BDR reduces adjacency count from N² to N

questions.question: An OSPF router has priority 0 configured on an interface. What does this mean for DR/BDR election?
options: The router is ineligible to become DR or BDR on that segment
The router automatically wins DR election
The interface uses the loopback IP as Router ID
Priority 0 disables OSPF on that interface
questions.explanation: OSPF priority 0 means the router will never be elected DR or BDR on that segment. It remains a DROther. Set with `ip ospf priority 0`.
hints: Priority 0 = never DR/BDR

questions.question: An EIGRP router's successor fails. A feasible successor exists in the topology table. What happens?
options: The feasible successor is immediately promoted to successor without a new DUAL computation, providing rapid convergence
DUAL runs a full diffusing computation before the FS is used
The router broadcasts a route query to all EIGRP neighbors
The route enters active state while querying neighbors
questions.explanation: A feasible successor (FS) satisfies the Feasibility Condition (its Reported Distance < current Feasible Distance). If the successor fails, the FS is instantly promoted without querying neighbors — this is EIGRP's key fast-convergence advantage.
hints: FS available = instant failover; no FS = route goes active and queries begin

questions.question: What is the EIGRP Feasibility Condition?
options: A neighbor's Reported Distance (RD) must be less than the local router's current Feasible Distance (FD) to be a feasible successor
RD must equal FD for a feasible successor
FD must be less than the successor's metric
The neighbor's FD must be less than all other neighbors' metrics
questions.explanation: Feasibility Condition: neighbor's RD < local FD. This guarantees the neighbor doesn't route through the local router (no loops). If RD >= FD, the neighbor could be routing through us, creating a loop risk.
hints: FC: neighbor RD < my FD → safe, no loop

questions.question: A router running OSPF shows a route as O E2 in the routing table. What does this mean?
options: External Type 2 — the metric is the external metric at the redistribution point and does NOT increase as the route traverses OSPF
External Type 1 — the metric increases as the route travels through OSPF
A summary LSA from an ABR
questions.explanation: O E2 routes use external (ASBR) metric only, which does not increase as the route traverses more OSPF hops. O E1 routes add the OSPF internal cost to the external metric.
hints: E2 metric = constant; E1 metric = external + internal OSPF cost

questions.question: What is the purpose of the OSPF `passive-interface` command?
options: Suppresses OSPF hello packets on the interface so neighbors cannot form adjacency on that link, while still advertising the connected network
Disables OSPF completely on the interface
Prevents routes learned via that interface from entering the routing table
Forces the interface to use stub area type
questions.explanation: Passive-interface stops OSPF hellos on the specified interface (preventing neighbor formation) but still advertises the connected subnet in OSPF LSAs. Used on LAN interfaces with no OSPF neighbors.
hints: Passive = advertise but no adjacency; good for LAN access ports

questions.question: Which command verifies OSPF neighbor adjacency and their current state?
questions.explanation: `show ip ospf neighbor` lists all OSPF neighbors with their state (FULL, 2WAY, EXSTART, etc.), dead interval, and interface.
hints: FULL = fully adjacent; 2WAY = hello exchanged but not fully adjacent (DROther to DROther)

questions.question: IPv6 uses which mechanism to discover the MAC address of a neighbor on the same link (equivalent to ARP)?
options: Neighbor Discovery Protocol (NDP) Neighbor Solicitation/Neighbor Advertisement
questions.explanation: NDP uses ICMPv6 Neighbor Solicitation (type 135) and Neighbor Advertisement (type 136) messages to resolve IPv6 addresses to MAC addresses, replacing ARP.
hints: NDP NS/NA = IPv6 ARP equivalent

questions.question: What is the IPv6 equivalent of the IPv4 default route (0.0.0.0/0)?
questions.explanation: ::/0 is the IPv6 default route (all zeros, all bits wildcard). ::1/128 is the loopback. FF00::/8 is multicast. FE80::/10 is link-local.
hints: IPv6 default route = ::/0

questions.question: An OSPF router is in a totally stubby area. What routes does it receive from the ABR?
options: Only a default route (0.0.0.0/0) — no Type 3, 4, or 5 LSAs
All intra-area and inter-area routes, but no external routes
All routes including external, but summarized
questions.explanation: Totally stubby areas (Cisco extension): only receive a default route from the ABR. Type 3 (inter-area), 4, and 5 LSAs are blocked. Simplest area type.
hints: Totally stubby = only default route; stub = default + intra-area

questions.question: EIGRP uses a composite metric. Which two components are used by default?
questions.explanation: EIGRP's default composite metric uses Bandwidth (minimum on path) and Delay (cumulative on path). Load, Reliability, and MTU are available but disabled (K values K1=K3=1, K2=K4=K5=0 by default).
hints: Default EIGRP metric: bandwidth (min) + delay (sum)

questions.question: What command configures a default route on Router A pointing to 192.168.1.1 as the next hop?
questions.explanation: `ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 <next-hop>` creates a default route (gateway of last resort). The all-zeros network and mask match any destination.
hints: Default route: ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 <next-hop>

questions.question: A router running OSPF shows a neighbor in 2WAY state indefinitely. What does this indicate?
options: Both routers are DROthers on a multi-access segment — 2WAY is normal between DROthers
The OSPF process has stalled during database exchange
The neighbor's hello timer is misconfigured
questions.explanation: On multi-access segments, DROther routers see each other in 2WAY state (they exchange hellos but do not form full adjacency). Full adjacency only forms between DROthers and the DR/BDR. This is normal.
hints: 2WAY between DROthers on Ethernet = normal behavior

questions.question: What is the purpose of OSPF Area 0 (backbone area)?
options: All inter-area routing must pass through Area 0 to prevent routing loops between areas
Area 0 contains only the highest-priority routers
Area 0 is the only area that supports external routes
Area 0 uses a different OSPF packet format than other areas
questions.explanation: OSPF requires all non-backbone areas to connect to Area 0. Inter-area routes must transit Area 0 to prevent intra-OSPF routing loops between areas.
hints: All areas connect to backbone (Area 0) for inter-area routing

questions.question: Which IPv6 address type is equivalent to IPv4's 169.254.x.x link-local range?
questions.explanation: FE80::/10 is the IPv6 link-local range. Link-local addresses are automatically assigned to all IPv6 interfaces and are only valid on the local link (not routed).
hints: FE80::/10 = link-local; always present on IPv6 interfaces

questions.question: A static route configured with a higher administrative distance than a dynamic route to the same destination is called what?
options: Floating static route — used as a backup when the dynamic route disappears
questions.explanation: Floating static routes have a manually set AD higher than the dynamic routing protocol (e.g., AD 130 for a backup to EIGRP's 90). They are inactive in the routing table while the dynamic route exists, floating into use when the dynamic route is gone.
hints: Floating static = higher AD than the dynamic protocol; appears only when dynamic fails

questions.question: An OSPF router's RID must be unique. If two routers have duplicate RIDs, what happens?
options: Adjacency forms but routes may be incorrect — the LSDB becomes inconsistent because LSAs from both routers share the same RID
The router with the lower RID is automatically renumbered
OSPF prevents adjacency and logs an error until the conflict is resolved
Both routers become DR for their segment
questions.explanation: Duplicate OSPF RIDs allow adjacencies to form (OSPF doesn't explicitly block it) but cause LSDB corruption — each router's LSAs overwrite the other's, producing incorrect or inconsistent routing.
hints: Duplicate RID = LSDB corruption; OSPF doesn't proactively prevent adjacency formation

questions.question: Which OSPF timer must match between neighbors for an adjacency to form?
options: Hello interval and Dead interval (must be equal on both sides of the link)
questions.explanation: OSPF requires Hello and Dead intervals to match on both routers of a link. If they differ, neighbors detect each other but reject the adjacency formation.
hints: Mismatched hello/dead timers = adjacency won't form

questions.question: What is EIGRP SIA (Stuck in Active) and what causes it?
options: A route remains in Active state indefinitely because a queried neighbor fails to respond within the active timer; EIGRP tears down the peering with non-responding neighbors
SIA occurs when EIGRP is overloaded with too many routes
SIA means the route metric is too high to be installed
SIA is an informational state indicating route convergence
questions.explanation: When a route goes Active (no feasible successor), EIGRP queries neighbors. If a neighbor doesn't respond within the active timer (default 3 minutes), the route is SIA. EIGRP resets the adjacency with non-responding neighbors.
hints: SIA = query never answered within active timer = neighbor reset

questions.question: Which IPv6 routing protocol is the IPv6 equivalent of RIPv2 and uses UDP port 521?
questions.explanation: RIPng is the IPv6 extension of RIP (RFC 2080). Uses UDP port 521, multicast FF02::9 (all RIP routers). Max 15 hops. OSPFv3 is the IPv6 OSPF version.
hints: RIPng = RIP for IPv6; UDP 521; multicast FF02::9

questions.question: What is the purpose of OSPF Type 3 LSAs?
options: Summary LSAs generated by ABRs to advertise inter-area routes — networks from one OSPF area summarized for other areas
Advertise external routes redistributed into OSPF from other protocols
Describe router links within a single area
Describe multi-access network segments with a DR
questions.explanation: Type 3 LSAs (Summary LSAs) are generated by ABRs to carry route information between areas. They represent inter-area routes (O IA) and enable routing between non-backbone areas via Area 0.
hints: Type 1=router, Type 2=network, Type 3=inter-area summary (ABR), Type 5=external (ASBR)

questions.question: What command enables IPv6 routing on a Cisco router?
questions.explanation: `ipv6 unicast-routing` enables IPv6 forwarding globally on the router. Without it, the router accepts IPv6 packets for itself but does not forward them between interfaces.
hints: ipv6 unicast-routing = enable IPv6 forwarding (like ip routing for IPv4)

questions.question: In a BGP context, what does 'iBGP' mean and how does it differ from 'eBGP'?
options: iBGP runs between routers in the same autonomous system; eBGP runs between routers in different autonomous systems
eBGP runs within the AS; iBGP runs between ASes
They are identical except for the hold timer value
questions.explanation: iBGP (internal BGP): peers within the same AS. eBGP (external BGP): peers in different ASes. Key difference: eBGP routes are forwarded; iBGP requires full mesh or route reflectors to prevent loops.
hints: iBGP = same AS; eBGP = different AS

questions.question: Which OSPF area type blocks Type 5 external LSAs but still receives Type 3 inter-area LSAs and injects a default route?
questions.explanation: Stub area blocks Type 4 and 5 LSAs (external routes) but still receives Type 3 inter-area summaries. A default route is injected for external destinations. Totally stubby also blocks Type 3.
hints: Stub: blocks external (T4/T5), keeps inter-area (T3), gets default route

questions.question: A router receives a packet destined for 10.1.5.100. The routing table shows two equal-cost paths (same AD, same metric) via two different interfaces. What does the router do by default?
options: Load-balances the traffic across both paths (per-destination or per-packet depending on config)
Always uses the path with the lower interface index
Sends the packet via the first path and ignores the second
Drops the packet because of ambiguity
questions.explanation: ECMP (Equal-Cost Multi-Path): Cisco IOS installs both routes and load balances. Default is per-destination CEF-based load balancing (same dest always uses same path for session stability).
hints: Equal-cost routes = ECMP load balancing; CEF uses per-destination by default

questions.question: Which command displays OSPF routes in the routing table?
questions.explanation: `show ip route ospf` filters the routing table to display only OSPF-learned routes (marked with 'O', 'O IA', 'O E1', 'O E2', 'O N1', 'O N2').
hints: show ip route ospf = OSPF routes only

questions.question: Which mechanism allows OSPF to operate across a non-broadcast multi-access (NBMA) network like Frame Relay?
options: Configure neighbor statements manually (ip ospf neighbor) or use point-to-multipoint network type
OSPF automatically detects NBMA and forms adjacencies via broadcast
Frame Relay is not supported by OSPF
Use RIP instead of OSPF for NBMA networks
questions.explanation: OSPF defaults on NBMA interfaces may not work because broadcast is not native. Solutions include manual `neighbor` statements, changing the OSPF network type to `point-to-multipoint`, or using `broadcast` type with a full mesh.
hints: NBMA solutions: manual neighbors OR change network type to p2mp

questions.question: What is the EIGRP successor route in the topology table?
options: The best path to a destination — the route with the lowest Feasible Distance, installed in the routing table
The path with the highest bandwidth
A backup path maintained in case the primary fails
The path through the highest-priority neighbor
questions.explanation: EIGRP Successor: the route with the lowest Feasible Distance (FD) to a destination. It is installed in the routing table. The Feasible Successor (FS) is a backup route meeting the Feasibility Condition.
hints: Successor = best path (lowest FD) = in routing table; Feasible Successor = backup

questions.question: OSPFv3 differs from OSPFv2 in which key way?
options: OSPFv3 supports IPv6 and runs per-link (not per-subnet); it uses link-local addresses for neighbor discovery and no longer carries subnet information in hello packets
OSPFv3 uses TCP instead of IP protocol 89
OSPFv3 does not support area types
questions.explanation: OSPFv3 (RFC 5340) is designed for IPv6. Key differences: runs per-link, uses link-local addresses for adjacency, carries no address semantics in OSPF headers (prefix info in new LSA types), and runs over IP protocol 89 like OSPFv2.
hints: OSPFv3 = OSPF for IPv6; uses link-local; runs per link

questions.question: What is the purpose of OSPF route redistribution?
options: Imports routes from one routing protocol (or static/connected) into OSPF so they can be advertised as external OSPF routes
Summarizes OSPF routes before advertising to BGP
Converts OSPF routes to EIGRP format automatically
Redistributes OSPF into RIP using equal metrics
questions.explanation: Redistribution injects routes from external sources (RIP, EIGRP, static, connected) into OSPF as Type 5 (or Type 7 in NSSA) external LSAs. The redistributing router becomes an ASBR.
hints: Redistribution = importing external routes into OSPF; redistributing router = ASBR

questions.question: Which EIGRP term describes the total metric to reach a destination network, including the cost reported by the next-hop neighbor?
questions.explanation: FD (Feasible Distance) = the total metric from the local router to the destination, including the metric to the neighbor plus the neighbor's RD. RD (Reported Distance/Advertised Distance) is what the neighbor reports as ITS distance.
hints: FD = local total; RD = neighbor's distance (what they tell you)

questions.question: Which EIGRP packet type is sent in response to a Query when a router has no feasible successor and no alternative path?
options: Reply with infinite metric — indicates the destination is unreachable through this router
Acknowledgment with an empty routing table
Update with the best available path
questions.explanation: When EIGRP puts a route in Active state and queries neighbors, a neighbor with no path to the destination replies with an infinite metric (metric = 0xFFFFFFFF or similar), indicating it is not a viable path.
hints: Query: do you have a path? Reply with infinite = I don't have one

questions.question: An OSPF router receives a Type 1 Router LSA from Router ID 1.1.1.1 in Area 0. This LSA describes what?
options: All of Router 1.1.1.1's directly connected links within Area 0 and their costs
All routes redistributed into OSPF from external sources
The DR/BDR network segment Router 1.1.1.1 is connected to
Inter-area summary routes from an ABR
questions.explanation: Type 1 Router LSA: generated by every OSPF router, describes the router's own links (interfaces) and costs within its area. Flooded within the area only.
hints: Type 1 = router's own links; flooded within area only

questions.question: Which IPv6 mechanism allows a host to automatically configure its own address from a router advertisement without a DHCPv6 server?
questions.explanation: SLAAC: host receives an RA from the router containing the /64 prefix. Host generates the IID using EUI-64 or a random value (RFC 7217) and forms the global unicast address without a DHCPv6 server.
hints: SLAAC = RA prefix + EUI-64/random IID = auto-configured IPv6 address

questions.question: What does 'recursive routing' mean in the context of a routing table lookup?
options: A route lookup requires multiple table lookups — the next-hop IP is looked up again in the routing table to find the outgoing interface
Routes that reference themselves, causing a routing loop
The router checks the routing table in both directions
Recursive routing is a term for equal-cost load balancing
questions.explanation: Recursive route lookup: when a route specifies a next-hop IP (not an exit interface), the router looks up the next-hop IP in the routing table to find which interface to use. May recurse multiple levels.
hints: Recursive lookup: find next-hop IP → look up that IP in routing table → find interface

questions.question: Which OSPF network type does NOT elect a DR/BDR?
questions.explanation: OSPF network types with DR/BDR: Broadcast, Non-Broadcast. Types without DR/BDR: Point-to-point (default on serial) and Point-to-multipoint. DR/BDR only needed where multiple routers share a segment.
hints: No DR/BDR: point-to-point, point-to-multipoint; DR/BDR: broadcast, NBMA

questions.question: What is the difference between an OSPF stub area and an NSSA?
options: Stub blocks external routes (Type 5 LSAs); NSSA also blocks Type 5 but allows a local ASBR to redistribute external routes as Type 7 LSAs within the area
Stub allows external routes; NSSA blocks them
They are identical — NSSA is just a newer name for stub
questions.explanation: Stub: no external LSAs (Type 5 blocked). NSSA (Not-So-Stubby Area): blocks Type 5 but allows redistribution via Type 7 LSAs locally. The ABR translates Type 7 to Type 5 when passing to Area 0.
hints: NSSA = stub + local redistribution allowed (Type 7 LSAs)

questions.question: Two EIGRP routers fail to form adjacency. `show ip eigrp neighbors` shows no neighbors. Which mismatch is the FIRST thing to verify?
options: AS number — EIGRP adjacencies only form between routers with the same autonomous system number
MTU — too large packets prevent Hello exchange
Authentication — incorrect key prevents Hello acceptance
Hello interval — different timers prevent adjacency
questions.explanation: EIGRP adjacency requirements (in order of common issues): same AS number, same K values, same subnet, authentication if configured. Hello/hold timer mismatch does NOT prevent EIGRP adjacency (unlike OSPF).
hints: EIGRP: AS number + K values + subnet must match; hello timers DON'T need to match

questions.question: What is the purpose of the OSPF reference bandwidth and why might you change it?
options: Reference bandwidth / link bandwidth = cost. Default 100 Mbps reference means 1 Gbps and 10 Gbps links both get cost 1 — increase reference bandwidth to differentiate fast links
Reference bandwidth sets the Hello interval
It determines which router becomes the DR
Reference bandwidth is the maximum routable bandwidth through OSPF
questions.explanation: OSPF cost = reference_bandwidth / interface_bandwidth. Default ref=100 Mbps means any link ≥100 Mbps gets cost 1, making 1G and 10G indistinguishable. Set `auto-cost reference-bandwidth 10000` (for 10 Gbps) to differentiate.
hints: auto-cost reference-bandwidth = set higher for modern fast links

questions.question: Which command enables EIGRP for IPv6 on a Cisco router interface?
options: eigrp ipv6 enable on the interface
questions.explanation: EIGRPv6 (Classic mode): configure `ipv6 router eigrp <AS>` globally, then `ipv6 eigrp <AS>` on each interface. Named EIGRP mode uses `address-family ipv6` under `router eigrp`.
hints: EIGRPv6 classic: ipv6 router eigrp globally + ipv6 eigrp <AS> per interface

questions.question: A network has two routers with OSPF. The routing table shows `O IA 10.5.0.0/24`. What does 'IA' indicate?
options: Inter-area route — learned from a different OSPF area via an ABR
An external route redistributed into OSPF
An intra-area route within the local area
An OSPF area not connected to Area 0
questions.explanation: OSPF route codes: O=intra-area, O IA=inter-area (from another area via ABR Type 3 LSA), O E1/E2=external (redistributed via ASBR), O N1/N2=NSSA external.
hints: O=intra-area, O IA=inter-area, O E1/E2=external redistributed

questions.question: What is the OSPF Hello interval default on a broadcast (Ethernet) network?
questions.explanation: OSPF Hello intervals: Broadcast and point-to-point networks = 10 seconds (dead = 40s). NBMA networks = 30 seconds (dead = 120s).
hints: OSPF Hello: broadcast=10s, NBMA=30s; dead = 4× hello

questions.question: What is the purpose of the OSPF Type 2 (Network) LSA?
options: Generated by the DR on a multi-access segment, lists all routers attached to that segment, and carries the subnet mask
Generated by ABRs to summarize routes between areas
Describes external routes redistributed into OSPF
Generated by each router to describe its own links
questions.explanation: Type 2 Network LSA: generated by the Designated Router (DR) on broadcast/NBMA segments. Lists all routers on that segment and includes the subnet mask. Flooded within the area only.
hints: Type 2 = DR generates; describes the multi-access network segment

questions.question: Which EIGRP table is checked FIRST when the routing table is consulted for a destination?
options: Routing table (from the topology table's successors)
questions.explanation: EIGRP topology table stores all routes (successors and feasible successors). The routing table only contains the best (successor) routes. When forwarding, the routing table is consulted (which got its entries from the topology table's successors).
hints: EIGRP lookup order: routing table (successor) → topology table (if route goes active)

questions.question: A static route is configured: `ip route 10.0.0.0 255.0.0.0 null0`. What is the purpose of routing to Null0?
options: Creates a summary/aggregate route that drops traffic matching sub-routes if no more specific route exists — prevents routing loops and black-hole routing
Null0 sends ICMP Unreachable to senders when no path exists
Null0 is used for testing only
Routes to Null0 always have AD 255 and are never used
questions.explanation: Routing to Null0 (bit bucket): used with route summarization to prevent sub-optimal routing. If a more specific route exists, it matches; if not, the Null0 route drops the packet cleanly instead of looping.
hints: Null0 route = discard; used with summaries to prevent sub-summary leakage

questions.question: What is the difference between `show ip route` and `show ip route summary`?
options: `show ip route` displays the full routing table; `show ip route summary` shows only counts by protocol (how many routes of each type)
summary shows routes with AD and metric only
questions.explanation: `show ip route summary` is a compact view showing the number of routes per source (static, connected, OSPF, EIGRP, etc.) and total route count. Useful for capacity planning.
hints: show ip route summary = counts by protocol; not the actual routes

questions.question: What is OSPFv3's method for supporting both IPv4 and IPv6?
options: Address families — OSPFv3 AF allows running one OSPFv3 process with separate IPv4 and IPv6 address families
OSPFv3 only supports IPv6; OSPFv2 handles IPv4
OSPFv3 automatically dual-stacks both IPv4 and IPv6
OSPFv3 requires two separate processes for IPv4 and IPv6
questions.explanation: Modern OSPFv3 with Address Families (RFC 5838) allows a single OSPFv3 process to carry both IPv4 and IPv6 routing information. Previously, OSPFv3 was IPv6-only.
hints: OSPFv3 AF = one process handles both IPv4 and IPv6 families

questions.question: Which command enables OSPF on specific interfaces by matching their IP subnets?
options: ip ospf <process> area <id> on each interface
questions.explanation: Under `router ospf <process-id>`, the `network <subnet> <wildcard> area <id>` command matches interfaces whose IP falls in the range. Alternatively, `ip ospf <process> area <id>` directly on each interface.
hints: network command under router ospf = match interfaces by IP; or use ip ospf per-interface

questions.question: What does the `debug ip routing` command display?
options: Shows real-time routing table changes — route additions, deletions, and modifications
Shows all routing protocol packets received
Displays CPU usage for routing computations
questions.explanation: `debug ip routing` outputs messages whenever routes are installed or removed from the routing table. Useful for diagnosing intermittent route flapping. Use carefully — high output volume.
hints: debug ip routing = routing table changes (install/remove); high volume in busy networks

questions.question: Which BGP attribute is used as the primary path selection criterion for routes advertised to eBGP peers?
options: AS Path (shortest AS Path length wins)
questions.explanation: BGP path selection order (simplified): Weight > Local Preference > AS Path > Origin > MED > eBGP over iBGP > IGP metric. AS Path is the primary globally-significant attribute — shorter is preferred.
hints: BGP: Weight > Local Pref (iBGP) > AS Path > Origin > MED

questions.question: What is the purpose of a loopback interface in OSPF and BGP configuration?
options: Provides a stable, always-up Router ID and peering address — loopbacks never go down when physical links fail
Loopbacks are only used for testing and have no production purpose
Loopbacks are required for NAT operation
questions.explanation: Loopback interfaces are software-only and never go down (unless explicitly shut down). Used as OSPF/BGP Router IDs and BGP peer addresses for stability. Physical interface flaps don't affect loopback-based peering.
hints: Loopback = stable RID + BGP peer address; never goes down

questions.question: What does 'OSPF convergence' mean and what factors affect its speed?
options: All routers reach a consistent, loop-free view of the topology; affected by SPF timer (delay + hold), LSA flood propagation time, and dead interval detection
Convergence means the OSPF process restarts
Convergence speed depends only on interface bandwidth
OSPF convergence is always exactly 40 seconds
questions.explanation: OSPF convergence: all routers have the same LSDB and computed routing table. Factors: dead interval (failure detection = 40s default), LSA flood time, SPF delay/hold timers (incrementally tunable).
hints: Convergence time ≈ dead interval + LSA flood + SPF computation

questions.question: Which EIGRP packet type does NOT require an acknowledgment?
questions.explanation: EIGRP packet types: Hello and Ack are unreliable (no ack required). Update, Query, Reply, and Request use RTP (Reliable Transport Protocol) — they require acknowledgment.
hints: Hello + ACK = unreliable (no ack); Update/Query/Reply = reliable (ack required)

questions.question: Which command shows the OSPF LSDB (Link State Database)?
questions.explanation: `show ip ospf database` displays the LSDB contents — all LSAs by type (Router, Network, Summary, etc.) that the router has received and stored.
hints: show ip ospf database = LSDB contents; show ip ospf neighbor = adjacency info

questions.question: What is the EIGRP variance feature used for?
options: Allows unequal-cost load balancing across paths with metrics within a multiplier of the successor's metric
Forces all traffic to the best path only
Sets the maximum metric for routes to be installed
Configures the K-value weights for EIGRP metric calculation
questions.explanation: EIGRP variance: `variance <multiplier>` allows feasible successors with FD up to <multiplier> × successor's FD to be installed in the routing table and share traffic proportionally.
hints: variance = unequal load balancing; metrics within variance × best FD are used

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 Null0` route commonly found in route summarization configurations?
options: Prevents routing loops by discarding traffic that matches the summary but has no more specific route
Creates a default route for internet traffic
Sends all traffic to the null interface for testing
Enables ECMP for the summary route
questions.explanation: A summary route with Null0 as the exit interface acts as a 'discard route' for sub-prefixes not specifically routed. Without it, routers might forward mismatched packets to the default gateway in a loop.
hints: Summary + Null0 = discard unmatched sub-routes; prevents routing loops

questions.question: OSPF uses which algorithm to calculate the shortest path through the network?
questions.explanation: OSPF is a link-state protocol that uses Dijkstra's SPF algorithm to compute the shortest path tree from the router's perspective using the LSDB. EIGRP uses DUAL. RIP uses Bellman-Ford.
hints: OSPF = link-state + Dijkstra SPF; EIGRP = DUAL; RIP = Bellman-Ford

questions.question: Which static route type doesn't require a recursive lookup and uses the exit interface directly?
options: Recursive static route with next-hop IP
questions.explanation: Directly attached static routes specify the exit interface instead of a next-hop IP. They don't require a recursive lookup to resolve the next-hop. Caveat on multi-access networks: they generate proxy ARP/neighbor entries for every destination.
hints: ip route X X GigabitEthernet0/1 = directly attached; no recursive lookup needed

questions.question: What is the purpose of the OSPF MaxAge value (3600 seconds)?
options: LSAs with age reaching MaxAge are considered expired and are flushed from the LSDB and refloded to notify neighbors
MaxAge is the hold-down timer after a neighbor failure
MaxAge defines the OSPF hello interval for stub areas
LSAs are refreshed every 3600 seconds regardless of topology changes
questions.explanation: OSPF LSA age: increments each second. At MaxAge (3600s), the LSA is considered stale, flushed from the LSDB, and re-flooded with MaxAge to tell neighbors to remove it. LSAs are refreshed every 1800s by the originator.
hints: MaxAge=3600s; LSAs refreshed every 1800s; at MaxAge = flush and re-flood

questions.question: Which EIGRP feature prevents routing loops without requiring periodic full routing table exchanges?
options: DUAL (Diffusing Update Algorithm) with the Feasibility Condition
questions.explanation: EIGRP's DUAL algorithm uses the Feasibility Condition (neighbor RD < local FD) to guarantee loop-free paths mathematically, without the need for periodic full table exchanges like RIP.
hints: DUAL + Feasibility Condition = loop-free guarantee without holddown timers

questions.question: What does `show ip eigrp topology all-links` display that `show ip eigrp topology` does not?
options: Shows ALL paths including those that don't meet the feasibility condition (not just successors and FSs)
Displays EIGRP hello interval and hold timers
questions.explanation: `show ip eigrp topology` shows only routes with successors and feasible successors. `all-links` adds paths that failed the feasibility condition — useful for troubleshooting why a route is active.
hints: all-links = shows paths that failed FC; useful when route goes active

questions.question: What is the purpose of OSPF authentication and what are the supported types?
options: Prevents unauthorized OSPF routers from injecting false LSAs; supports null (type 0), simple plaintext (type 1), and MD5 (type 2)
OSPF authentication encrypts all routing data
Authentication is required for all OSPF areas except Area 0
questions.explanation: OSPF auth: Type 0=no auth, Type 1=plaintext password (visible in packets), Type 2=MD5 hash. MD5 ensures only routers with the correct key form adjacencies and accept LSAs.
hints: OSPF auth: 0=none, 1=plaintext, 2=MD5; use MD5 in production

questions.question: Which IPv6 prefix is assigned to globally routable unicast addresses by IANA for general use?
questions.explanation: 2000::/3 is the globally routable unicast range (addresses starting with 001 in the first 3 bits, i.e., 2000:: through 3FFF::). FC00::/7 = unique local, FE80::/10 = link-local, FF00::/8 = multicast.
hints: Global unicast = 2000::/3 (starts with 2 or 3); unique local = FC/FD; link-local = FE80

questions.scenario_text: Two Cisco routers (R1: G0/0 = 10.1.1.1/24, R2: G0/0 = 10.1.1.2/24) are directly connected. OSPF Area 0 is configured on both interfaces, but no neighbor relationship forms. The engineer runs 'show ip ospf neighbor' and sees an empty table.

sub_questions.question: Which combination of mismatched parameters would prevent OSPF neighbors from forming?
options: Mismatched area ID, hello/dead interval, subnet mask, or MTU (if IP MTU check is enabled)
sub_questions.explanation: OSPF neighbors will not form if: area IDs differ, hello/dead intervals differ, subnet masks differ (on broadcast/point-to-point), stub area flags differ, or MTU mismatch (if enabled). Router ID mismatches do not prevent adjacency.

sub_questions.question: A router configured with OSPF priority 0 on an interface will never become which of the following?
sub_questions.explanation: OSPF priority 0 means the router is ineligible for DR/BDR election on that segment. It can still form neighbour relationships and become DROTHER.

sub_questions.question: OSPF calculates interface cost as: reference bandwidth ÷ interface bandwidth. A lower cost path is always preferred.
sub_questions.explanation: Default reference bandwidth is 100 Mbps. A FastEthernet (100 Mbps) interface has cost 1, GigabitEthernet (1000 Mbps) would also be cost 1 unless the reference bandwidth is increased. Lower cost = more preferred path.

questions.scenario_text: A branch router (R-Branch) connects to HQ via a point-to-point WAN link (10.0.0.0/30). R-HQ has interface Gi0/0 = 10.0.0.1/30. R-Branch has Gi0/0 = 10.0.0.2/30. The branch LAN is 192.168.100.0/24. HQ needs reachability to branch hosts.

sub_questions.question: Which static route on R-HQ correctly routes traffic to the 192.168.100.0/24 branch LAN?
sub_questions.explanation: The route is: destination network 192.168.100.0 /24, next hop 10.0.0.2 (R-Branch's WAN interface). Option D uses 10.0.0.1 which is R-HQ's own interface — that loops back.

sub_questions.question: A floating static route uses a higher administrative distance than the primary dynamic routing protocol so it activates only if the dynamic route is removed from the routing table.
sub_questions.explanation: A floating static route (e.g., AD=250) loses to OSPF (AD=110) under normal conditions. If OSPF loses the route (link failure), the floating static route becomes the active route.

sub_questions.question: What is the default administrative distance of a static route on Cisco IOS?
sub_questions.explanation: Static routes have a default AD of 1. Only directly connected routes (AD=0) are more trusted. AD=255 means the route is considered unreachable/unusable.

questions.scenario_text: A large campus network uses multi-area OSPF: Area 0 (backbone), Area 1, and Area 2. An ABR connects Area 1 to Area 0. The network team wants to limit LSA flooding to reduce memory and CPU overhead.

sub_questions.question: Which OSPF LSA type describes the links within a single area and is NOT flooded beyond that area's boundaries?
sub_questions.explanation: Type 1 Router LSAs are flooded only within the originating area. ABRs do not forward Type 1 LSAs; instead they generate Type 3 Summary LSAs to describe inter-area routes.

sub_questions.question: Which area type blocks Type 5 External LSAs but still receives Type 3 Summary LSAs from the ABR?
sub_questions.explanation: A stub area blocks Type 5 External LSAs. The ABR substitutes a default route (Type 3 LSA). A Totally Stubby area blocks both Type 3 and Type 5. NSSA allows limited external routes via Type 7 LSAs.

sub_questions.question: All non-backbone OSPF areas must connect directly to Area 0 for proper inter-area routing.
sub_questions.explanation: OSPF requires all inter-area routing to pass through Area 0. Non-backbone areas that cannot physically connect to Area 0 must use a virtual link through another area that does.

questions.scenario_text: A dual-stack router has IPv6 configured on two interfaces: G0/0 = 2001:db8:1::1/64 and G0/1 = 2001:db8:2::1/64. A host on G0/0 needs to reach a host on G0/1 but pings are failing.

sub_questions.question: Which global configuration command is required to enable IPv6 packet routing between interfaces on a Cisco router?
sub_questions.explanation: 'ipv6 unicast-routing' enables the router to forward IPv6 packets between interfaces. Without it, IPv6 behaves like a host — it accepts IPv6 traffic but does not route between interfaces.

sub_questions.question: Which command displays the IPv6 routing table on a Cisco router?
sub_questions.explanation: 'show ipv6 route' displays the IPv6 routing table including connected, static, and dynamic IPv6 routes.

sub_questions.question: OSPFv3 supports routing of IPv6 prefixes and uses the same Area 0 backbone requirement and router-ID concepts as OSPFv2.
sub_questions.explanation: OSPFv3 uses the same area hierarchy, DR/BDR election, and LSA flooding as OSPFv2. The router ID is still a 32-bit value (often taken from a loopback IPv4 address or configured manually).

questions.scenario_text: Router R1 has three routes to network 172.16.0.0/16 in its routing table: one learned via OSPF (AD=110), one via EIGRP (AD=90), and one configured as a static route (AD=1, next-hop 192.168.1.1).

sub_questions.question: Which route will be installed as the active route in the routing table?
sub_questions.explanation: Administrative distance selects the best source when multiple protocols know a route. Static (AD=1) < EIGRP (AD=90) < OSPF (AD=110). Only the static route is installed.

sub_questions.question: If the static route is removed, which remaining route is preferred?
options: Neither — the route is withdrawn until reconverged
sub_questions.explanation: With the static route gone, EIGRP (AD=90) beats OSPF (AD=110). EIGRP has the lower AD so its route is installed.

sub_questions.question: Administrative distance is used to choose between routes from different routing sources; metric is used to compare routes from the same source.
sub_questions.explanation: AD is the inter-protocol tiebreaker (which source to trust). Metric is the intra-protocol tiebreaker (which path within OSPF, EIGRP, etc. is best). A router only compares metrics among routes from the same protocol.

questions.question: What is the default Administrative Distance (AD) for OSPF?
questions.explanation: OSPF has a default AD of 110. Lower AD is preferred: EIGRP internal = 90, OSPF = 110, RIP = 120.
distractor_notes: 90 is EIGRP internal AD — a common mix-up since both are interior gateway protocols.
100 is IGRP (obsolete Cisco proprietary protocol) — rarely encountered but memorised by some learners.
120 is RIP AD — less preferred than OSPF, meaning the router chooses OSPF over RIP when both exist.

questions.question: A router has routes to 192.168.1.0/24 and 192.168.1.64/26. Which route is used for a packet destined for 192.168.1.80?
options: 192.168.1.64/26 — it is the longest prefix match
Neither — the router drops the packet (ambiguous match)
Both routes equally — traffic is load-balanced
questions.explanation: Routers always use the longest prefix match. 192.168.1.80 falls in both /24 and /26, but /26 is more specific (26 bits vs 24), so it wins.
distractor_notes: Installation order has no bearing on route selection — the routing table always uses the most specific match regardless of age.
Ambiguous matches don't cause drops — the most specific route always wins. Drops only occur when no route matches at all.
Equal-cost load balancing requires identical prefixes via multiple equal-cost paths — a /24 and /26 are different networks.

questions.question: On an OSPF multi-access segment, what is the primary criterion for DR election?
options: Highest OSPF priority wins; Router ID is the tiebreaker
Lowest OSPF priority wins; Router ID is the tiebreaker
questions.explanation: OSPF DR election first compares priority (default 1, range 0–255; 0 = ineligible). Highest priority wins. If tied, highest Router ID breaks the tie.
distractor_notes: Lowest Router ID would mean the least-capable router wins — OSPF uses highest to let administrators control DR placement.
Router ID alone is only the tiebreaker — priority is checked first and overrides Router ID.
Lowest priority would prevent administrators from pinning the DR — OSPF uses highest so you can force a specific router to win.

questions.question: Which route type acts as the gateway of last resort on a Cisco router?
questions.explanation: A default route (0.0.0.0/0) matches every destination and is used when no more specific route exists. 'ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 <next-hop>' sets the gateway of last resort.
distractor_notes: A /32 host route matches only one IP — it is the most specific possible route, the opposite of a gateway of last resort.
A summary route aggregates multiple networks into one prefix — it still requires a destination match, unlike a default route.
A floating static route is a backup route with elevated AD — it can be configured as a default, but 'floating static' alone doesn't make it a default.
